### ✅ [TCD] [NVL+] SoC PCI BDF Assignment to allow THC0/THC1 Enabled Independently

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458199 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case_definition |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L2 (TCD) |

**Description:**

> Objective:
>
>
>   - To verify
>      that THC0 and THC1 are independent PCI devices that can be
>      enabled/disabled independently, and also verify both ports are assigned as
>      root functions (function 0 in BDF).
>
>   - To
>      validate and provide test coverage for NVL PCR# :  https://hsdes.intel.com/appstore/article-one/22018945839  
>
> Overview:
>
> This test aims to verify that THC0 and THC1 are
> independent PCI devices that can be enabled/disabled independently and are
> assigned as root functions (function 0 in BDF). 
>
> Ensuring this independence is crucial to avoid
> additional integration work downstream, including BIOS, driver, enabling, and
> customer-related tasks. 
>
> This test will validate the correct BDF assignment and
> the ability to enable/disable THC0 and THC1 independently without affecting
> each other.
>
>
>
> Summary:
>
>
>   - THC0 and
>      THC1 shall be independent PCI devices that can be disabled independently
>      (hidden from software).
>
>   - Update BDF
>      assignment (NVL-H and NVL-S) so that both THC0 and THC1 are root functions
>      (function 0 in BDF).
>
> Rationale:
>
>
>   - In the past
>      products (e.g. MTL/LNL/PTL), THC0 is function 0 and THC1 is function x,
>      THC0 must be enabled for THC1 function to work (per PCI
>      specification). 
>
>           This causes additional integration work downstream (BIOS, driver,
>      enabling, and customers).
>
> [Refer to TC-level for more
> details.]
>
>
>
> References:
>
> Refer to THC HAS for more information
>
>
>
> According
> to https://docs.intel.com/documents/pch_doc/NVL/PCH/HAS/Chap06_NVP-S_RegMaps/Chap06_NVP-S_RegMaps.html,
> the following table listed the PCI global device IDs and BDF for NVL-PCH-S /
> NVP-S : 
>
> For NVL-PCH-S, THC0 and THC1 are assigned on different Device numbers so they can be disabled independently:
>
>
>
>
>
> Test Flow:
>
>
>   - RVP boots to BIOS/UEFI with
>      all hardware and BIOS configuration requirements met.
>
>
>   - Ensure the THC0 and THC1 is
>      assigned to Dev 17 func0 and Dev 16 func0 respectively.
>
>   - Disable THC0 and THC1
>      independently, verify that it is not visible in OS' device manager/PCI
>      device list respectively.
>
>   - Enable THC0 and THC1
>      independently, verify that they are visible and functional in OS
>      respectively.
>
>   - Check the BDF assignment and
>      ensure again the THCs are assigned to the correct BDF details.
>
>
>
> Hardware Requirements:
>
>
>   - System with
>      NVL-PCH chipset
>
>   - BIOS/UEFI
>      that supports independent disabling of THC0 and THC1
>
>   - PCI devices
>      with THC0 and THC1
>
>
>
> Test Pass Criteria:
>
>
>   - Verify THC0
>      and THC1 ports are independent ports, one port can be enabled and
>      functional without enabling another.
>
>   - Validate the
>      BDF assignment is as expected and match the THC design architecture.
>
>
>
> Test Fail Criteria:
>
>
>   - Unable to
>      meet the passing criteria above.
>
>
>
> HW/SW/Driver/BIOS/Other Dependencies:
>
>
>   - NVL RVP
>      booted to UEFI or Ubuntu/WinOS with feature rework to enable I2C/SPI
>      interface for both THC0 and/or THC1 ports with touch devices connected.
>
>   - BIOS
>      enabling and settings for HID-SPI/I2C on THC0 and/or THC1 ports.
>
>   - PythonSV
>      (via USB2DBC or XDP) connected on host system. 
>
>
>
> Coverage:
>
>
>   - Observe the
>      BDF details are as expected and match the design architecture.
>
>   - Functional
>      coverage via gesture validation when enable the THC0/THC1 ports
>      independently.
>
>   - Validate
>      that enabled THC port should be visible and functional via pci device
>      list/device manager.
>
>
>
> DFx Requirements:
>
>
>   - Each THC
>      port shall have an independent enable control with not dependency on other
>      THC port.

---

### ✅ [TCD] [PTL+] THC IP Telemetry Data via PMC IP_TELEM_MAILBOX mechanism [PM Telemetry#6]

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458207 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case_definition |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L2 (TCD) |

**Description:**

> Objective:
>
>
>   - To validate
>      the new PMC IP Telemetry Mailbox mechanism specifically the
>      PULL_IP_TELEMETRY command triggers PMC HW/FW to collect telemetry data
>      from THC0 and THC1 IPs and place them into SSRAM.
>
>   - To
>      validate and provide test coverage for NVL PCR# 15014380322
>
> Overview:
>
> The current energy reporting counters, S0ix blocker
> counters, S0ix breaker counters, and LTR blocker counters provide PM telemetry
> data used to debug and root cause failures to enter and unexpected early wakes
> from PkgC10/S0ix. This telemetry data is used to narrow down which IP is
> responsible this unexpected PkgC10/S0ix behavior. However, the current set of
> telemetry data does not provide insight into the internal IP behavior that
> prevent PkgC10/S0ix.
>
> The PMC IP telemetry mailbox mechanism is a new
> feature for PTL-PCD-P that helps address this lack of fidelity in the PM
> telemetry data. This mailbox mechanism is required to aggregate the IP internal
> telemetry data into software discoverable memory, i.e. the shared SRAM
> where all the other PM telemetry is located.
>
>
>
> PMC IP Telemetry Mailbox
>
> The PMC
> provides a host-accessible telemetry mailbox interface, allowing the host
> software to pull IP telemetry data into shared SRAM on demand as well as
> configure IP telemetry controls.
>
> The
> telemetry mailbox consists of the following 5 host space registers and 1
> private firmware register:
>
>
>   - TELEMETRY_MB_INTERFACE (PWRMBASE BAR @ offset 0x15E8)
>
>   - TELEMETRY_MB_DATA0 (PWRMBASE BAR @ offset 0x15F0)
>
>   - TELEMETRY_MB_DATA1 (PWRMBASE BAR @ offset 0x15F4)
>
>   - TELEMETRY_MB_DATA2 (PWRMBASE BAR @ offset 0x15F8)
>
>   - TELEMETRY_MB_DATA3 (PWRMBASE BAR @ offset 0x15FC)
>
>   - FW_TELEMETRY_MB_INTERFACE (Private PMU register @ offset
>      0x15FC)
>
> The host and firmware use separate registers to manage mailbox
> operations, with host setting and firmware clearing the run/busy bit. Four
> mailbox commands support IP telemetry, following the general mailbox protocol
> described in PMC HAS
>
> Telemetry Mailbox Commands
>
>
>
>
> Command
>
>    | 
>
>
> Op-Code
>
>    | 
>
>
> Parameter 1
>
>    | 
>
>
> Parameter 2
>
>    | 
>
>
> Parameter 3
>
>    | 
>
>
> Priority
>
>    | 
>
>
>
>
>
> NOP
>
>    | 
>
>
> 0x0
>
>    | 
>
>
> N/A
>
>    | 
>
>
> N/A
>
>    | 
>
>
> N/A
>
>    | 
>
>
> L
>
>    | 
>
>
>
>
>
> PULL_IP_TELEMETRY
>
>    | 
>
>
> 0x1
>
>    | 
>
>
> IP
>   Instance
>
>    | 
>
>
> Telemetry
>   IP ID
>
>    | 
>
>
> N/A
>   (0)
>
>    | 
>
>
> L
>   (within 10 ms)
>
>    | 
>
>
> Note: If the IP telemetry data table is
> missing in DCCM, the PMC firmware returns an ILLEGAL/ERROR (0x90) code and
> clears the run_busy bit for each telemetry command, indicating the data table
> was not properly delivered during boot.
>
>
>
>
>
> PULL_IP_TELEMETRY
>
> The PULL_IP_TELEMETRY command
> triggers the PMC firmware to collect telemetry data from IPs in the PCH/SOC/PCD
> and place the data into the shared SRAM.
>
> The PULL_IP_TELEMETRY command
> operates as follows:
>
>
>   - If the IP telemetry data table is missing, the PMC
>      firmware returns an error (0x90) and clears the run_busy bit.
>
>   - If the IP
>      telemetry data table is loaded into the data memory, PMC firmware
>      determine if pulling for all IPs (IP ID = 0):
>
>
>   - If no pullable IPs are defined, it returns an error.
>
>   - For each
>       pullable IP:
>
>
>   - If the IP instance is statically disabled, 0s are
>        written to SRAM.
>
>   - If
>        enabled, telemetry data is read and written to SRAM.
>
>
>
>
>
>   - If pulling
>      for a specific IP (IP ID ≠ 0):
>
>
>   - If the IP ID or instance is invalid, or not pullable,
>       it returns an error.
>
>   - If the IP
>       instance is statically disabled, 0s are written to SRAM.
>
>   - If enabled,
>       telemetry data is read and written to SRAM at the correct offset.
>
>
>
>   - On any
>      IOSF-SB access failure, an error is returned and logged.
>
>   - On success,
>      the completion code is set to SUCCESS (0x40) and the run_busy bit is
>      cleared.
>
>
>
> PMC
> Firmware IP Telemetry Flow
>
> https://docs.intel.com/documents/pch_doc/PTL/Feature%20HAS/auto/f1.PMC_FW_IP_Telemetry_Flow.79d085.zoom.html
>
>
>
> IP Telemetry Tables
>
> The following two tables define the IP telemetry data
> that PMC firmware requires to implement the flows for the IP telemetry mailbox.
>
>
>
> The first table specifies the IP telemetry ID 1 is
> assigned to the THC IP, with 2 instances of the IP. The table also specifies
> the port IDs for each instance, and if that IP needs to have the telemetry
> registers “pulled” by the PMC firmware (some IPs push their telemetry data into
> specific locations into the shared SRAM). 
>
> Telemetry IP Information (source)
>
>
>
>
> Telemetry_IP_ID
>
>    | 
>
>
> IP Name
>
>    | 
>
>
> Instances
>
>    | 
>
>
> Pull
>
>    | 
>
>
> Port IDs
>
>    | 
>
>
> PCD
>
>    | 
>
>
>
>
>
> 1
>
>    | 
>
>
> THC
>
>    | 
>
>
> 2
>
>    | 
>
>
> True
>
>    | 
>
>
> THC0
>   (0xF26E), THC1 (0xF26F)
>
>    | 
>
>
> PTL-P,
>   PTL-A, WCL-N
>
>    | 
>
>
>
>
>
> 1
>
>    | 
>
>
> THC
>
>    | 
>
>
> 2
>
>    | 
>
>
> True
>
>    | 
>
>
> THC0
>   (0xFC6E), THC1 (0xFC6F)
>
>    | 
>
>
> NVL-H
>
>    | 
>
>
>
>
>
> 1
>
>    | 
>
>
> THC
>
>    | 
>
>
> 2
>
>    | 
>
>
> True
>
>    | 
>
>
> THC0
>   (0xA2), THC1 (0xA3)
>
>    | 
>
>
> NVP-S
>
>    | 
>
>
> Note: Telemetry IP IDs are assigned per IP and the
> assignment shall be maintained over all products. For products that do not
> include a particular IP which has an assigned IP ID, the IP telemetry table
> shall include a dummy table for that telemetry IP ID where setting it to a
> non-pullable IP with the port ID is set to 0. So, a zero port ID in the IP
> telemetry table indicates that the IP is not included in the product and all
> the IP telemetry mailbox commands that target that IP ID shall return an ILLEGAL/ERROR
> (0x90) response.
>
>
>
> The second table specifies the register list that the
> PMC firmware needs to read for the IPs where PMC needs to pull the telemetry
> data. The order of the registers in the table is the order that these registers
> shall appear in the shared SRAM, starting with all the registers for the first
> instance of the IP, followed by the next IP instance, and so on.
>

>
>
> [Refer to TC-level for more
> details.]
>
>
>
> Test Flow:
>
>
>   - RVP successfully booted to UEFI shell/Ubuntu OS with all
>      the HW/SW requirements met.
>
>   - In
>      PythonSV, enter commands to log the start of pre-trigger phase and the
>      current status of the IP telemetry mailbox for THC0/THC1 port.
>
>
>   - The
>      commands will fetch the telemetry data from THC registers and return the
>      final result for the test.
>
>   - Attempt
>      multiple touch gestures on touch panel and run the command again to verify
>      the THC counter registers values are increasing.
>
>
>
> HW Requirements:
>
>
>   - RVP
>      booted to Ubuntu/WinOS with feature rework to enable SPI/I2C interface on
>      THC0 port.   
>
>   - 3PE POR touch panel 
>      (i.e. BOM52, BOM36/37) with touch cable connected on RVP via the THC0
>      touch port/connector.
>
>   - PythonSV with USB2DBC debug
>      cable connected from SUT/RVP to host system.
>
>
>
> Test Pass Criteria:
>
>
>   - No
>      errors and Yellow-Bang observed on THC0 / THC1 port.
>
>   - The
>      touch functionalities of the Touch Panel are fully operational and
>      validated, ensuring all touch gestures/operations perform as anticipated.
>
>   - The
>      test should return "PASS" as the final result.
>
>   - The
>      THC counter registers must be increasing after every touch gestures.
>
>
>
> Test Fail Criteria:
>
>
>   - Unable to meet any of the
>      passing criteria above.
>
>
>
> HW/SW/Driver/BIOS/Other Dependencies:
>
>
>   - RVP booted
>      to UEFI or WinOS with feature rework to enable I2C/SPI interface for both
>      THC0 and/or THC1 ports with touch devices connected.
>
>   - BIOS
>      enabling and settings for HID-SPI/I2C on THC0 and/or THC1 ports.
>
>   - PythonSV
>      (via USB2DBC or XDP) connected on host system.
>
>
>
> Coverage:
>
>
>   - The test
>      covers the full execution of the PULL_IP_TELEMETRY command, including both
>      successful and error scenarios (e.g., missing telemetry data table,
>      invalid ID/instance, statically disabled IPs).
>
>   - Functional
>      coverage includes: 
>
>
>   - Pulling
>       telemetry for all IPs and for specific IP instances.
>
>   - Verifying
>       that the telemetry data is correctly written to shared SRAM for enabled
>       IPs, and 0s are written for disabled IPs.
>
>   - Confirming
>       error handling when telemetry data table is missing or when invalid
>       parameters are provided.
>
>   - Observing
>       the increment of THC counter registers in response to touch panel
>       activity, ensuring dynamic telemetry data is captured.
>
>
>
>   - Coverage
>      also includes validation of mailbox status before and after command
>      execution, and confirmation that the run_busy bit and completion codes are
>      set/cleared as expected.
>
>
>
> DFx Requirements:
>
>
>   - Register read/write access to
>      all telemetry mailbox registers (host and firmware space) is required to
>      issue commands and verify results.
>
>   - Access to shared SRAM is
>      needed to confirm telemetry data placement and correctness.
>
>   - Logging and trace
>      capabilities should be enabled to capture command execution, completion
>      codes, and error conditions.
>
>   - Test environment should
>      support PythonSV for automation and status verification.
>
>   - Ability to simulate or
>      generate touch panel activity to validate dynamic telemetry data updates.
>
>   - The platform should support
>      controlled boot and configuration to ensure the telemetry data table is
>      properly delivered and accessible.

---

### ✅ [TCD] [S0ix_LPM] THC D3 Blocker/Breaker Counter on PMC [PM Telemetry#8, 14]

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458209 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case_definition |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L2 (TCD) |

**Description:**

> Objective:
>
>
>   - Ensure the PMC increments the
>      D3 blocker counters correctly when there is a blocker on S0ix state from
>      THC-IP. 
>
>   - Verify that D3 blockers
>      counters for THC0/THC1 will stop when SUT enter sleep state with display
>      OFF.
>
>
>
> Overview:
>
> LPM blocker and
> breaker counters, along with LPM status and live registers, are essential tools
> for identifying and debugging battery drain and low-power issues in Windows
> Modern Standby and similar scenarios. Although LPM register access is
> restricted when cores are not in C0, telemetry infrastructure in Windows OS
> enables large-scale collection and analysis of power management data from field
> systems. This capability helps pinpoint S0ix block and break events, while
> Intel’s internal use of these counters enhances post-silicon debug efficiency,
> significantly reducing troubleshooting time for power-related issues.
>
>
>
> Hardware LPM Blocker and
> Breaker Counters
>
>
>   - S0ix blocker counters are implemented in hardware for
>      all programs starting with Meteor Lake SOC-M.
>
>   - S0ix breaker counters are implemented in hardware for
>      all programs starting with Panther Lake PCD-P.
>
> LPM Blocker Counters
>
> Note: This feature applies to MTL-SOC-M, MTL-IOE-M/P,
> MTL-SOC-S, MTP-S and subsequent programs.
>
> The PMC
> shall implement up to 150 counters for LPM requirements blockers, one per
> relevant LPM requirements. This is defined by the LPM dashboard
> ("BLK_CNTR" or "lpm.blkr.cntr" columns).  
>
>
>
> The counter samples the blocking condition using the 32.768 KHz
> RTC clock, so each sample is taken every 30uS. To meet the requirement of
> counting up to 90 minutes without rollover, the counter has to be 28 bits,
> bringing the maximum time to 136 minutes.
>
> bits = log2(90 minutes /
> 30uS) = log2(5400 seconds/0.0000305
> seconds) = log2(176,947,200)
>
> log10(176,947,200) =
> 8.247844
>
> log10(2) = 0.30103
>
> bits = log10(176,947,200)/log10(2) = 8.247844/0.30103 = 27.40 bits =>
> 28 bits
>
> 228 = 268,435,456
>
> 228 * 30uS = 8192
> seconds = 136.5 minutes
>
>
>
> LPM Blocker Counters Control
> Register Fields
>
>
>   - Policy Bit:
>
> Controls when encounter increment:
>
>
>   - Only when enabled, or
>
>   - When enabled and CPU is in a
>      deep package C state
>
>
>   - Meteor Lake: PKGC10
>
>   - Lunar Lake and subsequent
>      programs: PKGC6.2, 10.2, 10.3
>
>   - Counters do not increase in
>      PKGC6.1 OR.10.1
>
>
>   - Clear Bit:
>
> Resets all counters when toggled from 0 -> 1
>
>
>   - Enable Bit:
>
> General enable/disable for counters
>
>
>
> LPM Breaker Counters
>
> Note: This feature applies to PTL-PCD-P, WCL, and
> subsequent products.
>
> The PMC shall implement up to 150 counters for LPM requirements
> breakers, one per relevant LPM
> requirements. This is defined by the LPM dashboard ("BLK_CNTR" or
> "lpm.blkr.cntr" columns).
>
> The breaker counters are incremented when there is a
> transition from deeper S0ix -> a shallower S0ix state / from an S0ix state
> -> S0 if the LPM requirement for that counter is de-asserted (i.e. the LPM
> requirement was not met and is either a primary or contributing factor to the
> reason there was a transition from a deeper S0ix state to a shallower S0ix
> state or S0).
>
>
>
> The breaker counters increment based on the S0ix state
> transitions whereas the blocker counters increment based on the RTC clock tick.
> The number of bits for a breaker counters need to span at least 90 minutes of
> wall clock time. Hence, the maximum frequency that the breaker counters could
> be incremented is driven by the smallest interval these S0ix state transitions
> can occur. Letting this period be an average of 500us, results in a minimum
> number of bits required for the breaker counters to be 24 bits:
>
> The counter needs to be sized large enough to
> capture an event every 500us over at least 90 minutes
>
> bits = log2(90
> minutes / 500uS) = log2(5400
> seconds/0.0005 seconds) = log2(10,800,000)
>
> log10(10,800,000)
> = 7.033424
>
> log10(2)
> = 0.30103
>
> So, the number of bits needs to be at least:
>
> bits = log10(10,800,000)/log10(2) = 7.033424/0.30103 = 23.365 bits =>
> 24 bits
>
> Using 24 bits the counter will span 139.8
> minutes of wall clock time using an event frequency of once per 500 uS
>
> 224 *
> 500uS = 8388 seconds = 139.8 minutes
>
> Note that this is a minimum time
> based on the worst case event average frequency of once per 500us, the S0ix
> exit interrupt/Vnn restore interrupt events should occur at a lower frequency
> therefore the counters should in practice cover much longer wall clock times.
>
>
>
> LPM
> Breaker Counter Control Register Fields
>
>
>   - Increment Bit:
>
> Allows FW increment all breaker counters for
> unmet LPM requirements, regardless of enable setting.
>
>
>   - Clear Bit:
>
> Resets all counters on a 0 -> 1 transition.
>
>
>   - Enable Bit:
>
>
>   - When enabled: counters increment on S0ix state exit for
>       unmet requirements and when firmware uses the increment bit.
>
>   - When disabled: counters increment only when firmware
>       uses the increment bit (not on S0ix state changes).
>
>
>
>
> References:
>
> Please
> refer to the THC HAS for more information.
>
> https://docs.intel.com/documents/pch_doc/PTL/HAS/Power/S0ix/S0ix.html#thc-was-tsi 
>
> https://docs.intel.com/documents/pch_doc/MTP/Feature%20HAS/THC_Workaround_for_Chassis22.html 
>
>
>
> Test Flow:
>
>
>   - Boot RVP to
>      Ubuntu OS with all HW/SW and BIOS configuration completed.
>
>   - EnsureS0ix + PKGC-10 power
>      management is enabled and stable; PMC telemetry feature is active.
>
>   - Record initial values of
>      THC0/THC1 D3 blocker counters and power state registers. 
>
>   - Leave the system idle with
>      display ON to triggerS0ix blocking; verify blocker counters are
>      increasing.
>
>   - Put system into Modern
>      Standby/Sleep (display OFF) to allow S0ix entry; verify blocker counters
>      stop incrementing.
>
>   - Confirm THC0/THC1 are in D3
>      state during deep sleep; record register values.
>
>   - Wake system from sleep,
>      verify THC0/THC1 exit D3 state; record register values. 
>
>   - Repeat steps #4 to #7
>      multiple times to ensure consistent counter and register behaviour.
>
>
>
> HW Requirements:
>
>
>   - RVP
>      booted to Ubuntu OS with feature rework to enable SPI/I2C interface on
>      THC0/THC1 port.   
>
>   - 3PE POR touch panel 
>      (i.e. BOM52, BOM36/37) with touch cable connected on RVP via the THC0/THC1
>      touch port/connector.
>
>   - PythonSV with USB2DBC debug
>      cable connected from SUT/RVP to host system.
>
>
>
> Test Pass Criteria:
>
>
>   - THC0 and
>      THC1 blocker counters increment only when THC0/THC1 is not in D3
>      state (SUT in S0ix with display-ON).
>
>   - Blocker
>      Counters stop incrementing when THC0/THC1 entered D3 state (SUT in
>      S0ix with display-OFF).
>
>
>
> Test Fail Criteria:
>
>
>   - Unable to meet any of the
>      passing criteria above.
>
>
>
> HW/SW/Driver/BIOS/Other Dependencies:
>
>
>   - RVP/SUT
>      booted to Ubuntu OS.
>
>   - Ubuntu OS is with built-in
>      HID-SPI/I2C kernel driver.
>
>   - BIOS
>      enabling and settings for HID-SPI/I2C on THC0 and/or THC1 ports.
>
>   - SUT able to
>      support PKGC-10 and S0ix features.
>
>
>
> Coverage:
>
>
>   - Test covers THC0/THC1
>      transitions between D0i2, D3 and S0ix power states, validating both
>      blocking and non-blocking conditions for S0ix entry.
>
>   - Functional coverage includes
>      verifying that PMC D3 blocker counters increment during S0ix blocking
>      (display ON, THC not in D3), and remain static during S0ix non-blocking
>      (display OFF, THC in D3).
>
>   - All relevant PMC and THC
>      registers are read and recorded before, during and after power state
>      transitions to confirm correct system and device behaviour.
>
>   - Negative coverage includes
>      scenarios where S0ix entry is blocked, ensuring counters increment and
>      where S0ix entry is not blocked, ensuring the counters stop.
>
>   - The test is repeated multiple
>      times to ensure consistent counter and register behaviour across cycles.
>
>
>
> DFx Requirements:
>
>
>   - Use of PythonSV for
>      automation and register dumps.
>
>   - Ability to read and write PMC
>      and THC registers (including D3 blocker counters, power state indicators,
>      and breaker counter controls).
>
>   - System must be configured
>      with a validated power management recipe (BKC) and PMC telemetry feature
>      enabled.
>
>   - Platform must support
>      monitoring and controlling S0ix, S0i2.x, and D3 power states, including
>      Modern Standby/Sleep transitions.
>
>   - Hardware support for reliably
>      entering and exiting sleep states (e.g., via Power button).

---

### ✅ [TCD] [S0ix_LPM] THC LTR Blocker/Breaker Counter on PMC  [PM Telemetry#10]

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458210 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case_definition |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L2 (TCD) |

**Description:**

> Objective:
>
>
>
>
>   - To verify that the PMC
>       correctly increments the LTR blocker counters for THC0/THC1, when LTR
>       values configured on THC0/THC1 are below the programmable PMC threshold
>       limit.
>   - Confirm that the LTR blocker
>       counters for THC0/THC1 will stop incrementing, when LTR values configured
>       on THC0/THC1 are above the programmable PMC threshold limit.
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> Overview:
>
>
> The current system can’t precisely identify which IP
>  is blocking S0ix low power mode with the shortest LTR signal, and the hardware
>  logic for tracking this is limited. To address this, the plan is to replace
>  the existing logic with individual 28-bit LTR counters for each IP. These
>  counters will increment when an IP sends an LTR below a set threshold
>  (initially 500μs, adjustable as needed). 
>
>
>
>
>
> Description
>  of Change
>
>
> PCH Telemetry HAS: The SoC/PCH will monitor each IP’s LTR and only
>  increment the IP’s counter if the LTR is below the threshold. 
>
>
> Counter Specifications: Counters meet Microsoft's 90-minute rollover
>  requirement and are populated by chassis runtime dashboards or IP XML files,
>  depending on the platform version. 
>
>
> Data Source: Each memory resource LTR source will have a
>  dedicated counter, and counters will be populated using runtime dashboards for
>  Chassis 2.0 IPs and IP XML files for Chassis 2.2 IPs (MTL onwards, this
>  includes ACE, CNVI, THC0, and THC1.)
>
>
>
>
>
> References:
>
>
> Refer to THC HAS for more information.
>
>
>
>
>
> Test Flow:
>
>
>
>
>   - Boot RVP to Ubuntu OS, with all HW/SW and BIOS
>       configured.
>   - Verify the
>       SUT support S0ix + PKGC-10 and enable PMC Telemetry.
>   - Read and
>       confirm the LTR threshold on the PMC (~250μs by default).
>   - Configure
>       the threshold-based incrementing method to 1, and enable counter with EN
>       bit. 
>   - Verify
>       system can achieve S0ix residency; record initial residency values.
>   - Read and
>       record the initial LTR_THC0_BLC_CNTR & LTR_C2P2_IP_THC0 register
>       values.
>   - Disable all
>       LTR control bits (LP_LTR_EN, LP_LTR_REQ, ACTIVE_LTR_EN, ACTIVE_LTR_REQ).
>   - Configure
>       THC0/THC1 to send a below-threshold LTR (< LTR_COUNTER_THRESHOLD) to
>       the PMC, or set the ACT_LTR_VAL to '1' and ACT_LTR_SCALE to '0'.
>   - Enable
>       ACTIVE_LTR_EN and ACTIVE_LTR_REQ, check register updates; verify LTR
>       block counter increments and LTR value change.
>   - Confirm
>       that as the block counter increases, S0ix residency does not increase
>       (S0ix is blocked).
>   - Configure
>       THC0/THC1 to send an above-threshold LTR (> LTR_COUNTER_THRESHOLD), or
>       enable LP_LTR_EN and LP_LTR_REQ.
>   - Check
>       registers again; block counter should stop incrementing and the LTR value
>       should return to 0x8BFF.
>   - Confirm
>       S0ix residency resumes and increases (no block).
>   - Repeat
>       steps #6–#13 with various LTR values and thresholds to validate counter
>       behaviour and consistency.
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> HW Requirements:
>
>
>
>
>   - RVP
>       booted to Ubuntu OS with feature rework to enable SPI/I2C interface on
>       THC0/THC1 port.   
>   - 3PE POR touch panel (i.e.
>       BOM52, BOM36/37) with touch cable connected on RVP via the THC0/THC1
>       touch port/connector.
>   - PythonSV with USB2DBC debug
>       cable connected from SUT/RVP to host system.
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> Test Pass Criteria:
>
>
>
>
>   - THC0 and THC1 blocker
>       counters increment only when their LTR is below the threshold.
>   - Blocker counters stop
>       incrementing when LTR is above the threshold.
>   - Blocker counters respond to
>       threshold changes.
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> Test Fail Criteria:
>
>
>
>
>   - Unable to meet any of the
>       passing criteria above.
>
>
>
>
>
>
> HW/SW/Driver/BIOS/Other Dependencies:
>
>
>
>
>   - RVP/SUT
>       booted to Ubuntu OS.
>   - Ubuntu OS is with built-in
>       HID-SPI/I2C kernel driver.
>   - BIOS
>       enabling and settings for HID-SPI/I2C on THC0 and/or THC1 ports.
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> Coverage:
>
>
>
>
>   - Vary the
>       LTP_COUNTER_THRESHOLD and a range of LTR values are tested to verify the
>       counter behaviour, threshold sensitivity, and confirm correct HW/SW
>       response.
>   - Cover transitions into and
>       out of S0ix, ensuring residency and counter increment/decrement aligns
>       with expected S0ix blocking and non-blocking events.
>   - Repeat the block/non-block
>       cycles multiple times to confirm behaviour is robust against repeated
>       transitions and does not degrade. 
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> DFx Requirements:
>
>
>
>
>   - Ensure THC block has a
>       dedicated LTR blocker counter, and all are accessible via platform
>       debug/telemetry infrastructure.
>   - LTR thresholds must be
>       programmable during test/runtime to enable flexibility for different
>       platforms or customer/OEM requirements.

---

### ✅ [TCD] [S0ix_LPM] THC PG Blocker/Breaker Counter on PMC [PM Telemetry#8, 14]

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458211 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case_definition |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L2 (TCD) |

**Description:**

> Objective:
>
>
>   - To verify that the PMC
>      correctly increments the PG blocker/breaker counters for THC0 and/or THC1
>      as expected, when THC0/THC1 is active state (not in idle/D0i2) due to
>      ongoing touch input interrupt/frames to THC controller.
>
>   - Confirm that the PG
>      blocker/breaker counters for THC0 and/or THC1 will stop incrementing once
>      THC0/THC1 entered idle state/D0i2 with NO ongoing touch input
>      interrupt/frames to THC controller.
>
>
>
> Overview:
>
>
>
> References:
>
> Refer to
> THC HAS for more information.
>
> https://docs.intel.com/documents/pch_doc/PTL/HAS/Power/S0ix/S0ix.html#thc-was-tsi 
>
> https://docs.intel.com/documents/pch_doc/MTP/Feature%20HAS/THC_Workaround_for_Chassis22.html 
>
>
>
> Test Flow:
>
>
>   - Boot SUT to
>      Ubuntu OS, ensure HW/SW and BIOS are correctly configured.
>
>   - Verify S0ix + PKGC-10 support
>      with PMC Telemetry enabled and verified.
>
>   - Read and record initial
>      THC0/THC1 PG blocker/breaker counters and power state registers.
>
>   - Trigger touch activity to put
>      THC0/THC1 into D0 (active state); verify the blocker counters increment
>      and PWRST reflects active state.
>
>   - Stop touch activity to allow
>      THC0/THC1 to enter D0i2 (idle state); verify the blocker counter hold (no
>      increment) and PWRST reflects idle state.
>
>   - Repeat steps #3 to #5
>      multiple times to ensure consistent counter and state behaviour.
>
>
>
> HW Requirements:
>
>
>   - RVP
>      booted to Ubuntu OS with feature rework to enable SPI/I2C interface on
>      THC0/THC1 port.   
>
>   - 3PE POR touch panel (i.e.
>      BOM52, BOM36/37) with touch cable connected on RVP via the THC0/THC1 touch
>      port/connector.
>
>   - PythonSV with USB2DBC debug
>      cable connected from SUT/RVP to host system.
>
>
>
> Test Pass Criteria:
>
>
>   - Verify that the PMC
>      increments the PG blocker/breaker counters for THC0/THC1 when THC0/THC1 is
>      in an active state (not idle or D0i2) as a result of ongoing touch input
>      interrupt/frames to the THC controller.
>
>   - Confirm that the PG
>      blocker/breaker counters for THC0/THC1 stop incrementing once THC0/THC1
>      enters the idle state (or D0i2) with no ongoing touch input
>      interrupt/frames to the THC controller.
>
>
>
> Test Fail Criteria:
>
>
>   - Unable to meet any of the
>      passing criteria above.
>
>
>
> HW/SW/Driver/BIOS/Other Dependencies:
>
>
>   - RVP/SUT
>      booted to Ubuntu OS.
>
>   - Ubuntu OS is with built-in
>      HID-SPI/I2C kernel driver.
>
>   - BIOS
>      enabling and settings for HID-SPI/I2C on THC0 and/or THC1 ports.
>
>
>
> Coverage:
>
>
>   - Validate blocker/breaker
>      counter operation during repeated transitions between D0 (active/touch
>      traffic present) and D0i2 (idle/no touch), ensuring counters increment
>      when expected and freeze otherwise.
>
>   - Perform tests for both THC0
>      and THC1 to confirm counters and power state transitions are independently
>      accurate for each controller.
>
>   - Record initial/pre/post-test
>      values for all relevant PMC registers (blocker/breaker counters, PWRST)
>      over multiple cycles to verify behavioural consistency.
>
>   - Test toggling between touch
>      and idle conditions to check counter and state robustness, also attempt
>      outlier states (e.g., holding THC in non-idle for extended periods).
>
>
>
> DFx Requirements:
>
>
>   - Ensure counters and power
>      state registers are readable during all system states, including S0ix, D0,
>      and D0i2.
>
>   - Confirm blocker/breaker
>      counter values are visible through PythonSV.
>
>   - THC0/THC1 counters and
>      registers should be separately controllable for accurate debug and
>      analysis.
>
>   - Align BIOS/PM configuration
>      with stable BKC and PM recipe for repeatable results, minimizing
>      external/new ingredient impact.

## L3 — TC (126 records)

### ✅ [TC] Cold Reset Cycles with THC0 (HID-I2C) Enabled only

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458303 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test
> Description:
>
>
>   - The objective/intent of this
>      test is to conduct a thorough system-level regression that includes
>      'N' iterations of PM Cycling, specifically Cold Resets (CR), while running
>      test traffic through a single port THC0 that configured as HID-I2C
>      interface.
>
>
>  (Note: 'N' iterations of CR Cycling will be specified/defined at TR
> level, the number will vary across different project milestones.)
>
>
>   - This test is designed to
>      implicitly encompass the verification of Fuses/Soft-straps loading, BIOS,
>      SW Driver and THC Controller + TIC device initialization/enumeration
>      sequences.
>
>   - It will also assess and
>      exercise the HID-I2C PIO and DMA traffic cycling on the THC0 port
>      to ensure robust touch operation between the host controller and TIC
>      device.
>
>   - Solar SW tool will be
>      utilized to initiate the PM regression/cycling test in Ubuntu OS
>      environment.
>
>   - Additionally, the THC
>      controller will undergo a reset during each Cold-Reset cycle. This CR
>      sequence will implicitly encompass the various elements outlined in the
>      IP-HAS. (Note: Refer to TCD --> 'Overview' Section for more
>      information.)
>
>
>
> Pre-Test Configuration:
>
>
>   - THC0 port will required to be enabled in BIOS settings.
>
>   - RVP booted to BIOS Menu, go to Intel Advance Menu ->
>      PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration ->   <Refer to
>      the BKM settings below to enable THC0 port.>
>
>   - Note: The BKM below are captured from NVL RVP
>
>
>
> Test
> Steps / Procedure: 
>
>
>   - RVP booted to Ubuntu OS
>      with all the HW/SW requirements met, follow
>      and configure all the BIOS settings accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test
>      Configuration' section above; Ensure latest Solar tool is installed.
>
>   - Check if there is yellow bang on THC touch
>      panel with the following commands
>
> sudo lspci                     #list pci devices
>
> lspci -kvv -s 00:10.0   #list pci device with very detail kernel
> info and which bus device function
>
> The expected results is where "Kernel driver in use: intel_quicki2c" & "Kernel modules: intel_quicki2c" can be observed, no yellow bang occurs
>
>
>   - In Ubuntu OS, go
>      into the Solar folder, right click to "open in terminal".
>
>   - Enter the following command to open the
>      Solar GUI
>
> sudo ./SolarPanel.sh
>
>
>   - In Solar tool, validator/user may use the
>      following settings below as example, which will enable 100 cycles of
>      Cold-Reset with Yellow-Bank Check, Exit On Failure and Bypass Windows
>      Login enabled.
>
>
>   - Tick to enable Yellow Bang Check
>       in Advanced Verification PRE/POST Check tab.
>
>   - Tick to enable Exit On Failure in Knobs
>       tab.
>
>   - Tick to enable Bypass Windows Login in
>       Knobs tab.
>
>   - Specify 'N' total number of cycles per
>       loop/test in Test Time tab. (e.g. 'N' = 100)
>
>   - Specify Start Delay Time (Optional)
>
>   - Specify total sleep time allowed, i.e. total allowed time
>       to reboot/reset/wake the system back to Ubuntu OS state (e.g. 180
>       seconds)
>
>   - Specify total Active Time in Ubuntu OS before
>       starting next PM cycle (e.g. 15 seconds) 
>
>   - Select the "Cold-Reset" button.
>       It will be shown as green box once clicked, and the
>       "Cold-Reset|sec:180|sec:15|Timer" will be shown in the big blue
>       box in the right, which implied it is confirmed being set.
>
>   - Finally, click on the green play button
>       to start the PM Regression/Cycling test. 
>
>
> (Note:
> Alternatively, instead of setup a new settings every time for each PM
> regression test, validator/user may load the existing config solar .xml file
> with pre-existing settings saved previously .)
>
>
>   - Wait for the CR cycling test to complete.
>      Solar tool will return a Pass/Fail result in the "Test Status"
>      section, the result will be shown in the log file as well.
>
> [Alternate
> Method: Automated Touch traffic using NGA Framework + IVT Released
> NGA supported WIM image  -- (More info TBD) ]
>
>
>   - NGA Testline for
>      CR Regression existing example used in MTL-S/MTP-S project :  https://nga.laas.intel.com/#/mtl_validation/planning/testlines/13898a8c-5b6d-4a13-873e-9eba1ef39b17 
>
>   - If using the existing
>      testline above, validator/user will need to port it over to PTL/NVL
>      compliant framework/environment,  using IVT released WIM image for
>      PTL/NVL with NGA support and all its' dependencies included.
>
> SW
> Requirements:
>
>
>   - RVP/SUT
>      booted to Ubuntu OS.
>
>   - Ubuntu OS is with built-in
>      HID-SPI/I2C kernel driver.
>
> HW
> Requirements:
>
>
>   - RVP booted to Ubuntu OS with
>      feature rework to enable I2C interface on THC0 port.
>
>   - 3PE POR touch panel 
>      (i.e. BOM52, BOM36A/37A) with touch cable connected on RVP via the THC0
>      touch port/connector.
>
>   - (Optional): Acute logic
>      analyzer to monitor the HID-I2C traffic for debug/analysis purposes.
>
> (Note: Please check RVP schematic/layout to confirm
> the THC0 port naming label and location) 
>
> Dependencies:
>
>
>   - BIOS implementation according
>      to THC BWG requirements / config.
>
> Test
> Pass Criteria:
>
>
>   - Using Solar
>      tool, verify and ensure targeted 'N' number of Cold-Reset (CR) PM cycling
>      count can be achieved successfully on RVP with NO error(s)
>      returned/encountered. 
>
>   - Make sure NO yellow-bang
>      occurred on THC0 controller/Device Driver (i.e. QuickI2C).
>
>   - Extra: Ensure
>      that the touch functionality of the HID-I2C touch device connected on THC0
>      still working normally after each CR cycled. Alternatively,
>      this functionality can be verified by conducting both PIO and DMA
>      operations/traffic tests.
>
> (Note: If NGA/Automation is not enabled
> especially during the early PO phase, manual touch gesture method by human input can be used to verify if
> touch panel still functional.)
>
> Test
> Fail Criteria:
>
>
>   - Unable to
>      meet the passing criteria above.
>
>
>   - Solar tool unable to
>      pass/complete the targeted CR cycling count with error code
>      returned, 
>
>
>   - Yellow-bang
>      occurred on THC - QuickI2C device and/or caused by any other
>      drivers/devices, 
>
>   - Touch
>      functionality of the touch device broken and not working after CR. (i.e.
>      PIO and DMA operations/traffic tests failed.)
>
>   - Other
>      IP/domains, WinOS, IFWI, SW driver or any other system/platform-level
>      components utilized in this test that may cause the CR cycle failed to
>      complete the targeted "N" Iterations of CR cycle.
>
> Intent
> Verify:
>
>
>   - Verify no
>      yellow-bang or driver errors occur on THC controller/driver after any
>      cold-reset cycle.
>
>   - Ensure the
>      touch device maintains functionality post-reset (can operate via
>      HID-SPI/I2C interface, PIO/DMA)
>
> Ecosystem SW Requirements:
>  Below is the example from NVL-Hx, please refer to Wiki for different project: THC Device Enablement on NVL-HX RVP1/RVP4 - Validation and Debug Encyclopedia - Intel Enterprise Wiki
>
>   - BIOS version:
>   - ifwi_nvl_hx_a1a0_pp_release_2025ww50.1.01/ or later version
>
>   - OS kernel:
>   - Window OS
>
> NVL-HX-CONS-25.48.2.287 or later version (NOTE: NON-POR, does not supported by driver team, intended solely for debugging/validation purposes)
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> \\amr.corp.intel.com\ec\proj\sv\Debug\PowerOn\NVL_Hx\NVL_Hx_A1\OS_Image\Ubuntu\ubuntu-24.04.3-desktop-amd64.iso
>
>   - Driver versions:
>   - Windows OS
>
>  https://ubit-artifactory-or.intel.com/artifactory/one-windows-local/Ingredients/hidi2c_touch/ OR https://ubit-artifactory-ba.intel.com/artifactory/owr-repos/Submissions/quickspi/
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
>                              Built in QuickSPI/QuickI2C kernel driver

---

### ✅ [TC] Cold Reset Cycles with THC0 (HID-I2C) and THC1 (HID-I2C) Enabled

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458304 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test Description:
>
>   - The objective/intent of this test is to conduct a thorough system-level regression that includes 'N' iterations of PM Cycling, specifically Cold Resets (CR), while running test traffic through both ports THC0 and THC1 that configured as HID-I2C interface.
>                (Note: 'N' iterations of CR Cycling will be specified/defined at TR level, the number will vary across different project milestones.)
>
>   - This test is designed to implicitly encompass the verification of Fuses/Soft-straps loading, BIOS, SW Driver and THC Controller + TIC device initialization/enumeration sequences.
>   - It will also assess and exercise the HID-I2C PIO and DMA traffic cycling on the THC0 port to ensure robust touch operation between the host controller and TIC device.
>   - Solar SW tool will be utilized to initiate the PM regression/cycling test in Ubuntu OS environment.
>   - Additionally, the THC controller will undergo a reset during each Cold-Reset cycle. This CR sequence will implicitly encompass the various elements outlined in the IP-HAS. (Note: Refer to TCD --> 'Overview' Section for more information.)
>
> Pre-Test Configuration:
>
>   - THC0 and THC1 ports will required to be enabled in BIOS settings.
>   - RVP booted to BIOS Menu, go to Intel Advance Menu -> PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration ->   <Refer to the BKM settings below to enable THC0 and THC1 ports.>
>   - Note: The BKM below are captured from NVL RVP
>
>
> Test Steps / Procedure: 
>
>   - RVP booted to Ubuntu OS with all the HW/SW requirements met, follow and configure all the BIOS settings accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test Configuration' section above; Ensure latest Solar tool is installed.
>   - Check if there is yellow bang on THC touch panel with the following commands
> sudo lspci                     #list pci devices
> lspci -kvv -s 00:10.0   #list pci device with very detail kernel info and which bus device function
> The expected results is where "Kernel driver in use: intel_quicki2c" & "Kernel modules: intel_quicki2c" can be observed, no yellow bang occurs
>
>   - In Ubuntu OS, go into the Solar folder, right click to "open in terminal".
>   - Enter the following command to open the Solar GUI
> sudo ./SolarPanel.sh
>
>   - In Solar tool, validator/user may use the following settings below as example, which will enable 100 cycles of Cold-Reset with Yellow-Bank Check, Exit On Failure and Bypass Windows Login enabled.
>   - Tick to enable Yellow Bang Check in Advanced Verification PRE/POST Check tab.
>   - Tick to enable Exit On Failure in Knobs tab.
>   - Tick to enable Bypass Windows Login in Knobs tab.
>   - Specify 'N' total number of cycles per loop/test in Test Time tab. (e.g. 'N' = 100)
>   - Specify Start Delay Time (Optional)
>   - Specify total sleep time allowed, i.e. total allowed time to reboot/reset/wake the system back to Ubuntu OS state (e.g. 180 seconds)
>   - Specify total Active Time in Ubuntu OS before starting next PM cycle (e.g. 15 seconds) 
>   - Select the "Cold-Reset" button. It will be shown as green box once clicked, and the "Cold-Reset|sec:180|sec:15|Timer" will be shown in the big blue box in the right, which implied it is confirmed being set.
>   - Finally, click on the green play button to start the PM Regression/Cycling test. 
>
> (Note: Alternatively, instead of setup a new settings every time for each PM regression test, validator/user may load the existing config solar .xml file with pre-existing settings saved previously .)
>
>   - Wait for the CR cycling test to complete. Solar tool will return a Pass/Fail result in the "Test Status" section, the result will be shown in the log file as well.
> [Alternate Method: Automated Touch traffic using NGA Framework + IVT Released NGA supported WIM image  -- (More info TBD) ]
>
>   - NGA Testline for CR Regression existing example used in MTL-S/MTP-S project :  https://nga.laas.intel.com/#/mtl_validation/planning/testlines/13898a8c-5b6d-4a13-873e-9eba1ef39b17 
>   - If using the existing testline above, validator/user will need to port it over to PTL/NVL compliant framework/environment,  using IVT released WIM image for PTL/NVL with NGA support and all its' dependencies included.
> SW Requirements:
>
>   - RVP/SUT booted to Ubuntu OS.
>   - Ubuntu OS is with built-in HID-SPI/I2C kernel driver.
> HW Requirements:
>
>   - RVP booted to Ubuntu OS with feature rework to enable I2C interface on THC0/THC1 port.
>   - 3PE POR touch panel (i.e. BOM52, BOM36A/37A) with touch cable connected on RVP via the THC0/THC1 touch port/connector.
>   - (Optional): Acute logic analyzer to monitor the HID-I2C traffic for debug/analysis purposes.
> (Note: Please check RVP schematic/layout to confirm the THC0/THC1 port naming label and location) 
> Dependencies:
>
>   - BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements / config.
> Test Pass Criteria:
>
>   - Using Solar tool, verify and ensure targeted 'N' number of Cold-Reset (CR) PM cycling count can be achieved successfully on RVP with NO error(s) returned/encountered. 
>   - Make sure NO yellow-bang occurred on THC0 and THC1 controller/Device Driver (i.e. QuickI2C).
>   - Extra: Ensure that the touch functionality of the HID-I2C touch device connected on THC0 still working normally after each CR cycled. Alternatively, this functionality can be verified by conducting both PIO and DMA operations/traffic tests.
> (Note: If NGA/Automation is not enabled especially during the early PO phase, manual touch gesture method by human input can be used to verify if touch panel still functional.)
> Test Fail Criteria:
>
>   - Unable to meet the passing criteria above.
>
>   - Solar tool unable to pass/complete the targeted CR cycling count with error code returned, 
>
>   - Yellow-bang occurred on THC - QuickI2C device and/or caused by any other drivers/devices, 
>   - Touch functionality of the touch device broken and not working after CR. (i.e. PIO and DMA operations/traffic tests failed.)
>   - Other IP/domains, WinOS, IFWI, SW driver or any other system/platform-level components utilized in this test that may cause the CR cycle failed to complete the targeted "N" Iterations of CR cycle.
> Intent Verify:
>
>   - Verify no yellow-bang or driver errors occur on THC controller/driver after any cold-reset cycle.
>   - Ensure the touch device maintains functionality post-reset (can operate via HID-SPI/I2C interface, PIO/DMA)
>
> Ecosystem SW Requirements:
>  Below is the example from NVL-Hx, please refer to Wiki for different project: THC Device Enablement on NVL-HX RVP1/RVP4 - Validation and Debug Encyclopedia - Intel Enterprise Wiki
>
>   - BIOS version:
>   - ifwi_nvl_hx_a1a0_pp_release_2025ww50.1.01/ or later version
>
>   - OS kernel:
>   - Window OS
>
> NVL-HX-CONS-25.48.2.287 or later version (NOTE: NON-POR, does not supported by driver team, intended solely for debugging/validation purposes)
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> \\amr.corp.intel.com\ec\proj\sv\Debug\PowerOn\NVL_Hx\NVL_Hx_A1\OS_Image\Ubuntu\ubuntu-24.04.3-desktop-amd64.iso
>
>   - Driver versions:
>   - Windows OS
>
>  https://ubit-artifactory-or.intel.com/artifactory/one-windows-local/Ingredients/hidi2c_touch/ OR https://ubit-artifactory-ba.intel.com/artifactory/owr-repos/Submissions/quickspi/
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
>                              Built in QuickSPI/QuickI2C kernel driver

---

### ✅ [TC] Cold Reset Cycles with THC0 (HID-I2C) and THC1 (HID-SPI) Enabled

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458305 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test Description:
>
>   - The objective/intent of this test is to conduct a thorough system-level regression that includes 'N' iterations of PM Cycling, specifically Cold Resets (CR), while running test traffic through both ports THC0, configured as HID-I2C interface and THC1 that configured as HID-SPI interface.
>                (Note: 'N' iterations of CR Cycling will be specified/defined at TR level, the number will vary across different project milestones.)
>
>   - This test is designed to implicitly encompass the verification of Fuses/Soft-straps loading, BIOS, SW Driver and THC Controller + TIC device initialization/enumeration sequences.
>   - It will also assess and exercise the HID-I2C/SPI PIO and DMA traffic cycling on the THC0/THC1 port to ensure robust touch operation between the host controller and TIC device.
>   - Solar SW tool will be utilized to initiate the PM regression/cycling test in Ubuntu OS environment.
>   - Additionally, the THC controller will undergo a reset during each Cold-Reset cycle. This CR sequence will implicitly encompass the various elements outlined in the IP-HAS. (Note: Refer to TCD --> 'Overview' Section for more information.)
>
> Pre-Test Configuration:
>
>   - THC0 and THC1 ports will required to be enabled in BIOS settings.
>   - RVP booted to BIOS Menu, go to Intel Advance Menu -> PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration ->   <Refer to the BKM settings below to enable THC0 and THC1 ports.>
>   - Note: The BKM below are captured from NVL RVP
>
> Test Steps / Procedure: 
>
>   - RVP booted to Ubuntu OS with all the HW/SW requirements met, follow and configure all the BIOS settings accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test Configuration' section above; Ensure latest Solar tool is installed.
>   - Check if there is yellow bang on THC touch panel with the following commands
> sudo lspci                     #list pci devices
> lspci -kvv -s 00:10.0   #list pci device with very detail kernel info and which bus device function
> The expected results is where "Kernel driver in use: intel_quicki2c" & "Kernel modules: intel_quicki2c" can be observed, no yellow bang occurs
>
>   - In Ubuntu OS, go into the Solar folder, right click to "open in terminal".
>   - Enter the following command to open the Solar GUI
> sudo ./SolarPanel.sh
>
>   - In Solar tool, validator/user may use the following settings below as example, which will enable 100 cycles of Cold-Reset with Yellow-Bank Check, Exit On Failure and Bypass Windows Login enabled.
>   - Tick to enable Yellow Bang Check in Advanced Verification PRE/POST Check tab.
>   - Tick to enable Exit On Failure in Knobs tab.
>   - Tick to enable Bypass Windows Login in Knobs tab.
>   - Specify 'N' total number of cycles per loop/test in Test Time tab. (e.g. 'N' = 100)
>   - Specify Start Delay Time (Optional)
>   - Specify total sleep time allowed, i.e. total allowed time to reboot/reset/wake the system back to Ubuntu OS state  (e.g. 180 seconds)
>   - Specify total Active Time in Ubuntu OS before starting next PM cycle (e.g. 15 seconds) 
>   - Select the "Cold-Reset" button. It will be shown as green box once clicked, and the "Cold-Reset|sec:180|sec:15|Timer" will be shown in the big blue box in the right, which implied it is confirmed being set.
>   - Finally, click on the green play button to start the PM Regression/Cycling test. 
>
> (Note: Alternatively, instead of setup a new settings every time for each PM regression test, validator/user may load the existing config solar .xml file with pre-existing settings saved previously .)
>
>   - Wait for the CR cycling test to complete. Solar tool will return a Pass/Fail result in the "Test Status" section, the result will be shown in the log file as well.
> [Alternate Method: Automated Touch traffic using NGA Framework + IVT Released NGA supported WIM image  -- (More info TBD) ]
>
>   - NGA Testline for CR Regression existing example used in MTL-S/MTP-S project :  https://nga.laas.intel.com/#/mtl_validation/planning/testlines/13898a8c-5b6d-4a13-873e-9eba1ef39b17 
>   - If using the existing testline above, validator/user will need to port it over to PTL/NVL compliant framework/environment,  using IVT released WIM image for PTL/NVL with NGA support and all its' dependencies included.
> SW Requirements:
>
>   - RVP/SUT booted to Ubuntu OS.
>   - Ubuntu OS is with built-in HID-SPI/I2C kernel driver.
> HW Requirements:
>
>   - RVP booted to Ubuntu OS with feature rework to enable I2C interface on THC0 port and SPI interface on THC1 port.
>   - 3PE POR touch panel (i.e. BOM52, BOM36A/37A) with touch cable connected on RVP via the THC0/THC1 touch port/connector.
>   - (Optional): Acute logic analyzer to monitor the HID-I2C traffic for debug/analysis purposes.
> (Note: Please check RVP schematic/layout to confirm the THC0/THC1 port naming label and location) 
> Dependencies:
>
>   - BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements / config.
> Test Pass Criteria:
>
>   - Using Solar tool, verify and ensure targeted 'N' number of Cold-Reset (CR) PM cycling count can be achieved successfully on RVP with NO error(s) returned/encountered. 
>   - Make sure NO yellow-bang occurred on THC0 and THC1 controller/Device Driver (i.e. QuickI2C).
>   - Extra: Ensure that the touch functionality of the HID-I2C/SPI touch device connected on THC0 still working normally after each CR cycled. Alternatively, this functionality can be verified by conducting both PIO and DMA operations/traffic tests.
> (Note: If NGA/Automation is not enabled especially during the early PO phase, manual touch gesture method by human input can be used to verify if touch panel still functional.)
> Test Fail Criteria:
>
>   - Unable to meet the passing criteria above.
>
>   - Solar tool unable to pass/complete the targeted CR cycling count with error code returned, 
>
>   - Yellow-bang occurred on THC - QuickI2C/SPI device and/or caused by any other drivers/devices, 
>   - Touch functionality of the touch device broken and not working after CR. (i.e. PIO and DMA operations/traffic tests failed.)
>   - Other IP/domains, Ubuntu OS, IFWI, SW driver or any other system/platform-level components utilized in this test that may cause the CR cycle failed to complete the targeted "N" Iterations of CR cycle.
> Intent Verify:
>
>   - Verify no yellow-bang or driver errors occur on THC controller/driver after any cold-reset cycle.
>   - Ensure the touch device maintains functionality post-reset (can operate via HID-SPI/I2C interface, PIO/DMA)
>
> Ecosystem SW Requirements:
>  Below is the example from NVL-Hx, please refer to Wiki for different project: THC Device Enablement on NVL-HX RVP1/RVP4 - Validation and Debug Encyclopedia - Intel Enterprise Wiki
>
>   - BIOS version:
>   - ifwi_nvl_hx_a1a0_pp_release_2025ww50.1.01/ or later version
>
>   - OS kernel:
>   - Window OS
>
> NVL-HX-CONS-25.48.2.287 or later version (NOTE: NON-POR, does not supported by driver team, intended solely for debugging/validation purposes)
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> \\amr.corp.intel.com\ec\proj\sv\Debug\PowerOn\NVL_Hx\NVL_Hx_A1\OS_Image\Ubuntu\ubuntu-24.04.3-desktop-amd64.iso
>
>   - Driver versions:
>   - Windows OS
>
>  https://ubit-artifactory-or.intel.com/artifactory/one-windows-local/Ingredients/hidi2c_touch/ OR https://ubit-artifactory-ba.intel.com/artifactory/owr-repos/Submissions/quickspi/
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
>                              Built in QuickSPI/QuickI2C kernel driver

### ✅ [TC] Cold Reset Cycles with THC0 (HID-SPI) Enabled only

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458307 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test Description:
>
>   - The objective/intent of this test is to conduct a thorough system-level regression that includes 'N' iterations of PM Cycling, specifically Cold Resets (CR), while running test traffic through a single port THC0 that configured as HID-SPI interface.
>                (Note: 'N' iterations of CR Cycling will be specified/defined at TR level, the number will vary across different project milestones.)
>
>   - This test is designed to implicitly encompass the verification of Fuses/Soft-straps loading, BIOS, SW Driver and THC Controller + TIC device initialization/enumeration sequences.
>   - It will also assess and exercise the HID-SPI PIO and DMA traffic cycling on the THC0 port to ensure robust touch operation between the host controller and TIC device.
>   - Solar SW tool will be utilized to initiate the PM regression/cycling test in Ubuntu OS environment.
>   - Additionally, the THC controller will undergo a reset during each Cold-Reset cycle. This CR sequence will implicitly encompass the various elements outlined in the IP-HAS. (Note: Refer to TCD --> 'Overview' Section for more information.)
>
> Pre-Test Configuration:
>
>   - THC0 port will required to be enabled in BIOS settings.
>   - RVP booted to BIOS Menu, go to Intel Advance Menu -> PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration ->   <Refer to the BKM settings below to enable THC0 port.>
>   - Note: The BKM below are captured from NVL RVP
>
> Test Steps / Procedure: 
>
>   - RVP booted to Ubuntu OS with all the HW/SW requirements met, follow and configure all the BIOS settings accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test Configuration' section above; Ensure latest Solar tool is installed.
>   - Check if there is yellow bang on THC touch panel with the following commands
> sudo lspci                     #list pci devices
> lspci -kvv -s 00:10.0   #list pci device with very detail kernel info and which bus device function
> The expected results is where "Kernel driver in use: intel_quickspi" & "Kernel modules: intel_quickspi" can be observed, no yellow bang occurs
>
>   - In Ubuntu OS, go into the Solar folder, right click to "open in terminal".
>   - Enter the following command to open the Solar GUI
> sudo ./SolarPanel.sh
>
>   - In Solar tool, validator/user may use the following settings below as example, which will enable 100 cycles of Cold-Reset with Yellow-Bank Check, Exit On Failure and Bypass Windows Login enabled.
>   - Tick to enable Yellow Bang Check in Advanced Verification PRE/POST Check tab.
>   - Tick to enable Exit On Failure in Knobs tab.
>   - Tick to enable Bypass Windows Login in Knobs tab.
>   - Specify 'N' total number of cycles per loop/test in Test Time tab. (e.g. 'N' = 100)
>   - Specify Start Delay Time (Optional)
>   - Specify total sleep time allowed, i.e. total allowed time to reboot/reset/wake the system back to Ubuntu OS state (e.g. 180 seconds)
>   - Specify total Active Time in Ubuntu OS before starting next PM cycle (e.g. 15 seconds) 
>   - Select the "Cold-Reset" button. It will be shown as green box once clicked, and the "Cold-Reset|sec:180|sec:15|Timer" will be shown in the big blue box in the right, which implied it is confirmed being set.
>   - Finally, click on the green play button to start the PM Regression/Cycling test. 
>
> (Note: Alternatively, instead of setup a new settings every time for each PM regression test, validator/user may load the existing config solar .xml file with pre-existing settings saved previously .)
>
>   - Wait for the CR cycling test to complete. Solar tool will return a Pass/Fail result in the "Test Status" section, the result will be shown in the log file as well.
> [Alternate Method: Automated Touch traffic using NGA Framework + IVT Released NGA supported WIM image  -- (More info TBD) ]
>
>   - NGA Testline for CR Regression existing example used in MTL-S/MTP-S project :  https://nga.laas.intel.com/#/mtl_validation/planning/testlines/13898a8c-5b6d-4a13-873e-9eba1ef39b17 
>   - If using the existing testline above, validator/user will need to port it over to PTL/NVL compliant framework/environment,  using IVT released WIM image for PTL/NVL with NGA support and all its' dependencies included.
> SW Requirements:
>
>   - RVP/SUT booted to Ubuntu OS.
>   - Ubuntu OS is with built-in HID-SPI kernel driver.
> HW Requirements:
>
>   - RVP booted to Ubuntu OS with feature rework to enable SPI interface on THC0 port.
>   - 3PE POR touch panel (i.e. BOM52, BOM36A/37A) with touch cable connected on RVP via the THC0 touch port/connector.
>   - (Optional): Acute logic analyzer to monitor the HID-SPI traffic for debug/analysis purposes.
> (Note: Please check RVP schematic/layout to confirm the THC0 port naming label and location) 
> Dependencies:
>
>   - BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements / config.
> Test Pass Criteria:
>
>   - Using Solar tool, verify and ensure targeted 'N' number of Cold-Reset (CR) PM cycling count can be achieved successfully on RVP with NO error(s) returned/encountered. 
>   - Make sure NO yellow-bang occurred on THC0 controller/Device Driver (i.e. QuickSPI).
>   - Extra: Ensure that the touch functionality of the HID-SPI touch device connected on THC0 still working normally after each CR cycled. Alternatively, this functionality can be verified by conducting both PIO and DMA operations/traffic tests.
> (Note: If NGA/Automation is not enabled especially during the early PO phase, manual touch gesture method by human input can be used to verify if touch panel still functional.)
> Test Fail Criteria:
>
>   - Unable to meet the passing criteria above.
>
>   - Solar tool unable to pass/complete the targeted CR cycling count with error code returned, 
>
>   - Yellow-bang occurred on THC - QuickSPI device and/or caused by any other drivers/devices, 
>   - Touch functionality of the touch device broken and not working after CR. (i.e. PIO and DMA operations/traffic tests failed.)
>   - Other IP/domains, Ubuntu OS, IFWI, SW driver or any other system/platform-level components utilized in this test that may cause the CR cycle failed to complete the targeted "N" Iterations of CR cycle.
> Intent Verify:
>
>   - Verify no yellow-bang or driver errors occur on THC controller/driver after any cold-reset cycle.
>   - Ensure the touch device maintains functionality post-reset (can operate via HID-SPI interface, PIO/DMA)
>
> Ecosystem SW Requirements:

>
> Pre-Test Configuration:
>
>   - THC0 and THC1 ports will required to be enabled in BIOS settings.
>   - RVP booted to BIOS Menu, go to Intel Advance Menu -> PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration ->   <Refer to the BKM settings below to enable THC0 and THC1 ports.>
>   - Note: The BKM below are captured from NVL RVP
>
> Test Steps / Procedure: 
>
>   - RVP booted to Ubuntu OS with all the HW/SW requirements met, follow and configure all the BIOS settings accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test Configuration' section above; Ensure latest Solar tool is installed.
>   - Check if there is yellow bang on THC touch panel with the following commands
> sudo lspci                     #list pci devices
> lspci -kvv -s 00:10.0   #list pci device with very detail kernel info and which bus device function
> The expected results is where "Kernel driver in use: intel_quicki2c" & "Kernel modules: intel_quicki2c" can be observed, no yellow bang occurs
>
>   - In Ubuntu OS, go into the Solar folder, right click to "open in terminal".
>   - Enter the following command to open the Solar GUI
> sudo ./SolarPanel.sh
>
>   - In Solar tool, validator/user may use the following settings below as example, which will enable 100 cycles of Cold-Reset with Yellow-Bank Check, Exit On Failure and Bypass Windows Login enabled.
>   - Tick to enable Yellow Bang Check in Advanced Verification PRE/POST Check tab.
>   - Tick to enable Exit On Failure in Knobs tab.
>   - Tick to enable Bypass Windows Login in Knobs tab.
>   - Specify 'N' total number of cycles per loop/test in Test Time tab. (e.g. 'N' = 100)
>   - Specify Start Delay Time (Optional)
>   - Specify total sleep time allowed, i.e. total allowed time to reboot/reset/wake the system back to Ubuntu OS state  (e.g. 180 seconds)
>   - Specify total Active Time in Ubuntu OS before starting next PM cycle (e.g. 15 seconds) 
>   - Select the "Cold-Reset" button. It will be shown as green box once clicked, and the "Cold-Reset|sec:180|sec:15|Timer" will be shown in the big blue box in the right, which implied it is confirmed being set.
>   - Finally, click on the green play button to start the PM Regression/Cycling test. 
>
> (Note: Alternatively, instead of setup a new settings every time for each PM regression test, validator/user may load the existing config solar .xml file with pre-existing settings saved previously .)
>
>   - Wait for the CR cycling test to complete. Solar tool will return a Pass/Fail result in the "Test Status" section, the result will be shown in the log file as well.
> [Alternate Method: Automated Touch traffic using NGA Framework + IVT Released NGA supported WIM image  -- (More info TBD) ]
>
>   - NGA Testline for CR Regression existing example used in MTL-S/MTP-S project :  https://nga.laas.intel.com/#/mtl_validation/planning/testlines/13898a8c-5b6d-4a13-873e-9eba1ef39b17 
>   - If using the existing testline above, validator/user will need to port it over to PTL/NVL compliant framework/environment,  using IVT released WIM image for PTL/NVL with NGA support and all its' dependencies included.
> SW Requirements:
>
>   - RVP/SUT booted to Ubuntu OS.
>   - Ubuntu OS is with built-in HID-SPI/I2C kernel driver.
> HW Requirements:
>
>   - RVP booted to Ubuntu OS with feature rework to enable SPI interface on THC0 port and I2C interface on THC1 port.
>   - 3PE POR touch panel (i.e. BOM52, BOM36A/37A) with touch cable connected on RVP via the THC0/THC1 touch port/connector.
>   - (Optional): Acute logic analyzer to monitor the HID-SPI/I2C traffic for debug/analysis purposes.
> (Note: Please check RVP schematic/layout to confirm the THC0/THC1 port naming label and location) 
> Dependencies:
>
>   - BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements / config.
> Test Pass Criteria:
>
>   - Using Solar tool, verify and ensure targeted 'N' number of Cold-Reset (CR) PM cycling count can be achieved successfully on RVP with NO error(s) returned/encountered. 
>   - Make sure NO yellow-bang occurred on THC0 and THC1 controller/Device Driver (i.e. QuickSPI/I2C).
>   - Extra: Ensure that the touch functionality of the HID-SPI/I2C touch device connected on THC0/THC1 still working normally after each CR cycled. Alternatively, this functionality can be verified by conducting both PIO and DMA operations/traffic tests.
> (Note: If NGA/Automation is not enabled especially during the early PO phase, manual touch gesture method by human input can be used to verify if touch panel still functional.)
> Test Fail Criteria:
>
>   - Unable to meet the passing criteria above.
>
>   - Solar tool unable to pass/complete the targeted CR cycling count with error code returned, 
>
>   - Yellow-bang occurred on THC - QuickSPI/I2C device and/or caused by any other drivers/devices, 
>   - Touch functionality of the touch device broken and not working after CR. (i.e. PIO and DMA operations/traffic tests failed.)
>   - Other IP/domains, Ubuntu OS, IFWI, SW driver or any other system/platform-level components utilized in this test that may cause the CR cycle failed to complete the targeted "N" Iterations of CR cycle.
> Intent Verify:
>
>   - Verify no yellow-bang or driver errors occur on THC controller/driver after any cold-reset cycle.
>   - Ensure the touch device maintains functionality post-reset (can operate via HID-SPI/I2C interface, PIO/DMA)
>
> Ecosystem SW Requirements:
>  Below is the example from NVL-Hx, please refer to Wiki for different project: THC Device Enablement on NVL-HX RVP1/RVP4 - Validation and Debug Encyclopedia - Intel Enterprise Wiki
>
>   - BIOS version:
>   - ifwi_nvl_hx_a1a0_pp_release_2025ww50.1.01/ or later version
>
>   - OS kernel:
>   - Window OS
>
> NVL-HX-CONS-25.48.2.287 or later version (NOTE: NON-POR, does not supported by driver team, intended solely for debugging/validation purposes)
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> \\amr.corp.intel.com\ec\proj\sv\Debug\PowerOn\NVL_Hx\NVL_Hx_A1\OS_Image\Ubuntu\ubuntu-24.04.3-desktop-amd64.iso
>
>   - Driver versions:
>   - Windows OS
>
>  https://ubit-artifactory-or.intel.com/artifactory/one-windows-local/Ingredients/hidi2c_touch/ OR https://ubit-artifactory-ba.intel.com/artifactory/owr-repos/Submissions/quickspi/
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
>                              Built in QuickSPI/QuickI2C kernel driver

---

### ✅ [TC] Cold Reset Cycles with THC0 (HID-SPI) and THC1 (HID-SPI) Enabled

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458309 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test Description:
>
>   - The objective/intent of this test is to conduct a thorough system-level regression that includes 'N' iterations of PM Cycling, specifically Cold Resets (CR), while running test traffic through both ports THC0 and THC1 that configured as HID-SPI interface.
>                (Note: 'N' iterations of CR Cycling will be specified/defined at TR level, the number will vary across different project milestones.)
>
>   - This test is designed to implicitly encompass the verification of Fuses/Soft-straps loading, BIOS, SW Driver and THC Controller + TIC device initialization/enumeration sequences.
>   - It will also assess and exercise the HID-SPI PIO and DMA traffic cycling on the THC0/THC1 port to ensure robust touch operation between the host controller and TIC device.
>   - Solar SW tool will be utilized to initiate the PM regression/cycling test in Ubuntu OS environment.
>   - Additionally, the THC controller will undergo a reset during each Cold-Reset cycle. This CR sequence will implicitly encompass the various elements outlined in the IP-HAS. (Note: Refer to TCD --> 'Overview' Section for more information.)
>
> Pre-Test Configuration:
>
>   - THC0 and THC1 ports will required to be enabled in BIOS settings.
>   - RVP booted to BIOS Menu, go to Intel Advance Menu -> PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration ->   <Refer to the BKM settings below to enable THC0 and THC1 ports.>
>   - Note: The BKM below are captured from NVL RVP
>
> Test Steps / Procedure: 
>
>   - RVP booted to Ubuntu OS with all the HW/SW requirements met, follow and configure all the BIOS settings accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test Configuration' section above; Ensure latest Solar tool is installed.
>   - Check if there is yellow bang on THC touch panel with the following commands
> sudo lspci                     #list pci devices
> lspci -kvv -s 00:10.0   #list pci device with very detail kernel info and which bus device function
> The expected results is where "Kernel driver in use: intel_quickspi" & "Kernel modules: intel_quickspi" can be observed, no yellow bang occurs
>
>   - In Ubuntu OS, go into the Solar folder, right click to "open in terminal".
>   - Enter the following command to open the Solar GUI
> sudo ./SolarPanel.sh
>
>   - In Solar tool, validator/user may use the following settings below as example, which will enable 100 cycles of Cold-Reset with Yellow-Bank Check, Exit On Failure and Bypass Windows Login enabled.
>   - Tick to enable Yellow Bang Check in Advanced Verification PRE/POST Check tab.
>   - Tick to enable Exit On Failure in Knobs tab.
>   - Tick to enable Bypass Windows Login in Knobs tab.
>   - Specify 'N' total number of cycles per loop/test in Test Time tab. (e.g. 'N' = 100)
>   - Specify Start Delay Time (Optional)
>   - Specify total sleep time allowed, i.e. total allowed time to reboot/reset/wake the system back to Ubuntu OS state  (e.g. 180 seconds)
>   - Specify total Active Time in Ubuntu OS before starting next PM cycle (e.g. 15 seconds) 
>   - Select the "Cold-Reset" button. It will be shown as green box once clicked, and the "Cold-Reset|sec:180|sec:15|Timer" will be shown in the big blue box in the right, which implied it is confirmed being set.
>   - Finally, click on the green play button to start the PM Regression/Cycling test. 
>
> (Note: Alternatively, instead of setup a new settings every time for each PM regression test, validator/user may load the existing config solar .xml file with pre-existing settings saved previously .)
>
>   - Wait for the CR cycling test to complete. Solar tool will return a Pass/Fail result in the "Test Status" section, the result will be shown in the log file as well.
> [Alternate Method: Automated Touch traffic using NGA Framework + IVT Released NGA supported WIM image  -- (More info TBD) ]
>
>   - NGA Testline for CR Regression existing example used in MTL-S/MTP-S project :  https://nga.laas.intel.com/#/mtl_validation/planning/testlines/13898a8c-5b6d-4a13-873e-9eba1ef39b17 
>   - If using the existing testline above, validator/user will need to port it over to PTL/NVL compliant framework/environment,  using IVT released WIM image for PTL/NVL with NGA support and all its' dependencies included.
> SW Requirements:
>
>   - RVP/SUT booted to Ubuntu OS.
>   - Ubuntu OS is with built-in HID-SPI kernel driver.
> HW Requirements:
>
>   - RVP booted to Ubuntu OS with feature rework to enable SPI interface on THC0 and THC1 ports.
>   - 3PE POR touch panel (i.e. BOM52, BOM36A/37A) with touch cable connected on RVP via the THC0/THC1 touch port/connector.
>   - (Optional): Acute logic analyzer to monitor the HID-SPI traffic for debug/analysis purposes.
> (Note: Please check RVP schematic/layout to confirm the THC0/THC1 port naming label and location) 
> Dependencies:
>
>   - BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements / config.
> Test Pass Criteria:
>
>   - Using Solar tool, verify and ensure targeted 'N' number of Cold-Reset (CR) PM cycling count can be achieved successfully on RVP with NO error(s) returned/encountered. 
>   - Make sure NO yellow-bang occurred on THC0 and THC1 controller/Device Driver (i.e. QuickSPI).
>   - Extra: Ensure that the touch functionality of the HID-SPI touch device connected on THC0/THC1 still working normally after each CR cycled. Alternatively, this functionality can be verified by conducting both PIO and DMA operations/traffic tests.
> (Note: If NGA/Automation is not enabled especially during the early PO phase, manual touch gesture method by human input can be used to verify if touch panel still functional.)
> Test Fail Criteria:
>
>   - Unable to meet the passing criteria above.
>
>   - Solar tool unable to pass/complete the targeted CR cycling count with error code returned, 
>
>   - Yellow-bang occurred on THC - QuickSPI device and/or caused by any other drivers/devices, 
>   - Touch functionality of the touch device broken and not working after CR. (i.e. PIO and DMA operations/traffic tests failed.)
>   - Other IP/domains, Ubuntu OS, IFWI, SW driver or any other system/platform-level components utilized in this test that may cause the CR cycle failed to complete the targeted "N" Iterations of CR cycle.
> Intent Verify:
>
>   - Verify no yellow-bang or driver errors occur on THC controller/driver after any cold-reset cycle.
>   - Ensure the touch device maintains functionality post-reset (can operate via HID-SPI interface, PIO/DMA)
>
> Ecosystem SW Requirements:
>  Below is the example from NVL-Hx, please refer to Wiki for different project: THC Device Enablement on NVL-HX RVP1/RVP4 - Validation and Debug Encyclopedia - Intel Enterprise Wiki
>
>   - BIOS version:
>   - ifwi_nvl_hx_a1a0_pp_release_2025ww50.1.01/ or later version
>
>   - OS kernel:
>   - Window OS
>
> NVL-HX-CONS-25.48.2.287 or later version (NOTE: NON-POR, does not supported by driver team, intended solely for debugging/validation purposes)
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> \\amr.corp.intel.com\ec\proj\sv\Debug\PowerOn\NVL_Hx\NVL_Hx_A1\OS_Image\Ubuntu\ubuntu-24.04.3-desktop-amd64.iso
>
>   - Driver versions:
>   - Windows OS
>
>  https://ubit-artifactory-or.intel.com/artifactory/one-windows-local/Ingredients/hidi2c_touch/ OR https://ubit-artifactory-ba.intel.com/artifactory/owr-repos/Submissions/quickspi/
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
>                              Built in QuickSPI/QuickI2C kernel driver
