### ✅ [TC] THC0 Interrupt Line Quiescing Mode Enabled/Disabled with Back-to-Back Device Interrupt

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458284 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test
> Description :
>
>
>   - This test is to verify and
>      ensure that the SW controlled touch device interrupt quiescing
>      mechanism/feature in THC-IP able to function normally as described in
>      below.   
>
>
>   - Once THC_DEVINT_QUIESCE_EN
>       bit enabled, THC HW will set THC_DEVINT_QUIESCE_HW_STS bit itself once RX
>       sequencer is IDLE, after completing processing of any current microframe
>       that started before the THC_DEVINT_QUIESCE_EN.
>
>             (Important note: SW cannot clear THC_DEVINT_QUIESCE_EN
>       until THC_DEVINT_QUIESCE_HW_STS bit is already set.)   
>
>   - Expectation:
>       With THC_DEVINT_QUIESCE_EN bit being set, THC HW (i.e. RxDMA
>       sequencer) will temporarily ignore any new touch device
>       interrupt (including touch and non-touch data interrupt), until
>       THC_DEVINT_QUIESCE_EN is cleared by SW later.
>
>   - When SW writes 1 to
>       THC_DEVINT_QUIESCE_EN bit, THC HW shall keep the bit read as 0 until it
>       completely received any ongoing micro-frame interrupt and then THC set it
>       to 1.
>
>   - After THC_DEVINT_QUIESCE_EN
>       is cleared by SW, THC shall start service device interrupt as usual.
>
>
> (Note: This quiescing mechanism does not
> affect/impact TXDMA and PIO operations. Both of these operations are supposed
> to work even when touch device interrupt is quiesced.)
>
>
>
> Pre-Test Configuration :
>
>
>   - THC0 port will required to be enabled in BIOS settings.
>
>   - RVP booted to BIOS Menu, go to Intel Advance Menu ->
>      PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration ->   <Refer to
>      the BKM settings below to enable THC0 port.>
>
>
>   - Note: The BKM below are captured from NVL RVP
>
>
>
>
>
> Test
> Steps / Procedure : 
>
>
>   - RVP successfully booted to
>      UEFI shell/Ubuntu OS with all the HW/SW requirements met, follow and
>      configure all the BIOS settings accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test
>      Configuration' section above.
>
>
>   - Make sure to save the new changes before exiting
>      BIOS Setup Menu, then allow the SUT to reboot itself for these changes to
>      take effect.
>
>   - In PythonSV, make sure to check the current status of
>      the quiesce registers with the following commands in below.
>
>           By default, quiescing mode should be disabled in normal operations,
>      i.e. both THC_DEVINT_QUIESCE_EN and THC_DEVINT_QUIESCE_HW_STS should
>      be cleared to 0. If not, configure the THC_DEVINT_QUIESCE_EN to 0.
>
>           soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_control.thc_devint_quiesce_en = 0
>
>           soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_control.thc_devint_quiesce_hw_sts = 0
>
>
>
>           Phase#1 - Enable Quiescing Mode:
>
>           (i) Enable
>      the touch device interrupt quiescing mode, and read to the register bit
>      should immediately reflect 1 as well (if no ongoing/pending touch device
>      micro-frame interrupt):
>
>
>      soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_control.thc_devint_quiesce_en = 1
>
>
>      soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_control.thc_devint_quiesce_en.read()   
>
>
>
>           (ii) Then, THC HW will flagged the THC_DEVINT_QUIESCE_HW_STS to 1
>      indicating the quiescing mode is already enabled and in effect. 
>
>                 Verify THC_DEVINT_QUIESCE_HW_STS bit is being set
>      to 1:
>
>                 soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_control.thc_devint_quiesce_en.read()
>
>
>
>           (iii) Perform PIO or TxDMA operations to make sure they are still
>      functioning as usual, even after interrupt quiesce mode is enabled. (Note:
>      Refer to PIO TCs for more details on how to execute PIO operations)
>
>
>
>           (iv) Trigger new touch device interrupt by asserting the interrupt
>      line on the INT# GPIO pin for THC0 port. (Note: Touch device
>      interrupt can be either touch data or non-touch data interrupt.)
>
>                 No RxDMA frames will be observed on the HID-SPI
>      traffic, as THC HW ignored the touch device interrupt. 
>
>
>
>           (v)  Once the THC_DEVINT_QUIESCE_EN bit enabled, the touch
>      functionality of the touch device will not work in this mode, as the
>      human-interface touch data interrupt/frame will only work in RxDMA
>      mode. 
>
>
>
>                    Extra: Additional checking on THC
>      Interrupt Status Register (THC_M_PRT_INT_STATUS)
>
>
>
>
> Bits
>
>    | 
>
>
> Access Type
>
>    | 
>
>
> Default
>
>    | 
>
>
> Description
>
>    | 
>
>
>
>
>
> 15
>
>    | 
>
>
> RW/1C/V
>
>    | 
>
>
> 1'b0
>
>    | 
>
>
>
> THC Device Raw Interrupt Status (DEV_RAW_INT_STS)
>
>
> Interrupt
>    Status when the THC device (I2C or SPI) asserts the device GPIO interrupt.
>    This bit is detected after level/edge, polarity detection and is independent
>    of the interrupt quiescing (THC_DEVINT_QUIESCE_EN). Even
>    THC_DEVINT_QUIESCE_EN=1, this bit is still set if the device asserts the
>    interrupt (in all level/edge, pos/neg interrupt modes). SW clears the bit by
>    writing 1 to the bit.
>
>
> If
>    the DEV_RAW_INT_EN bit is also 1, then an SW interrupt is generated. 
>
>
> 1 -
>    THC device (I2C or SPI) asserts the device GPIO interrupt.
>
>
> 0 -
>    No device raw GPIO interrupt assertion is detected.
>
>
> Note:
>    On LNL, due to an RTL issue,
>
>
>
>   - If Raw_INT_STS MSI is needed : Quiesce should be off
>
>   - If RAW_INT_STS MSI is not
>         needed , just plan to read : hardware should come out of D0i2 and check
>         interrupt and set RAW_INT_STS
>
>   - For line level-triggered interrupt, if TIC does not
>         toggle the line interrupt between uframe/frames, this bit will not be
>         set for the subsequent level interrupts even SW clears this bit.
>
>
>
>
>    | 
>
>
>
>
> Phase#2 - Disable Quiescing Mode:
>
> (i) Disable the quiesce mode by setting the following
> command: 
>
>
> soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_control.thc_devint_quiesce_en = 0
>
>
> soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_control.thc_devint_quiesce_en.read() 
>
>
>
> (ii)  Verify THC_DEVINT_QUIESCE_HW_STS bit
> is cleared to 0 by THC HW:
>
>
>  soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_control.thc_devint_quiesce_hw_sts.read()
>
>
>
> (iii) After both THC_DEVINT_QUIESCE_EN/_HW_STS
> bits are cleared, the RxDMA operation and touch functionality of the touch
> device will be working as usual. 
>
>
>   - Repeat for same test in step#3 for THC-I2C
>      interface. Or, THC-SPI depending on which interface is being validated
>      first. 
>
> SW
> Requirements :
>
>
>   - Install SW HID-SPI/-I2C
>      Driver on Ubuntu/Windows OS. (i.e. QuickSPI/QuickI2C driver for THC0 port)
>
>   - (Optional) For debugging,
>      users can utilize the TraceView tool to capture .ETL log files for
>      detailed analysis.
>
> HW
> Requirements :
>
>
>   - RVP booted to UEFI shell/Ubuntu OS/WinOS with feature
>      rework to enable SPI interface on THC0 port.
>
>   - PythonSV connected to host
>      system (via USB2DBC or XDP)
>
>   - 3PE POR touch panel 
>      (i.e. BOM52, BOM36A/37A) with touch cable connected on RVP via the THC0
>      touch port/connector.
>
>   - Acute logic analyzer to
>      monitor the HID-SPI traffic for debug/analysis purposes.
>
> (Note: Please check RVP schematic/layout to confirm
> the THC0 port naming label and location) 
>
> Dependencies :
>
>
>   - BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements /
>      config.
>
> Test
> Pass Criteria :
>
>
>   - Touch device interrupt quiescing mechanism function as
>      expected on THC0 port, i.e. observed expected outcome in both Phase#1
>      and Phase#2 testing (as detailed above).
>
> Test
> Fail Criteria :
>
>
>   - Unable to meet the passing criteria above. 
>
> Intent
> Verify:
>
>
>   - Verify that
>      the register readbacks of the quiesce enabled/HW status matches the
>      intended configuration.
>
>   - Validate that touch panel
>      interrupts are suppressed when quiesce mode is enabled, and that normal
>      interrupt handling resumes when quiesce mode is disabled.
>
> Ecosystem
> SW Requirements:
> Below is the example from NVL-Hx, please refer to Wiki for different project: THC Device Enablement on NVL-HX RVP1/RVP4 - Validation and Debug Encyclopedia - Intel Enterprise Wiki
>
>   - BIOS version:
>   - ifwi_nvl_hx_a1a0_pp_release_2025ww50.1.01/ or later version
>
>   - OS kernel:
>   - Window OS
>
> NVL-HX-CONS-25.48.2.287 or later version (NOTE: NON-POR, does not supported by driver team, intended solely for debugging/validation purposes)
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> \\amr.corp.intel.com\ec\proj\sv\Debug\PowerOn\NVL_Hx\NVL_Hx_A1\OS_Image\Ubuntu\ubuntu-24.04.3-desktop-amd64.iso
>
>   - Driver versions:
>   - Windows OS
>
>  https://ubit-artifactory-or.intel.com/artifactory/one-windows-local/Ingredients/hidi2c_touch/ OR https://ubit-artifactory-ba.intel.com/artifactory/owr-repos/Submissions/quickspi/
>
>   - Ubuntu OSBuilt in QuickSPI kernel driver

---

### ✅ [TC] THC0 RLTR (Memory) Programming to PMC via QoS_DMD [PM Telemetry#11, 12]

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458263 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test
> Description:
>
>
>   - THC
>      implements a controller-level Latency Tolerance Reporting (LTR) mechanism,
>      which allows the IP to send LTP message and its' values to PMC via IOSF-SB
>      whenever the LTR states changes (THC_LTR_EN bit = 1). If there are no
>      changes in the LTR states, THC shall not send the LTR message to PMC.
>
>   - The LTR
>      values for both active and low-power states are programmable by SW (e.g. BIOS, Driver) with {ACTIVE/LP}_LTR_VAL and {ACTIVE/LP}_LTR_SCALE registers.
>
>
>   - This test is
>      to verify that the Infinite, Active and Low power LTR messages and its'
>      values (from THC) are sent to PMC, whenever THC LTR states change (via
>      LAST_LTR_SENT_register).
>
>   - Once PMC
>      successfully received the LTR messages from THC, those LTR values will be
>      reflected in the PMC register (i.e. LTR_C2P2_IP_THC0/1)
>
>   - The test
>      will be run in PythonSV while SUT is booted to WinOS or UEFI Shell
>      environment with THC Port0/1 function enabled in BIOS . 
>
>
>
> Starting from MTL program onwards, THC will support
> Chassis 2.2 specification, which replaced the legacy LTR for Memory Access
> resources.
>
> THC will send memory resources LTR (a.k.a RLTR) to PMC
> via QOS_DMD mapped signals  on IOSF-SB channel.
>
>
>
>  Pre-Test Configuration:
>
>
>   - THC0 port will required to be enabled in BIOS settings.
>
>   - RVP booted to BIOS Menu, go to Intel Advance Menu ->
>      PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration ->   <Refer to
>      the BKM settings below to enable THC0 port.>
>
>   - Note: The BKM below are captured from NVL RVP
>
>
>
> Test Steps
> / Procedure:
>
>
>   - RVP
>      successfully booted to UEFI shell with all the HW requirements met with
>      all the BIOS configuration done accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test
>      Configuration' section above.
>
>   - Perform
>      Active/LP/Infinite LTR tests separately, by switching to one LTR_state at
>      a time.
>
> (a) Active LTR (LAST_LTR_SENT = 1)
>
>  THC sends Active LTR messages to PMC in any one of the following
> conditions in below:
>
>
>   - Any port's
>      RXDMA Start bit = 1
>
>   - THC_LTR_EN =
>      1
>
>   - Program both
>      ACT_LTR_VAL/SCALE accordingly
>
>   - When SW set
>      both THC ACT_LTR_EN/REQ bits
>
> [Note:
> Make sure to clear both LP_LTR_EN/REQ bits.]
>
>
>
> SW Flow:
>
> pch.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_read_dma_cntrl_1.start = 0
>
> pch.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_read_dma_cntrl_2.start = 1
>
> pch.thc0.pvt.thc_sb_pm_ctrl.thc_ltr_en = 1
>
> pch.thc0.mem.thc_m_cmn_ltr_ctrl.lp_ltr_en = 0 
>
> pch.thc0.mem.thc_m_cmn_ltr_ctrl.lp_ltr_req = 0 
>
>
>
> pch.thc0.mem.thc_m_cmn_ltr_ctrl.act_ltr_val = (Any value - Max 10
> bits supported)
>
> pch.thc0.mem.thc_m_cmn_ltr_ctrl.act_ltr_scale = (Any value between
> 0 ~ 5 supported)
>
> pch.thc0.mem.thc_m_cmn_ltr_ctrl.act_ltr_en = 1
>
> pch.thc0.mem.thc_m_cmn_ltr_ctrl.act_ltr_req = 1
>
>
>
> (b)  LP LTR (LAST_LTR_SENT = 2)
>
>  THC sends Low-Power LTR message to PMC in one of the following
> conditions:
>
>
>   - Any port's
>      RXDMA Start bit = 1 
>
>   - THC _LTR_EN
>      = 1 
>
>   - SW program
>      the LP_LTR_VAL/SCALE accordingly 
>
>   - SW set both
>      LP_LTR_EN/REQ bits
>
> [Note:
> Make sure to clear both ACT_LTR_EN/REQ bits.]
>
> [Note: If the LP_LTR_EN is set to 1
> while THC receives an device interrupt assertion, the LP LTR request will be
> ignored and THC won't send the LP_LTR message.]
>
>
>
> SW Flow:
>
> pch.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_read_dma_cntrl_1.start = 0
>
> pch.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_read_dma_cntrl_2.start = 1
>
> pch.thc0.pvt.thc_sb_pm_ctrl.thc_ltr_en = 1
>
> pch.thc0.mem.thc_m_cmn_ltr_ctrl.act_ltr_en = 0 
>
> pch.thc0.mem.thc_m_cmn_ltr_ctrl.act_ltr_req = 0 
>
>
>
> pch.thc0.mem.thc_m_cmn_ltr_ctrl.lp_ltr_val = (Any value - Max 10
> bits supported)
>
> pch.thc0.mem.thc_m_cmn_ltr_ctrl.lp_ltr_scale = (Any value between
> 0 ~ 5 supported)
>
> pch.thc0.mem.thc_m_cmn_ltr_ctrl.lp_ltr_en = 1
>
> pch.thc0.mem.thc_m_cmn_ltr_ctrl.lp_ltr_req = 1
>
>
>
> (c) Infinite LTR (LAST_LTR_SENT
> = 0)
>
>  THC sends the infinite LTR/No
> Requirement messages to PMC in any
> one of the following conditions:
>
>
>   - When all
>      port's RXDMA start bits are cleared, or
>
>   - SW can set
>      the LP_LTR_EN bit and clears the LP_LTR_REQ bit  [Note: Need to clear both
>      ACT_LTR_EN/REQ bits as well.], or
>
>
>   - Cold boot exit/VNN removal
>      exit, or
>
>   - Host warm/cold reset exit
>
>
>
> SW Flow:
>
> pch.thc0.pvt.thc_sb_pm_ctrl.thc_ltr_en = 1
>
>
>
> pch.thc0.mem.thc_m_cmn_ltr_ctrl.act_ltr_en = 0 
>
> pch.thc0.mem.thc_m_cmn_ltr_ctrl.act_ltr_req = 0 
>
> pch.thc0.mem.thc_m_cmn_ltr_ctrl.lp_ltr_req = 0 
>
> pch.thc0.mem.thc_m_cmn_ltr_ctrl.lp_ltr_en =
> 1  
>
>
>
> OR
>
>
>
> ## When the all ports' RXDMA's start bits are cleared
>
> pch.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_read_dma_cntrl_1.start = 0
>
> pch.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_read_dma_cntrl_2.start = 0
>
>
>
> SW
> Requirements:
>
>
>   - N/A
>
> HW
> Requirements:
>
>
>   - RVP booted to UEFI Shell or Windows OS.
>
>   - PythonSV with USB2DBC debug
>      cable connected from SUT/RVP to host system.
>
> Dependencies:
>
>
>   - BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements /
>      config.
>
> Test
> Pass Criteria:
>
>
>   - Verified the LAST_LTR_SENT
>      register is sent by THC HW, whenever THC LTR state changes
>
>   - Check if the LTR values are
>      reflected in the PMC register as well (i.e. LTR_C2P2_IP_THC0/1)
>
> Test
> Fail Criteria:
>
>
>   - Unable to meet any of the passing criteria above.
>
> Intent
> Verify:
>
>
>   - Verify that each LTR state are able to send messages to
>      PMC with its respective conditions
>
>   - Validate that the LTR values are reflected in the PMC
>      register when message is sent from THC to PMC
>
> Ecosystem
> SW Requirements:
>
> Below is the example from NVL-Hx, please refer to Wiki for different project: THC Device Enablement on NVL-HX RVP1/RVP4 - Validation and Debug Encyclopedia - Intel Enterprise Wiki
>
>   - BIOS version:
>   - ifwi_nvl_hx_a1a0_pp_release_2025ww50.1.01/ or later version
>
>   - OS kernel:
>   - Window OS
>
> NVL-HX-CONS-25.48.2.287 or later version (NOTE: NON-POR, does not supported by driver team, intended solely for debugging/validation purposes)
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> \\amr.corp.intel.com\ec\proj\sv\Debug\PowerOn\NVL_Hx\NVL_Hx_A1\OS_Image\Ubuntu\ubuntu-24.04.3-desktop-amd64.iso
>
>   - Driver versions:
>   - Windows OS
>
>  https://ubit-artifactory-or.intel.com/artifactory/one-windows-local/Ingredients/hidi2c_touch/ OR https://ubit-artifactory-ba.intel.com/artifactory/owr-repos/Submissions/quickspi/
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> Built in QuickSPI kernel driver

### ✅ [TC] THC0 Register Checkout (PCI Config, MMIO, Private Spaces) via PythonSV

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458275 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test
> Description:
>
>
>   - The objective/intent of this
>      test is to validate both register accessibility of the THC0 port's
>      register space.
>
>   - Ensure the access over both
>      IOSF Primary and Sideband are supported and functional as expected.
>
>   - Ensure all default values
>      defined for every register field or register field-bit (check via
>      PythonSV) comply with the CRIF .xml file, as outlined for the program.
>
>
>
> This
> test is also designed to implicitly cover the PTL PCR below:
>
>
>   - This test is/can be carried
>      out in UEFI shell or WinOS environment during PTL PO.
>
>   - The THC-IP is located in
>      PTL-SOC/PCD support 16-bit port ID endpoint. 
>
>   - [PTL] CRIF/RDL
>      .xml file Link: thc_top_ptl.xml 
>
> Implicit coverage for PCR below:  
>
>
>
>
> HSD Link
>
>    | 
>
>
> PCR Title
>
>    | 
>
>
> Action/Coverage for Post-Si FV
>
>    | 
>
>
> Comments
>
>    | 
>
>
>
>
>
> 15010721030
>
>    | 
>
>
> [PTL-SoC] IP to
>   support 16-bit port ID endpoint
>
>    | 
>
>
> Able to cover
>
>    | 
>
>
> Existing register
>   checkout test in PythonSV able to cover this PCR.
>
>    | 
>
>
>
>
> Pre-Test Configuration:
>
>
>   - No specific configuration needed.
>
> Test Steps / Procedure: 
>
>
>   - Execute
>      bootscript in PythonSV to bring up RVP to post SOC/PCD bootstall
>      state/breakpoint, THC-IP register spaces should become accessible at this
>      stage.
>
>   - The bootscript is expected to halt/hook
>      to specified boot breakpoint, i.e. means that system will not continue
>      booting further past this breakpoint.
>
>   - Configure all 4 THC0 register spaces
>      access method to 'TAP2SB' to validate register accessibility flow via
>      IOSF-SB channel in PythonSV.
>
>   - Import
>      'thc_registers' script in PythonSV with the following command: "from vjt.thc import thc_registers".
>
>   - Read all the THC0 registers
>      with PythonSV command below: [Note:
>      THC0 register access method set to 'TAP2SB' by default.]
>
> thc_registers.thc_registers_dump(thc_num=0) 
>
>              # Read Register-level (By Default)
>
> thc_registers.thc_registers_dump(thc_num=0,
> reg_bit_en=True)      # Read Register Bits-level
>
>
>   - Write to THC0 register bits
>      with PythonSV command below: 
>
> thc_registers.thc_registers_dump(thc_num=0,
> write=True)   
>            # Write Register Bits-level (By Default)
>
>
>   - Check and review if there are
>      any failures/errors return by the script. (e.g. Mismatch register read
>      default values, missing registers, failed register write, etc.)
>
>   - Switch to IOSF-Primary access method
>      for all THC0 register spaces by using the PythonSV command shown in table
>      below. Then, Repeat steps #5 to #7.
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> Access Method 
>
>
> THC0
>   REG Spaces
>
>
>
>

>
>
>
> IOSF-Sideband
>
>
>
>
>
> (i.e. TAP2SB)
>
>
>
>
>    | 
>
>
>
>
> IOSF-Primary
>

>
>
> (i.e. PCI-CFG, MEM/MMIO, P2SB)
>

>    |

>   (PCI-CFG)

>

>

>

>

>
> SW/HW
> Requirements:
>
>
>   - RVP successfully booted to UEFI shell or WinOS.
>
>   - PythonSV with USB2DBC debug
>      cable connected from SUT/RVP to host system.
>
> Dependencies:
>
>
>   - N/A
>
> Test Pass Criteria:
>
>
>   - Able to perform register read/write
>      access on all THC0 registers in PythonSV, via both IOSF-SB and
>      IOSF-Primary access methods (shown in table above).
>
>   - No missing register in PythonSV vs
>      CRIF.
>
>   - Read out expected default value in
>      PythonSV for all the THC0 related registers as specified in the CRIF.
>      (with the exceptional of certain error/status register - if any)
>
> Test Fail Criteria:
>
>
>   - Unable to meet any of the passing
>      criteria above.
>
>   - Identified one or more missing THC0
>      register in PythonSV. 
>
>   - Failed to access one or more THC0
>      register or field bit via PythonSV.
>
>   - Unexpected default value of any THC0
>      register identified in PythonSV.
>
> Intent Verify:
>
>
>   - Cross-check each THC0 registers' value
>      between each access method via PythonSV.
>
>   - Verify the value for all THC related
>      registers are as expected and there are no missing register/mismatch in
>      value/failed register write.
>
> Ecosystem
> SW Requirements:
>
> Below is the example from NVL-Hx, please refer to Wiki for different project: THC Device Enablement on NVL-HX RVP1/RVP4 - Validation and Debug Encyclopedia - Intel Enterprise Wiki

>
>   - OS kernel:
>   - Window OS
>
> NVL-HX-CONS-25.48.2.287 or later version (NOTE: NON-POR, does not supported by driver team, intended solely for debugging/validation purposes)
>

> \\amr.corp.intel.com\ec\proj\sv\Debug\PowerOn\NVL_Hx\NVL_Hx_A1\OS_Image\Ubuntu\ubuntu-24.04.3-desktop-amd64.iso
>

### ✅ [TC] THC0 SW GPIO Interrupt driven from THC MMIO register (SWGPIO_INT) to vGPIO_THC2 pin

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458286 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test
> Description :
>
>
>   - The goal of this test is to
>      evaluate and confirm that the THC0's SW-GPIO interrupt register bit can
>      effectively assert and de-assert the vGPIO signal level (i.e. THC0 port =
>      vgpio_thc2), ensuring its accurate reflection on the GPIO-IP side via
>      register verification on vgpio_thc2.
>
>   - With a stretch goal, validate
>      the SW interrupt flow, specifically by triggering and sending Tier-1
>      SCI/GPE interrupt via vgpio_thc2 to PMC side. 
>
>   - This test can be carried out
>      with RVP on either UEFI shell or Ubuntu/WinOS environment. 
>
>   - This test is also designed to
>      have coverage for the PCR below:
>
>
>
>
> HSD Link
>
>
>
>
>    | 
>
>
>
>
>
> PCR Title
>
>
>
>
>    | 
>
>
>
>
>
> Comments
>
>
>
>
>    | 
>
>
>
>
>
> 22010402304 
>
>
>
>
>    | 
>
>
> THC (aka hSPI) IP
>   driven virtual GPIO for HIDclass operation
>
>
>
>
>    | 
>
>
> This BIOS changes
>   can be implicitly covered during the THC-SPI GPIO Register Configuration
>   checkout.
>
>
>
>
>    | 
>
>
>
>
>
>
> THC SW-GPIO Interrupt (via
> thc_m_prt_control register)
>
>
>
>
> Bit
>
>    | 
>
>
> Attribute
>
>    | 
>
>
> Default
>
>    | 
>
>
> Description
>
>    | 
>
>
>
>
>
> 4
>
>    | 
>
>
> RW
>
>    | 
>
>
> 1'b0
>
>    | 
>
>
> SW
>   GPIO Interrupt (SWGPIO_INT)
>
>
> 1:
>   Assert SW GPIO interrupt through vGPIO.
>
>
> 0:
>   De-assert SW GPIO interrupt through vGPIO.
>
>    | 
>
>
>
> Pre-Test Configuration :
>
>
>   - THC0 port will required to be enabled in BIOS settings.
>
>   - RVP booted to BIOS Menu, go to Intel Advance Menu ->
>      PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration ->   <Refer to
>      the BKM settings below to enable THC0 port.>
>
>   - To unlock all GPIO pads for SW configuration purpose, go
>      to Intel Advance Menu -> PCH-IO Configuration -> Security
>      Configuration -> Force unlock on all GPIO pads -> Enabled
>
>   - Note: The BKM below are captured from NVL RVP
>
>
>
> Test
> Steps / Procedure: 
> 1) RVP successfully booted to UEFI Shell with
> all the HW requirements met with all the BIOS configuration done accordingly as
> described in 'Pre-Test Configuration' section above.
> 2) In PythonSV, check and verify all the respective GPIO register
> bits are configured accordingly by BIOS for each and every THC0-SPI's GPIO
> pins. Compare the register output result with the table above.
>     E.g. Using MTL-P682 RVP as an example,
> read/show the following register values: 
>       (i) Perform itp.halt(), and then make sure to check for all cores are in
> halted status.  
>
>
>        (ii) Encode and configure all GPE*_DW* registers on both GPIO and PMC ends.
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> GPIO-IP
>
>
>    | 
>
> PMC-IP
>
>    | 
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>    | 
>
>
>
>
>
>    | 
>
> 3) Enable
> SCI Interrupt on vgpio_thc2 pad, when all of the following registers are
> configured accordingly:
>
>     (a) Pad under host ownership
>
>           soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.pad_own_vgpio_3_1.pad_own_vgpio_thc2 = 0 
>
>      (b)
> GPIO input mode
>           soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.pad_cfg_dw0_vgpio_thc2.gpiorxdis = 0 
>           soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.pad_cfg_dw0_vgpio_thc2.gpiotxdis = 1 
>
>      (c)
> Host Software Pad Owner (HOSTSW_OWN) is set to 0x0 (ACPI mode)
>             soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.hostsw_own_vgpio_3_0.hostsw_own_vgpio_thc2 = 0 
>
>     (d)
> The corresponding GPI GPE enable bit is set to 0x1
>             soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.gpi_gpe_en_vgpio_3_0.gpi_gpe_en_vgpio_thc2 = 1 
>
>     (e)
> GPIO Input Route SCI (GPIRoutSCI) is set to 0x1
>            soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.pad_cfg_dw0_vgpio_thc2.gpiroutsci = 1 
>
>
>     (f)  GPE
> GPI/SCI interrupt is a level based interrupt: 
>             soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.pad_cfg_dw0_vgpio_thc2.rxevcfg = 0 
>
>     (g)
> RxINV bit set to 0 (No inversion - interrupt trigger at active-high signal
> level): 
>             soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.pad_cfg_dw0_vgpio_thc2.rxinv = 0 
>
>     (h)
> Enable IOSF-SB message initiation of GPIO interrupt event: 
>
>
> 4)
> Find and read the current value of PCI BAR (bar1/ba_hi << 32 +
> bar0/ba_low) for THC0 port: 
> In :  soc.south.psf.showsearch('thc0')
> psf8.psf_8_agnt_t0_shdw_bar0_thc0_rs0_d16_f0_offset29 = 0x1ca10000
> psf8.psf_8_agnt_t0_shdw_bar1_thc0_rs0_d16_f0_offset29 = 0x60
> psf8.psf_8_agnt_t0_shdw_pcien_thc0_rs0_d16_f0_offset29 = 0x2
> psf8.psf_8_agnt_t0_shdw_pmcsr_thc0_rs0_d16_f0_offset29 = 0x0
> psf8.psf_8_agnt_t0_shdw_cfg_dis_thc0_rs0_d16_f0_offset29 = 0x0
> psf8.psf_8_t0_agent_function_config_thc0_rs0_d16_f0_offset29 = 0x100
> psf8.psf_8_agent_tgt_rootspace_thc0_rs0_d16_f0_offset29 = 0x0
>
> pcibar_hi = soc.south.psf.psf8.psf_8_agnt_t0_shdw_bar1_thc0_rs0_d16_f0_offset29.read()
> pcibar_low = soc.south.psf.psf8.psf_8_agnt_t0_shdw_bar0_thc0_rs0_d16_f0_offset29.read()
>
> pcibar = (pcibar_hi << 32) + pcibar_low
>
> In : pcibar 
> 0x601CA10000
>
> 5) Find and read the address offset for THC_M_PRT_CONTROL register, and the field-bit position of SWGPIO_INT.
>    Then, trigger SCI interrupt by writing to the same register as follows: 
> reg_addr = soc.south.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_control.get_address_map_info()['u_thc_mmio_port']['offset']
> swgpio_bit = soc.south.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_control.swgpio_int.lowerbit
>
> # Read THC_M_PRT_CONTROL register using IPC/ITP memory read method vs Namednodes read (via 'tap2sb') 
> # Expect both read methods to have the same values
> itp.threads[0].mem('0x%xp' % (pcibar + reg_addr), 0x4)
> soc.south.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_control
>
> read_thc0_prt_ctrl = int(itp.threads[0].mem('0x%xp' % (pcibar + reg_addr),0x4))
>
> # Write 1 to SWGPIO_INT[4] bit in THC_M_PRT_CONTROL register. 
> itp.threads[0].mem('0x%xp' % (pcibar + reg_addr), 0x4, (read_thc0_prt_ctrl + (1 << swgpio_bit)))
>
> # Read back THC_M_PRT_CONTROL register and the namednodes 
>
> itp.threads[0].mem('0x%xp' % (pcibar + reg_addr), 0x4)
> soc.south.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_control.read()
> soc.south.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_control.swgpio_int.read()
>
> 6) Check and read the status PAD_CFG_DW0_VGPIO_THC2 register, i.e. GPIORxState will change from 0  to 1  (After SWGPIO_INT set to 1).
> In :  soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.pad_cfg_dw0_vgpio_thc2
> 0x40080102
>

> SW
> Requirements:
>
>
>   - N/A
>
> HW
> Requirements:
>
>
>   - RVP booted to UEFI Shell with feature rework to enable
>      SPI interface on THC0 port.
>
>   - 3PE POR touch panel 
>      (i.e. BOM52, BOM36A/37A) with touch cable connected on RVP via the THC0
>      touch port/connector.
>
>   - PythonSV with USB2DBC debug
>      cable connected from SUT/RVP to host system.
>
>   - (Optional): Acute logic
>      analyzer to monitor the HID-SPI traffic for debug/analysis purposes. 
>
>
> (Note: Please check RVP schematic/layout to confirm
> the THC0 port naming label and location) 
> Dependencies:
>
>
>   - BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements /
>      config.
>
> Test
> Pass Criteria:
>
>
>   - THC0's SW-GPIO interrupt
>      register bit can effectively assert and de-assert the vGPIO signal level
>      (i.e. THC0 port = vgpio_thc2), ensuring it is reflected on GPIO via
>      register verification on vgpio_thc2. (i.e.
>      thc_m_prt_control.swgpio_int  =  pad_cfg_dw0_vgpio_thc2.gpiorxstate
>      )
>
>   - [Stretch-Goal]:  The
>      assertion of THC0's SW-GPIO interrupt able to trigger and generate Tier-1
>      SCI/GPE interrupt via vgpio_thc2 to PMC.
>
>   - On contrary, Tier-1
>      SCI/GPE interrupt can be cleared/de-asserted once THC0's SW-GPIO
>      interrupt is de-asserted and the interrupt removed on
>      vGPIO_THC2. 
>
> Test
> Fail Criteria:
>
>
>   - Unable to meet any of the passing criteria
>      above.  
>
> Intent
> Verify:
>
>
>   - Verify that the system correctly reflects interrupt
>      status changes at each stage, including GPIO and PMC status indicators.
>
>   - Ensure the
>      touch controller interface and related system components respond as
>      expected to interrupt events, with no unexpected error/loss of
>      functionality.
>
>   - Confirm the
>      SCI interrupt for THC can be successfully configured, asserted and
>      de-asserted throught both software and hardware mechanisms.
>
> Ecosystem
> SW Requirements:
> Below is the example from NVL-Hx, please refer to Wiki for different project: THC Device Enablement on NVL-HX RVP1/RVP4 - Validation and Debug Encyclopedia - Intel Enterprise Wiki
>
>   - BIOS version:
>   - ifwi_nvl_hx_a1a0_pp_release_2025ww50.1.01/ or later version
>
>   - OS kernel:
>   - Window OS
>
> NVL-HX-CONS-25.48.2.287 or later version (NOTE: NON-POR, does not supported by driver team, intended solely for debugging/validation purposes)
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> \\amr.corp.intel.com\ec\proj\sv\Debug\PowerOn\NVL_Hx\NVL_Hx_A1\OS_Image\Ubuntu\ubuntu-24.04.3-desktop-amd64.iso
>
>   - Driver versions:
>   - Windows OS
>
>  https://ubit-artifactory-or.intel.com/artifactory/one-windows-local/Ingredients/hidi2c_touch/ OR https://ubit-artifactory-ba.intel.com/artifactory/owr-repos/Submissions/quickspi/
>
>   - Ubuntu OSBuilt in QuickSPI kernel driver

---

### ❌ [TC] THC0 Timestamping on Touch Input Report from RxDMA (when THC entering D0i2/D3)

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458399 |
| **Status** | rejected |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test Description :
>
>   - The objective/intent of this test is to verify that Timestamping feature on THC0 port is enabled and functional as expected as stated in the test steps and TCD.
>
> Pre-Test Configuration :
>
>   - BKM for enabling BOM36A Touch Panel (QuickSPI/HIDSPI), or
>   - BKM for enabling BOM37A Touch Panel (QuickI2C/HIDI2C)
> SW Requirements :
>
>   - Install SW HID-SPI/I2C Driver on Windows OS. (i.e. QuickSPI/QuickI2C driver for THC0 port) 
>   - (Optional) For debugging, users can utilize the TraceView tool to capture .ETL log files, enabling detailed analysis of HID-SPI/I2C traffic/data payloads, interrupt rates/frequencies, and error/failure logging.
>
> HW Requirements :
>
>   - PTL RVP booted to WinOS with feature rework to enable SPI/I2C interface on THC0 port.   
>   - 3PE POR touch panel  (i.e. BOM36/37) with touch cable connected on PTL RVP via the THC0 touch port/connector.
>   - Acute logic analyzer to monitor the HID-SPI/I2C traffic for debug/analysis purposes.
>   - PythonSV with USB2DBC debug cable connected from SUT/RVP to host system.
>   - Optional: Custom rework required to wire-out all the THC-SPI/I2C IO pins for Acute LA signals tracing. (Note: Make sure to use single-core wires and keep the length as short as possible to avoid/minimize any signal integrity impact.)
> (Note: Please check PTL RVP schematic/layout to confirm the THC0 port naming label and location) 
> Dependencies:
>
>   - BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements / config.  
>
> Test Steps / Procedure : 
> 1) PTL RVP successfully booted to WinOS with all the HW/SW requirements met with all the BIOS configuration done accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test Configuration' section above.
>
> 2) In PythonSV, enable timestamping feature on THC0 port by programming the following registers, and read back the registers to verify the configured values: 
>      soc.thc0.pvt.thc_sb_pm_ctrl.thc_ts_d0i2_mode = 0         # TS timer paused during D0i2 entry
>      soc.thc0.pvt.thc_sb_pm_ctrl.thc_microsec_cnt= 0x19     # Using SoC PGCB_CLK frequency for THC generate 10 us tick internally
>      soc.thc0.pvt.thc_sb_pm_ctrl.thc_timestamp_src= 0         # 0 - External PMC clocks tick as reference , 1 - Internal counter on core CLK 
>      soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_timestamp_2.thc_timestamp_en = 1  # Enable timestamp feature
>
> 3) Generate RxDMA input touch report by performing touch gestures onto the touch device, and then obtain the current timestamp value by reading the following register namednode in PythonSV. 
>      Poll reading the register for continuous dumping the timestamp value in PythonSV for ease of analysis/verification purpose. 
>       while(1):  print(soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_timestamp_2.thc_timestamp.read());     # Poll read timestamp value 
>
> 4) Check and verify that the returned value of timestamp for each and every input report keeps incrementing from previous returned value (in every 10 us tick), 
>      whether or not THC0 port entered D0i2 idle state, as timer is paused and will NOT reset back to 0 (when THC_TS_D0I2_MODE set to 0) during D0i2 entry. 
>      (Note: After every D0i2 exit, the timer starts incrementing immediately out of D0i2 without waiting for the next input report frame.)
>
> 5) Wait for a couple of seconds (depending on the configured D0I2_ENTRY_TIMER register value).
>     When THC0 port entered D0i2 idle state (i.e. No activity on THC-SPI/I2C interface signal lines), the timestamp's timer will paused and the THC_TIMESTAMP register will not reset back to a value of 0.
>     Read to THC_TIMESTAMP register will returned the previous timestamp value from the last input report 
>      soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_timestamp_2.thc_timestamp.read()       # Read current THC timestamp 
>
> 6) Place the THC0 port into D3 power state by using the following PythonSV command,  or by disabling the THC0 device in WinOS Device Manager.
>     Read to THC_TIMESTAMP register will returned a value of 0, as the timestamp's timer will always reset to 0 during D3 entry.
>      soc.thc0.cfg.thc_cfg_pmd_pmcsrbse_pmcsr.pwrst = 0x3        # 0 - D0 state, 3 - D3 state 
>
> 7) (a) Enable timestamp timer mode (i.e. THC_TS_D0I2_MODE=1):  
>            soc.thc0.pvt.thc_sb_pm_ctrl.thc_ts_d0i2_mode = 1
>     (b) Configure the D0i2 entry timeout value to max value (i.e. 0b1001 / 0x9h = 10 seconds).
>            soc.thc0.pvt.thc_sb_pm_ctrl.d0i2_entry_timer = 0x9
>
>     (c) Poll reading to THC_TIMESTAMP register in PythonSV ; the returned value will keep incrementing in every 10 us tick as expected. 
>            while(1):  print(soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_timestamp_2.thc_timestamp.read());     # Poll read timestamp value 
>
>     (d) And, then perform the following tests for two different scenarios: 
> 		Scenario A: D0I2_ENTRY_TIMER timeout occurred between each input report
>                  Expected outcome: The timestamp's timer will reset to 0 during D0i2 entry. Read to THC_TIMESTAMP register will returned a value of 0. 
>                  After every D0i2 exit, the timer starts incrementing when the first input report frame is received, i.e. the timestamp for the first frame out of D0i2 is always starts from 0. 
>
> 		Scenario B: Continuous Input Touch Report before D0I2_ENTRY_TIMER timeout occurred
> 		Perform tap-and-hold gesture onto the touch device to generate input touch reports back-to-back so that THC0 port will always remained in active state. 
>                 Expected outcome: Poll reading to THC_TIMESTAMP register; the returned value will keep incrementing in every 10 us tick as expected. 
>
> 8) Stop the register poll reading in PythonSV.  
>
> 9) Repeat same steps #3 to #8 with a different timestamp clock reference/source (i.e. THC_TIMESTAMP_SRC = 1) 
>      soc.thc0.pvt.thc_sb_pm_ctrl.thc_timestamp_src= 1         # 1 - Internal counter on core CLK 
>
> Additional Note: 
>
>   - THC has option to use either an external microsecond tick reference from SoC(PMC), or an internal core clock counter to generate the 10 microsecond pulse based on THC_TIMESTAMP_SRC register bit configuration.
>   - Timestamping is not supported on SWDMA, TXDMA or PIO.
>
> Test Pass Criteria :
>
>   - Timestamping feature on THC0 port is working as expected as stated in this TC's test steps above. 
> Test Fail Criteria :
>
>   - Unable to meet any of the passing criteria above.

---

### ❌ [TC] THC0 Touch Panel Display Sync (DSYNC) Signal to THC via TCON GPIO

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458401 |
| **Status** | rejected |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test Description :
>
>   - The objective/intent of this test is to verify and ensure that TCON Display SYNC (DSYNC) GPIO function working as expected on THC0 port when touching the BOM36A/37A display screen. 
>
> Pre-Test Configuration :
>
>   - BKM for enabling BOM36A Touch Panel (QuickSPI/HIDSPI), or
>   - BKM for enabling BOM37A Touch Panel (QuickI2C/HIDI2C)
> SW Requirements :
>
>   - Install SW HID-SPI/I2C Driver on Windows OS. (i.e. QuickSPI/QuickI2C driver for THC0 port) 
>   - (Optional) For debugging, users can utilize the TraceView tool to capture .ETL log files, enabling detailed analysis of HID-SPI/I2C traffic/data payloads, interrupt rates/frequencies, and error/failure logging. 
>
> HW Requirements :
>
>   - PTL RVP booted to WinOS with feature rework to enable SPI/I2C interface on THC0 port.   
>   - 3PE POR touch panel  (i.e. BOM36/37) with touch cable connected on PTL RVP via the THC0 touch port/connector.
>   - Acute logic analyzer to monitor the HID-SPI/I2C traffic for debug/analysis purposes.
>   - PythonSV with USB2DBC debug cable connected from SUT/RVP to host system.
>   - Optional: Custom rework required to wire-out all the THC-SPI/I2C/DSYNC IO pins for Acute LA signals tracing. (Note: Make sure to use single-core wires and keep the length as short as possible to avoid/minimize any signal integrity impact.)
> (Note: Please check PTL RVP schematic/layout to confirm the THC0 port naming label and location)
> Dependencies:
>
>   - BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements / config.  
>
> Test Steps / Procedure : 
> 1) PTL RVP successfully booted to WinOS with all the HW/SW requirements met with all the BIOS configuration done accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test Configuration' section above.
>
> 2) Dump the GPP_E_22 GPIO pin/pad related registers in PythonSV, check and ensure that all the configuration are done appropriately as expected for TCON-GPIO display sync support.
> (Note: Please check the PAD_CFG_DWx, pmode, iosstate, iosterm registers configuration on GPP_E_22 pin.)
>
> TCON DSYNC Disabled
>  | 
> TCON DSYNC Enabled
>
>  | 
>
>  | 
>
>  | 
>
> 3) In PythonSV, enable TCON Display Sync feature on THC0 port by programming the following registers, and read back the registers to verify the configured values: 
>     Important Note:
>
>   - The Sync signal source is selected by SW based on user or policy setting before enabling the coalescing using DISP_SYNC_EVT_SRC register.
>   - SW shall make sure the corresponding DISP_SYNC_EVT_SRC is enabled before enabling the SYNC_EVT interrupt.
> (a) Enable timestamp capability on THC0 port as DSYNC event timestamping using the exact same timer as Timestamping on Input Report (LNL+).
>      soc.thc0.pvt.thc_sb_pm_ctrl.thc_ts_d0i2_mode = 0         # TS timer paused during D0i2 entry (Will not reset back to 0)
>      soc.thc0.pvt.thc_sb_pm_ctrl.thc_microsec_cnt= 0x19     # Using SoC PGCB_CLK frequency for THC generate 10 us tick internally
>      soc.thc0.pvt.thc_sb_pm_ctrl.thc_timestamp_src= 0         # 0 - External PMC clocks tick as reference , 1 - Internal counter on core CLK 
>      soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_timestamp_2.thc_timestamp_en = 1  # Enable timestamp feature
> (b) Enable TCON DSYNC via GPIO capability on THC0 port. 
>       soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_disp_sync.disp_sync_evt_src = 0b10              # or 2 = Select GPIO SYNC as the source of DSYNC Event
>       soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_disp_sync.c_enable = 0                                      # 0- Disabled coalescing, 1- Enable coalescing based on the sync signal
>       soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_disp_sync.disp_sync_delay = 0                         # No Delay [William]: May need to change with delay timing ?? 
>       soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_disp_sync_2.disp_sync_gpio_mode = 0          # 0: SYNC assertion on GPIO rising edge, 1: Falling edge, 2: Both edge
>       soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_disp_sync_2.disp_sync_coal_delay_en = 0     #  No delay sync event for coalescing [William]: May need to change with delay timing ??
>       soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_disp_sync_2.disp_sync_int_delay_en = 0        #  No delay sync event for interrupt [William]: May need to change with delay timing ??
>       soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_int_en.disp_sync_evt_int_en = 1                        # Enable DSYNC event interrupt
>       soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_int_status.disp_sync_evt_int_sts = 1                # Write 1 to clear DSYNC event interrupt status bit
>
> 4) Read the initial starting value of these registers in below: 
>      soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_int_status.disp_sync_evt_int_sts.read()            # Must be 0 after write-1-to-clear
>      soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_timestamp_2.thc_timestamp.read()                   # Read the initial RxDMA input report timestamp
>      soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_sync_timestamp.read()                                         # Read the initial DSYNC Event timestamp
>
> 5) Poll reading the register for continuous dumping the timestamp value in PythonSV for ease of analysis/verification purpose. 
>     (Note: Both the RxDMA input report and DSYNC event timestamping capability are using the exact same timestamp timer.)
>       while(1): 
>           if soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_int_status.disp_sync_evt_int_sts == 1:     # DSYNC event occurred 
>
>               # Read current timestamp value: RxDMA2 input report/packet , Display SYNC Event Timestamp 
>                print(soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_timestamp_2.thc_timestamp.read(), soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_sync_timestamp.read())    
>                soc.thc0.port.mem.thc_m_prt_int_status.disp_sync_evt_int_sts = 1   # Write 1 to clear DSYNC event interrupt status bit
>
> 6) Generate multiple RxDMA input touch reports by performing touch gestures repetitively (e.g. tap-and-hold) onto the touch device for at least couple of seconds, and check the current DSYNC event timestamp register value.
> Expected outcome: 
>
>   - TCON Display Sync signal originated from Touch Panel (via eDP connector) is expected to toggle / logic transition "0->1->0" or vice-versa over time,
> whenever TCON GPIO input is received as a display frame sync signal on every display frame. 
>   - Once every DSYNC input signal assertion on GPIO rising/falling-edge detected on THC0 port, THC HW shall write the current time stamp to the CUR_SYNC_EVT_TS register. As a result, THC_M_PRT_SYNC_TIMESTAMP register returned value will increases over time whenever there's input touch activity/traffic on-going. 
>   - DISP_SYNC_EVT_INT_STS will be asserted when DSYNC event occurs, if DISP_SYNC_EVT_INT_EN is enabled. 
> (Note: SW is required to clear this bit before THC sends another SYNC MSI.)
>
> 7) Stop the register poll reading in PythonSV. And then, check and verify if the THC_M_PRT_SYNC_TIMESTAMP register returned value increased over time in each and every DSYNC input signal assertion.
>
> 8) Repeat the same test steps #2 to #7 with touch filter/smoothing INF driver extension installed on SUT's WinOS. 
>      (Note: For step #3, only check/verify if both SW driver/BIOS have configured all the required THC/GPIO registers to enable this TCON DSYNC feature.)
>
> Additional Note: 
>
>   - THC's timestamp timer has option to use either an external microsecond tick reference from SoC(PMC), or an internal core clock counter to generate the 10 microsecond pulse based on THC_TIMESTAMP_SRC register bit configuration.
>   - Timestamping is not supported on SWDMA, TXDMA or PIO.
>
> Test Pass Criteria :
>
>   - TCON Display SYNC (DSYNC) GPIO function working as expected on THC0 port as stated in this TC's test steps above. 
>   - Expectation: SW driver will enable and utilize this particular TCON GPIO DSYNC feature.
> Test Fail Criteria :
>
>   - Unable to meet any of the passing criteria above.

---

### ✅ [TC] THC0 WoT via vGPIO_THC0 as Interrupt pin to Wake System from S0ix

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458288 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test Description :
>
>
>   - The objective/intent of this
>      test is to verify that THC0 Wake-on-Touch (WoT) feature is
>      enabled and functional as expected in Ubuntu OS via a virtual GPIO pin
>      (i.e. vGPIO_THC0).
>
>
>
> Pre-Test Configuration :
>
>
>   - THC0 port will required to be enabled in BIOS settings.
>
>   - RVP booted to BIOS Menu, go to Intel Advance Menu ->
>      PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration ->   <Refer to
>      the BKM settings below to enable THC0 port.>
>
>   - RVP booted to BIOS Menu, go to Intel Advance Menu ->
>      PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration ->  Enabled Wake on
>      Touch 
>
>   - To unlock all GPIO pads for SW configuration purpose, go
>      to Intel Advance Menu -> PCH-IO Configuration -> Security
>      Configuration -> Force unlock on all GPIO pads -> Enabled
>
>   - To ensure the system goes into S0ix state, go to Intel
>      Advance Menu -> ACPI Configuration -> Enabled Low Power Idle
>      Capability
>
>   - Note: The BKM below are captured from NVL RVP
>
>
>
>
>
> Test
> Steps / Procedure : 
>
>
>   - RVP successfully booted to
>      Ubuntu OS with all the HW/SW requirements met with all the BIOS
>      configuration done accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test Configuration'
>      section above.
>
>   - Dump the vGPIO_THC0 related registers in PythonSV, check
>      and ensure that all the configurations are done appropriately as expected
>      for WoT support. 
>
> (Note: Please check the
> PAD_CFG_DW0, HOSTSW_OWN, and GPI_INT_EN/STS registers configuration on
> vGPIO_THC0 pin.)
>
>
>
>
> WoT
>   Disabled
>
>    | 
>
>
> WoT
>   Enabled
>
>    | 
>
>
>
>
>
>
>    | 
>
>
>
>    | 
>
>
>
>   - Enter the following command to verify kernel version and
>      THC kernel modules
>
> uname
> -r                                                           
> #kernel version
>
> lsmod
> | grep thc                                             
> #THC kernel modules
>
> lspci
> -nn | grep "Touch Host Controller"    
> #THC0 PCI device
>
>
>
>
>   - Check the Wake-on-Touch
>      enablement by entering the following commands
>
>
>
> THC_BDF=$(lspci
> -D | grep "Touch Host Controller" | head -1 | awk '{print $1}')              # Find THC0 BDF first
>
> echo
> "THC0 BDF: $THC_BDF"
>
> cat
> /sys/bus/pci/devices/$THC_BDF/power/wakeup                                                             
> # Check if wake is enabled, expected: "enabled"
>
>
>
> #
> If it shows "disabled", enable it
>
> echo
> enabled | sudo tee /sys/bus/pci/devices/$THC_BDF/power/wakeup
>
>
>   - On the RVP/SUT: Go to top right corner, click on the
>      power button -> suspend, to put the system into sleep state (a.k.a
>      Modern/Connected Standby, S0ix). Read the residency counters, the
>      residencies should be incrementing.
>
> (Note: Please wait for a couple
> of seconds for the system to completely entered S0ix state. Observe postcode
> transition from 0000 -> 00C5 --> 01C5)
>
>
>
>
>   - Perform Wake-on-Touch to wake SUT from CS/sleep state
>      (PostCode = ABC5). Read the residency counters again, S0i2.1/2.2 should
>      stop incrementing.
>
>
>   - Repeat steps #5 and #6 to perform WoT again for
>      another 5 or 10 cycles, to check for consistency.
>
>   - When
>      enter sleep state -> HID-SPI traffic traced with Saleae/Acute
>      LA: THC0 will send an single output report/packet to TIC for touch
>      panel device to enter sleep state.
>
> (Note: Expecting only single output report/packet,
> and NO other packets or activity observed on signal lines.)
>
>
>   - When wake from touch -> Observe
>      postcode transition from 01C5 -> ABC5. HID-SPI traffic traced with Saleae/Acute
>      LA: THC0 will send an single output report/packet to TIC for
>      touch panel device to power on.
>
>
>
> SW
> Requirements :
>
>
>   - Ubuntu
>      OS with built in HID-SPI/I2C kernel driver. 
>
>   - Install
>      SW HID-SPI/I2C driver in WinOS (i.e. QuickSPI/QuickI2C driver for THC0
>      port). 
>
>   - For
>      WinOS, install the WoT Extension INF Driver (i.e. WoT_Extension.inf) for
>      Wake-on-Touch support.
>
>   - For
>      WinOS, install Intel Serial IO GPIO Driver (which is part of the LPSS
>      driver stack) 
>
>   - (Optional)
>      For debugging, users can utilize the TraceView tool to capture .ETL log
>      files, enabling detailed analysis of HID-SPI/I2C traffic/data payloads,
>      interrupt rates/frequencies, and error/failure logging.
>
> HW
> Requirements :
>
>
>   - RVP
>      booted to Ubuntu/WinOS with feature rework to enable SPI/I2C interface on
>      THC0 port.   
>
>   - 3PE POR touch panel 
>      (i.e. BOM52, BOM36/37) with touch cable connected on RVP via the THC0
>      touch port/connector.
>
>   - Acute logic analyzer to
>      monitor the HID-SPI/I2C traffic for debug/analysis purposes.
>
>   - PythonSV with USB2DBC debug
>      cable connected from SUT/RVP to host system.
>
>   - Optional: Custom rework
>      required to wire-out all the THC-SPI/I2C IO pins for Acute LA signals
>      tracing. (Note: Make sure to use single-core wires and keep the
>      length as short as possible to avoid/minimize any signal integrity
>      impact.)
>
> (Note: Please check RVP schematic/layout to confirm
> the THC0 port naming label and location) 
>
> Dependencies:
>
>
>   - BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements /
>      config. 
>
>   - Ensure that WoT function is enabled on THC0 port in BIOS
>      settings.  
>
>   - GPIO-IP: Legacy GPIO Interrupt controlled by Host SW
>      GPIO driver mode.
>
>   - THC0 able to PG + CG, and entered into D3 power
>      state. 
>
> Test
> Pass Criteria :
>
>
>   - Able
>      to achieve N targeted number of cycles of WoT successfully.
>
>   - No
>      errors and Yellow-Bang observed on THC0 port. 
>
>   - SleepStudy
>      indicates system wake caused by Wake-on-Touch (WoT) only.
>
>   - THC0-SPI/I2C
>      signal lines are clean and no activity after the S0iX entry (i.e. No
>      periodic wakes/interrupts occurred) 
>
> Test
> Fail Criteria :
>
>
>   - Unable to meet any of the passing criteria above.
>
> Intent
> Verify:
>
>
>   - Verify that VGPIO_THC0/THC1 and PCI wake up settings are
>      correctly configured for WoT operation.
>
>   - Ensure kernel modules and PCI device are properly
>      detected and operational.
>
>   - Verify HID-SPI traffic shows only a single packet during
>      sleep/wake, indicating proper touch panel power management.
>
>   - Validate that the system reliably enters S0ix and wakes
>      on touch input, with correct residency counter and postcode transitions.
>
> Ecosystem
> SW Requirements:
> Below is the example from NVL-Hx, please refer to Wiki for different project: THC Device Enablement on NVL-HX RVP1/RVP4 - Validation and Debug Encyclopedia - Intel Enterprise Wiki
>
>   - BIOS version:
>   - ifwi_nvl_hx_a1a0_pp_release_2025ww50.1.01/ or later version
>
>   - OS kernel:
>   - Window OS
>
> NVL-HX-CONS-25.48.2.287 or later version (NOTE: NON-POR, does not supported by driver team, intended solely for debugging/validation purposes)
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> \\amr.corp.intel.com\ec\proj\sv\Debug\PowerOn\NVL_Hx\NVL_Hx_A1\OS_Image\Ubuntu\ubuntu-24.04.3-desktop-amd64.iso
>
>   - Driver versions:
>   - Windows OS
>
>  https://ubit-artifactory-or.intel.com/artifactory/one-windows-local/Ingredients/hidi2c_touch/ OR https://ubit-artifactory-ba.intel.com/artifactory/owr-repos/Submissions/quickspi/
>
>   - Ubuntu OSBuilt in QuickSPI kernel driver

### ✅ [TC] THC1  Function Disable Flow (BWG + HAS) and check PG/CGs

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458262 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test Description:
>
>   - The objective/intent of this test is to verify and ensure function disable flow initiated by BIOS on THC1 port is in compliance with the THC HAS specifications/requirements.
>   - This test can be carried out on the RVP either on UEFI shell or WinOS environment. 
>   - THC-IP Requirements for "Function Disable" according to HAS:   
>
>
> Pre-Test Configuration:
>
>   - THC1 port will required to be disabled in BIOS settings.   
>   - RVP booted to BIOS Menu, go to Intel Advance Menu -> PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration -> THC Port Configuration ->  <None>
> Test Steps / Procedure: 
>
>   - RVP successfully booted to UEFI shell with all the HW requirements met with all the BIOS configuration done accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test Configuration' section above.
>   - In PythonSV, use show or search CLI functions/commands to find and read the current value for relevant register namednodes, as shown in below. (e.g. using MTL-P682 RVP as an example)
>   -  According to THC HAS, BIOS will initiate a function disable on THC0 port by configuring the registers in the following sequence:
> ﻿i. Verify all clock gating controls enabled on THC registers
>
> In :  soc.south.showsearch('thc_sb_.*_c.._cfg')
> thc0.pvt.thc_sb_pr_cdc_cfg = 0xccc0
> thc0.pvt.thc_sb_sd_cdc_cfg = 0xccc0
> thc0.pvt.thc_sb_ssc_clk_cfg = 0x1
> thc0.pvt.thc_sb_rosc_clk_cfg = 0x1
> thc1.pvt.thc_sb_pr_cdc_cfg = 0xccc0
> thc1.pvt.thc_sb_sd_cdc_cfg = 0xccc0
> thc1.pvt.thc_sb_ssc_clk_cfg = 0x1
> thc1.pvt.thc_sb_rosc_clk_cfg = 0x1
>
> In :  soc.south.showsearch('thc_sb_dcge_cfg')
> thc0.pvt.thc_sb_dcge_cfg = 0x3f
> thc1.pvt.thc_sb_dcge_cfg = 0x3f
>
> ii. Verify that the device reset signal asserted low. Device reset signal level can be checked via "GPIOTxState" register bit on the THC_SPI/I2C_RST GPIO pin. [Note: Ensure PMODE is configured to 0 (non-native GPIO mode) with GPIOTxDis set to 0]
> In [84]: # THC1: SPI_RST# (for MTL-P682 RVP)
> In [86]: soc.south.gpio.com1.sb.pad_cfg_dw0_xxgpp_e_6.show
> -------> soc.south.gpio.com1.sb.pad_cfg_dw0_xxgpp_e_6.show()
> 0x00000001 : padrstcfg (31:30) -- \n                    [p]This register controls which reset is used to reset the specified Pad Register fields.  It is the SoC responsibilities to decide the number of confi...
> 0x00000000 : rxpadstsel (29:29) -- \n                    [p]Determine from which node the RX pad state for native function should be source from.[/p]\n                    [p]This field only affects the pad s...
> 0x00000000 : rxraw1 (28:28) -- \n                    [p]This bit determines if the selected pad state is being overridden to '1'.  This field only makes sense when the buffer is configured as an input in eit...
> 0x00000000 : naf_vwe (27:27) -- \n                    [p]This bit enables Native Function IOSF-SB Virtual wire message generation.  Upon the enabling of this bit, the virtual wire message is sent to the appr...
> 0x00000002 : rxevcfg (26:25) -- \n                    [p]Determine if the internal RX pad state (RXPadStSel=1) should be passed on to the next logic stage as is, as a pulse, or level signal.  This field does...
> 0x00000000 : pregfrxsel (24:24) -- \n                    [p]Determine if the synchronized version of the raw RX pad state should be subjected to glitch filter or not.  [/p]\n                    [p]This field...
> 0x00000000 : rxinv (23:23) -- \n                    [p]This bit determines if the selected pad state should go through the polarity inversion stage.  This field only makes sense when the RX buffer is configu...
> 0x00000000 : rxtxencfg (22:21) -- \n                    [p]This controls the RX and TX buffer enables for the function selected by Pad Mode, but is not applicable when Pad Mode is 0 (i.e. GPIO mode).  [/p]\n...
> 0x00000000 : gpiroutioxapic (20:20) -- \n                    [p]Determine if the pad can be routed to cause peripheral IRQ when configured in GPIO input mode.  If the pad is not configured in GPIO input mode...
> 0x00000000 : gpiroutsci (19:19) -- \n                    [p]Determine if the pad can be routed to cause SCI when configured in GPIO input mode.  If the pad is not configured in GPIO input mode, this field ha...
> 0x00000000 : gpiroutsmi (18:18) -- \n                    [p]Determine if the pad can be routed to cause SMI when configured in GPIO input mode.  If the pad is not configured in GPIO input mode, this field ha...
> 0x00000000 : gpiroutnmi (17:17) -- \n                    [p]Determine if the pad can be routed to cause NMI when configured in GPIO input mode.  If the pad is not configured in GPIO input mode, this field ha...
> 0x00000000 : rsvd_0 (16:14) -- Reserved
> 0x00000000 : pmode (13:10) -- \n                    [p]This field determines whether the Pad is controlled by GPIO controller logic or one of the native functions muxed onto the Pad.[/p]\n                   ...
> 0x00000001 : gpiorxdis (09:09) -- \n                   [p] RX buffer enable control when PMode = 0 ONLY.  No effect when the pad in native mode.[/p]\n                    0 = Enable the input buffer (active l...
> 0x00000000 : gpiotxdis (08:08) -- \n                    [p] TX buffer enable control when PMode = 0 ONLY.  No effect when the pad in native mode.[/p]\n                    0 = Enable the output buffer (active...
> 0x00000000 : rsvd_1 (07:02) -- Reserved
> 0x00000000 : gpiorxstate (01:01) -- \n                    [p]This is the current internal RX pad state after Glitch Filter logic stage and is not affected by PMode and RXINV, hardware debouncer (if any) sett...
> 0x00000000 : gpiotxstate (00:00) -- \n                    [p] TX state in when PMode = 0 ONLY.  No effect when the pad in native mode.[/p]\n                    0 = Drive a level '0' to the TX output pad[br]\...
>
> Alternatively, observe that THC_RST# signal will be asserted low as expected from the HID-SPI/I2C LA trace.
>
> iii. Verify the HAE bit in THC_CFG_PCE Space is set to 1.
>
> soc.south.thc0.cfg.thc_cfg_pce.show()
> In :  soc.south.thc0.cfg.thc_cfg_pce.show
> 0x00000000 : rsvd_31_6 (31:06) -- Reserved.
> 0x00000001 : hae (05:05) -- If set, then the PGCB may request a PG whenever it is idle.[br]\nThis bit is loaded with the value from the Power...
> 0x00000000 : rsvd_4 (04:04) -- Reserved.
> 0x00000001 : se (03:03) -- if clear, then IP will never asset Sleep to the retention flops. If set, then IP may assert Sleep during PG'ing. N...
> 0x00000000 : d3he (02:02) -- if set, then IP will PG when idle and the PMCSR[1:0] register in the IP = 11.
> 0x00000000 : i3e (01:01) -- if set, then IP will PG when idle and the D0i3 register (D0i3C[2] = 1) is set.
> 0x00000000 : spe (00:00) -- Software PowerGate Enable: If this bit is set, the IP will PG when pmc_sw_pg_req_b =0. NOTE: PMCSR[1:0] must not ...
>
> iv. Verify the THC0 function is set into D3 power state.
> In :  soc.south.thc0.showsearch('pwrst', 'f')
> cfg.thc_cfg_pmd_pmcsrbse_pmcsr.pwrst = 0x3
>
> In :  soc.south.psf.showsearch('thc1')
> psf8.psf_8_agnt_t0_shdw_bar0_thc1_rs0_d16_f1_offset30 = 0x0
> psf8.psf_8_agnt_t0_shdw_bar1_thc1_rs0_d16_f1_offset30 = 0x0
> psf8.psf_8_agnt_t0_shdw_pcien_thc1_rs0_d16_f1_offset30 = 0x100
> psf8.psf_8_agnt_t0_shdw_pmcsr_thc1_rs0_d16_f1_offset30 = 0x3          # THC0 D3 Status reflected on PSF8
> psf8.psf_8_agnt_t0_shdw_cfg_dis_thc1_rs0_d16_f1_offset30 = 0x0
> psf8.psf_8_t0_agent_function_config_thc1_rs0_d16_f1_offset30 = 0x100
> psf8.psf_8_agent_tgt_rootspace_thc1_rs0_d16_f1_offset30 = 0x0
>
> v. Verify the THC0 function disable bit in THC_CFG_UR_STS_CTL.FD is set to 1. (Note: When FD=1, THC will respond with UR for all IOSF primary non-posted cycles to THC and drop all incoming IOSF primary posted cycles.)
> In :  soc.south.thc0.showsearch('^fd$', 'f')
> cfg.thc_cfg_ur_sts_ctl.fd = 0x1
>
> vi. The THC shall enter PG by this stage. (Note: The entry sequence is the same to D3 state entry)
> # Import PM Test Script to check all SOC's IP CLK-Gating(CG) and PWR-Gating(PG) Status 
>
> from meteorlake.vjt.pm import cgpg 
>
> # Check and print out the current IP PG Status on SOC die
>
> cgpg.print_soc_all_ip_pg_sts()
>
> # Check and print out the current IP CG/CLKREQ Status on SOC die
>
> cgpg.print_socpll_pcg_clkreq_status()
>
>   - [Additional Verification]: Use Intel Trace-Hub tool to check the VISA signal states as shown in below for THC0/THC1.
>
> 5. Host reset is the only way to exit from the function disabled state on THC, as the FD bit will be cleared after the reset exit. After system reset, make sure to check the register bit is cleared to 0. (Note: just right before BIOS attempting to set this bit again in the next reboot/reset cycle)
>
> In :  soc.south.thc0.showsearch('^fd$','f')
> cfg.thc_cfg_ur_sts_ctl.fd = 0x0
> 6. Repeat steps #2 to #4 again for two more attempts to ensure the consistency of the test outcomes.
> SW Requirements:
>
>   - N/A
> HW Requirements:
>
>   - RVP booted to UEFI Shell or Windows OS.
>   - PythonSV with USB2DBC debug cable connected from SUT/RVP to host system.
>   - (Optional) Acute logic analyzer to monitor the THC-SPI/I2C GPIO signal states for debug/analysis purposes.
>   - (Optional) Intel Trace Hub tool for VISA signal analysis
> Dependencies:
>
>   - BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements / config.
> Test Pass Criteria:
>
>   - BIOS correctly configured all the relevant registers involved in function disable flow as specified in the test steps above. 
>   - [Additional/Stretch-Goal]: VISA signals states reflected correctly as expected as shown in the figure above. 
> Test Fail Criteria:
>
>   - Unable to meet any of the passing criteria above.
> Intent Verify:
>
>   - Verify THC enters D3 and PG states after FD bit is set, confirm the FD bit behavior as the following:
>   - FD=1 -> THC responds with UR for non-posted IOSF cycles and drops posted cycles.
>   - FD clears only after host reset.
>
>   - Validate debug visibility via Trace-Hub VISA signals for THC state.
>   - Ensure the BIOS re-applies FD bit after reset.
>
> Ecosystem SW Requirements:
> Below is the example from NVL-Hx, please refer to Wiki for different project: THC Device Enablement on NVL-HX RVP1/RVP4 - Validation and Debug Encyclopedia - Intel Enterprise Wiki
>
>   - BIOS version:
>   - ifwi_nvl_hx_a1a0_pp_release_2025ww50.1.01/ or later version
>
>   - OS kernel:
>   - Window OS
>
> NVL-HX-CONS-25.48.2.287 or later version (NOTE: NON-POR, does not supported by driver team, intended solely for debugging/validation purposes)
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> \\amr.corp.intel.com\ec\proj\sv\Debug\PowerOn\NVL_Hx\NVL_Hx_A1\OS_Image\Ubuntu\ubuntu-24.04.3-desktop-amd64.iso
>
>   - Driver versions:
>   - Windows OS
>
>  https://ubit-artifactory-or.intel.com/artifactory/one-windows-local/Ingredients/hidi2c_touch/ OR https://ubit-artifactory-ba.intel.com/artifactory/owr-repos/Submissions/quickspi/
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> Built in QuickSPI kernel driver

### ✅ [TC] THC1 (HID-I2C) BDF and DID Assignments

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458268 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test Description :
>
>   - The objective / intent of this test is to verify and ensure the Vendor/Device ID values are valid and correctly mapped to its' corresponding hardcoded BDF for both THC0 and THC1 ports on RVP, which can be verified in UEFI Shell, Windows' Device Manager, or THC register check via PythonSV.
>   - E.g. Using NVL-PCH-S (a.k.a NVP-S) as example for this instance: According to Chap06_NVP-S_RegMaps, the following table listed the PCI global device IDs and BDF for NVP-S :
>
>
> Pre-Test Configuration:
>
>   - In BIOS setting, enable both THC0 and THC1 ports, go to Intel Advance Menu -> PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration ->
>   - THC Port Configuration -> <THC0> , and, THC Mode <HID over I2C or HID over SPI>    ## To enable THC0 port
>   - THC Port Configuration -> <THC1> , and, THC Mode <HID over I2C or HID over SPI>    ## To enable THC1 port
>   - Note: The BKM below are captured from NVL RVP
>
>
> Test Steps / Procedure: 
> Ubuntu OS
>
>   - RVP successfully booted to UEFI shell with all the HW requirements met with all the BIOS configuration done accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test Configuration' section above.
>   - Boot the system to Ubuntu OS.
>   - Enter "lspci" command via PuTTY to print out a complete list of devices enabled by BIOS.
>   - Look for "Digitizer Pen" device name from the print-out PCI device list. Using NVL as example for this instance:
> THC0 port -> Bus:0, Device: 16 (0x10h), Function: 0, Device ID for HID-SPI -> d349
> THC1 port -> Bus:0, Device: 8 (0x08h), Function: 0, Device ID for HID-SPI -> d34b
>   - Then, enter "lspci -kvvv -s 00:10.0" command to check for device bus device function details. [Note: Enter desire BDF in highlighted part]
>   - To check for BDF and Vendor/Device ID, it can be checked via PuTTY with following command: "lspci -nn -s 00:08.0" [Note: Enter desire BDF in highlighted part]
>   - Alternatively, both BDF, and Vendor/Device ID can be checked via register namednodes in PythonSV (as shown below). 
>
> Windows OS (Non-POR)
>
>   - RVP successfully booted to UEFI shell with all the HW requirements met with all the BIOS configuration done accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test Configuration' section above.
>   - Enter "pci" command to print out a complete list of devices enabled by BIOS.
>   - Look for "Input Devices - Digitizer (pen)" device name from the print-out PCI device list. Using PTL as example for this instance:
> THC0 port -> Bus:0, Device: 16 (0x10h), Function: 0, Device ID for HID-I2C -> E348 or E448, HID-SPI -> E349 or E449
> THC1 port -> Bus:0, Device: 16 (0x10h), Function: 0, Device ID for HID-I2C -> E34A or E44A, HID-SPI -> E34B or E44B
> For reference, below screenshot is taken from MTL-P RVP with a different Device ID.
>
>   - Finally, boot the system to Windows OS. Go to Device Manager, and click on "Intel QuickSPI/I2C Host Controller" properties to check for Vendor/Device ID with its' respective PCI BDF mapped location. 
> For reference, below screenshot is taken from MTL-P RVP with a different Device ID.
>
>   - Alternatively, both BDF, and Vendor/Device ID can be checked via register namednodes in PythonSV (as shown below). 
>
>
> SW Requirements:
>
>   - Install SW HID-SPI/-I2C Driver on Windows OS. (i.e. QuickSPI or QuickI2C driver)
> HW Requirements:
>
>   - RVP booted to UEFI/WinOS with feature rework to enable I2C/SPI interface for both THC0 and/or THC1 ports.
>   - 3PE POR touch panel  
> Dependencies:
>
>   - BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements / config.
> Test Pass Criteria :
>
>   - Both UEFI Shell and Windows' Device Manager able to reflect the valid Vendor/Device ID values and mapped to respective BDF for both THC0 and THC1 ports, as required according to HAS.
>   - In PythonSV, register namednodes verification able to reflect the correct values as well. 
> Test Fail Criteria :
>
>   - Unable to meet the passing criteria above, i.e. incorrect/invalid BDF, Vendor or Device ID. 
> Intent Verify:
>
>   - Validate the BDF, Vendor and Device ID match the expected values from THC design specification.
>   - Ensure THC hardware is detected with correct identifiers during system boot and after device initialization in OS.
> Ecosystem SW Requirements:
> Below is the example from NVL-Hx, please refer to Wiki for different project: THC Device Enablement on NVL-HX RVP1/RVP4 - Validation and Debug Encyclopedia - Intel Enterprise Wiki
>
>   - BIOS version:
>   - ifwi_nvl_hx_a1a0_pp_release_2025ww50.1.01/ or later version
>
>   - OS kernel:
>   - Window OS
>
> NVL-HX-CONS-25.48.2.287 or later version (NOTE: NON-POR, does not supported by driver team, intended solely for debugging/validation purposes)
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> \\amr.corp.intel.com\ec\proj\sv\Debug\PowerOn\NVL_Hx\NVL_Hx_A1\OS_Image\Ubuntu\ubuntu-24.04.3-desktop-amd64.iso
>
>   - Driver versions:
>   - Windows OS
>
>  https://ubit-artifactory-or.intel.com/artifactory/one-windows-local/Ingredients/hidi2c_touch/ OR https://ubit-artifactory-ba.intel.com/artifactory/owr-repos/Submissions/quickspi/
>
>   - Ubuntu OSBuilt in QuickSPI kernel driver

> THC1 port -> Bus:0, Device: 16 (0x10h), Function: 0, Device ID for HID-I2C -> E34A or E44A, HID-SPI -> E34B or E44B

### ✅ [TC] THC1 D0i2 Entry/Exit Flow (D0-->D0i2-->D0) and check PG/CGs (Allow SUT/RVP to enter S0i2.0)

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458258 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test Description:
>
>   - The objective/intent of this test is to assess and verify THC1 meets the platform power management requirements and aligns with architecture specifications for low-power states.
>   - D0i2 entry can help to verify that the controller properly transitions when touch panel is idle to save power.
>   - When touch event happens, the D0i2 exit helps to ensure that system wakes up without latency or missed gestures.
>
> Pre-Test Configuration:
>
>   - THC0 port will required to be enabled in BIOS settings (Note: It can be with I2C interface or SPI interface)
>   - RVP booted to BIOS Menu, go to Intel Advance Menu -> PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration ->   <Refer to the BKM settings below to enable THC1 port.>
>   - Note: The BKM below are captured from NVL RVP
>
>
> Test Steps / Procedure:
>
>   - RVP booted to BIOS Setup Menu, follow and configure all the BIOS settings accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test Configuration' section above.
>   - For MTL-S/MTP-S VISA tracing on ITH tool , user will need to enable the Platform Debug Consent to <Enabled - Trace Active> in BIOS settings. (i.e. Intel Advance Menu --> Debug Settings)
>
>   - Make sure to save the new changes before exiting BIOS Setup Menu, then allow the SUT to reboot itself for these changes to take effect.
>   - Verify touch panel is able to touch and leave the touch panel idle (i.e. no touch activity) for a few seconds to enter D0i2 state.
>   - Modify the D0i2 entry timer register value in PythonSV to have better visual and capture on D0i2 state transition between entry and exit (e.g. pch.thc1.pvt.thc_sb_pm_ctrl.d0i2_entry_timer = 0x9     ## .getspec() for more information on the selection of timer configuration available)
>
>   - To trigger D0i2 exit, simply touch the panel, observe the ITH tool for state transition.
>
>
> SW Requirements:
>
>   - ITH tool to check the D0i2 entry and exit state transition. Press the "Update All Signal State" or  "Get Signal State", user can see from "L" to "H" or vice-versa between D0i2 entry and exit transition.
>
> HW Requirements:
>
>   - RVP booted to UEFI Shell with feature rework to enable I2C interface on THC1 port.
>   - 3PE POR touch panel  (i.e. BOM36A, BOM37A, BOM52) with touch cable connected on RVP via the THC1 touch port/connector.
>   - PythonSV with USB2DBC debug cable connected from SUT/RVP to host system.
> (Note: Please check RVP schematic/layout to confirm the THC0 port naming label and location)
>
> Dependencies:
>
>   - BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements / config.
>
> Test Pass Criteria:
>
>   - For D0i2 Entry, both PG_REQ_b and PG_ACK_b signals will be asserted to LOW state; whereas, both FET_EN_b and FET_EN_ACK_b will be de-asserted to HIGH state.
>   - For D0i2 Exit, both PG_REQ_b and PG_ACK_b signals will be de-asserted to HIGH state; whereas, both FET_EN_b and FET_EN_ACK_b will be asserted to LOW state.
>
> Test Fail Criteria:
>
>   - Unable to meet any of the passing criteria above
>
> Intent Verify:
>
>   - Validate that the THC IP controller transitions correctly between D0i2 entry and exit states
>   - Use ITH tool to monitor the signal changes during idle-triggered entry and touch-triggered exit
>   - Verify that the observed transitions align with expected behaviour define in the architecture specification
>   - According to https://docs.intel.com/documents/pch_uarch_doc/MTL/MAS/THC_D0_S0ix.html , the figure below shows the complete flow for the D0i2 Entry requirement.
>
> THC D0i2/S0ix Entry Sequence
>
>
> THC D0i2/S0ix Exit Sequence
>
>
>
>   - PCR:   https://hsdes.intel.com/appstore/article/#/1409894130
>   - Summary:  From MTL+ onwards, allow S0ix with THC in D0i2, i.e. system S0i2.0 substate entry is allowed once THC entered D0i2.
>
>
> Ecosystem SW Requirements:
> Below is the example from NVL-Hx, please refer to Wiki for different project: THC Device Enablement on NVL-HX RVP1/RVP4 - Validation and Debug Encyclopedia - Intel Enterprise Wiki
>
>   - BIOS version:
>   - ifwi_nvl_hx_a1a0_pp_release_2025ww50.1.01/ or later version
>
>   - OS kernel:
>   - Window OS
>
> NVL-HX-CONS-25.48.2.287 or later version (NOTE: NON-POR, does not supported by driver team, intended solely for debugging/validation purposes)
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> \\amr.corp.intel.com\ec\proj\sv\Debug\PowerOn\NVL_Hx\NVL_Hx_A1\OS_Image\Ubuntu\ubuntu-24.04.3-desktop-amd64.iso
>
>   - Driver versions:
>   - Windows OS
>
>  https://ubit-artifactory-or.intel.com/artifactory/one-windows-local/Ingredients/hidi2c_touch/ OR https://ubit-artifactory-ba.intel.com/artifactory/owr-repos/Submissions/quickspi/
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> Built in QuickSPI kernel driver

### ✅ [TC] THC1 D3 Entry/Exit Flow (D0-->D3-->D0) and check PG/CGs  (Allow SUT/RVP S0i2.1/S0i2.2)

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458260 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test Description:
>
>   - The objective/intent of this test is to ensure both THC0 and THC1 can perform D3 entry and exit.
>   - This will be carried out using touch panel specifically on the platform, connected via the THC1 port.
>   - Expectation is that the SW driver will have the capability to perform D3 entry/exit on both THC ports when system entering/exiting S0ix.
>   - If THC entered and maintained at D3 power state, SoCWatch should capture D3 residency increases over time.   
>
> Pre-Test Configuration:
>
>   - THC1 port will required to be enabled in BIOS settings.
>   - RVP booted to BIOS Menu, go to Intel Advance Menu -> PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration ->   <Refer to the BKM settings below to enable THC1 port.>
>   - Note: The BKM below are captured from NVL RVP
>
>
> Test Steps / Procedure: 
>
>   - RVP booted to BIOS Setup Menu, follow and configure all the BIOS settings accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test Configuration' section above.
>   - Use SoCWatch tool to capture the D3 residency on THC enabled port(s).
>   - On RVP/SUT, press the PWRBTN for system to enter CS state. (Note: expect the postcode transition from:0000 -> 00C5 -> 01C5, in "01C5" system fully entered CS/S0ix state)
>   - In PythonSV, perform read to both THC's "pwrst" and PMC's "thc1_d3_sts" register bits, should obtained "pwrst = 0x3" and "thc1_d3_sts = 0x1" which indicates THC1 has entered D3 power state.
>   - Leave the system to be idle for at least 3 minutes.
>   - Press PWRBTN to wake the system from CS state. (Note: expect the postcode transition from: 01C5 -> ABC5, in "ABC5" system wake from CS/S0ix state)
>   - In PythonSV, perform read to both THC's "pwrst" and PMC's "thc1_d3_sts" register bits again, should obtained "pwrst = 0x0" and "thc1_d3_sts = 0x0" which indicates THC1 has entered D0 power state.
>   - Stop the SoCWatch capture and analyze the log, expect to get high D3 residency on THC1.
> SW Requirements :
>
>   - SoCWatch Tool
> HW Requirements :
>
>   - RVP with feature rework to enable I2C/SPI interface on THC1 port.
>   - 3PE POR touch panel  (i.e. BOM36A, BOM37A, BOM52) with touch cable connected on RVP via the THC1 touch port/connector.
> (Note: Please check RVP schematic/layout to confirm the THC1 port naming label and location) 
> Dependencies :
>
>   - BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements / config.
>
> Test Pass Criteria :
>
>   - Able to perform D3 entry and exit on THC1 port as expected.
>   - When perform SoCWatch capture for at least 3 minutes (or more), a high D3 residency of at least >70% on THC1 port is expected.
>
> Test Fail Criteria :
>
>   - Unable to meet the passing criteria above.
> Intent Verify:
>
>   - Validate that the THC IP controller transitions correctly between D3 entry and exit
>   - Use SoCWatch tool to monitor the D3 residency on THC port
>   - Verify that the observed transitions align with expected behaviour
>   - For reference,
>
> Ecosystem SW Requirements:
> Below is the example from NVL-Hx, please refer to Wiki for different project: THC Device Enablement on NVL-HX RVP1/RVP4 - Validation and Debug Encyclopedia - Intel Enterprise Wiki
>
>   - BIOS version:
>   - ifwi_nvl_hx_a1a0_pp_release_2025ww50.1.01/ or later version
>
>   - OS kernel:
>   - Window OS
>
> NVL-HX-CONS-25.48.2.287 or later version (NOTE: NON-POR, does not supported by driver team, intended solely for debugging/validation purposes)
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> \\amr.corp.intel.com\ec\proj\sv\Debug\PowerOn\NVL_Hx\NVL_Hx_A1\OS_Image\Ubuntu\ubuntu-24.04.3-desktop-amd64.iso
>
>   - Driver versions:
>   - Windows OS
>
>  https://ubit-artifactory-or.intel.com/artifactory/one-windows-local/Ingredients/hidi2c_touch/ OR https://ubit-artifactory-ba.intel.com/artifactory/owr-repos/Submissions/quickspi/
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> Built in QuickSPI kernel driver

### ✅ [TC] THC1 Fuse/Softstrap Checkout  (NVL - New Strap)

**Description:**

> Test Description:
>
>   - The objective/intent of this test is to ensure that the THC1's Fuses/Softstraps configuration aligns with expectations according to both HAS and Harness requirements.
>   - This test can be carried out in early boot phase during PO once SOC/PCD registers access available post 'socn_bootstall' breakpoint, up to the point before BIOS initialization starts.
>   - Fuse list from THC SIP HAS:  
>
>
>
>   - [PTL] Harness 2.0 showing a complete list of softstraps available/supported for THC0 and THC1. (Link: Harness 2.0)
