>
> SW/HW Requirements:
>
>   - RVP successfully booted to UEFI shell or WinOS.
>   - PythonSV with USB2DBC debug cable connected from SUT/RVP to host system.
> Dependencies:
>
>   - N/A
> Test Pass Criteria:
>
>   - Able to perform register read/write access on all THC1 registers in PythonSV, via both IOSF-SB and IOSF-Primary access methods (shown in table above).
>   - No missing register in PythonSV vs CRIF.
>   - Read out expected default value in PythonSV for all the THC1 related registers as specified in the CRIF. (with the exceptional of certain error/status register - if any)
> Test Fail Criteria:
>
>   - Unable to meet any of the passing criteria above.
>   - Identified one or more missing THC1 register in PythonSV. 
>   - Failed to access one or more THC1 register or field bit via PythonSV.
>   - Unexpected default value of any THC1 register identified in PythonSV.
> Intent Verify:
>
>   - Cross-check each THC1 registers' value between each access method via PythonSV.
>   - Verify the value for all THC related registers are as expected and there are no missing register/mismatch in value/failed register write.
> Ecosystem SW Requirements:
> Below is the example from NVL-Hx, please refer to Wiki for different project: THC Device Enablement on NVL-HX RVP1/RVP4 - Validation and Debug Encyclopedia - Intel Enterprise Wiki
>
>   - BIOS version:
>   - ifwi_nvl_hx_a1a0_pp_release_2025ww50.1.01/ or later version
>
>   - OS kernel:
>   - Window OS
>
> NVL-HX-CONS-25.48.2.287 or later version (NOTE: NON-POR, does not supported by driver team, intended solely for debugging/validation purposes)
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> \\amr.corp.intel.com\ec\proj\sv\Debug\PowerOn\NVL_Hx\NVL_Hx_A1\OS_Image\Ubuntu\ubuntu-24.04.3-desktop-amd64.iso
>
>   - Driver versions:
>   - Windows OS
>
>  https://ubit-artifactory-or.intel.com/artifactory/one-windows-local/Ingredients/hidi2c_touch/ OR https://ubit-artifactory-ba.intel.com/artifactory/owr-repos/Submissions/quickspi/
>
>   - Ubuntu OSBuilt in QuickSPI kernel driver

---

### ✅ [TC] THC1 SW GPIO Interrupt driven from THC MMIO register (SWGPIO_INT) to vGPIO_THC3 pin

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458287 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test Description :
>
>   - The goal of this test is to evaluate and confirm that the THC1's SW-GPIO interrupt register bit can effectively assert and de-assert the vGPIO signal level (i.e. THC1 port = vgpio_thc3), ensuring its accurate reflection on the GPIO-IP side via register verification on vgpio_thc3.
>   - With a stretch goal, validate the SW interrupt flow, specifically by triggering and sending Tier-1 SCI/GPE interrupt via vgpio_thc3 to PMC side. 
>   - This test can be carried out with RVP on either UEFI shell or Ubuntu/WinOS environment. 
>   - This test is also designed to have coverage for the PCR below:
> HSD Link
>
>  | 
>
> PCR Title
>
>  | 
>
> Comments
>
>  | 
> 22010402304 
>
>  | 
> THC (aka hSPI) IP driven virtual GPIO for HIDclass operation
>
>  | 
> This BIOS changes can be implicitly covered during the THC-SPI GPIO Register Configuration checkout.
>
>  | 
>
>
>
>
> THC SW-GPIO Interrupt (via thc_m_prt_control register)
> Bit
>  | 
> Attribute
>  | 
> Default
>  | 
> Description
>  | 
> 4
>  | 
> RW
>  | 
> 1'b0
>  | 
> SW GPIO Interrupt (SWGPIO_INT)
> 1: Assert SW GPIO interrupt through vGPIO.
> 0: De-assert SW GPIO interrupt through vGPIO.
>  | 
>
>
> Pre-Test Configuration :
>
>   - THC1 port will required to be enabled in BIOS settings.
>   - RVP booted to BIOS Menu, go to Intel Advance Menu -> PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration ->   <Refer to the BKM settings below to enable THC1 port.>
>   - To unlock all GPIO pads for SW configuration purpose, go to Intel Advance Menu -> PCH-IO Configuration -> Security Configuration -> Force unlock on all GPIO pads -> Enabled
>   - Note: The BKM below are captured from NVL RVP
>
>
>
> Test Steps / Procedure: 
> 1) RVP successfully booted to UEFI Shell with all the HW requirements met with all the BIOS configuration done accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test Configuration' section above.
> 2) In PythonSV, follow the steps below. E.g. using MTL-P682 RVP as example:
>       (i) Perform itp.halt(), and then make sure to check for all cores are in halted status. 
>
>        (ii) Encode and configure all GPE*_DW* registers on both GPIO and PMC ends.
> GPIO-IP
>  | 
> PMC-IP
>  | 
>
>
>
>  | 
>
>
>  | 
>
> 3) Enable SCI Interrupt on vgpio_thc3 pad, when all of the following registers are configured accordingly:
>      (a) Pad under host ownership
>           soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.pad_own_vgpio_3_1.pad_own_vgpio_thc3 = 0 
>
>      (b) GPIO input mode
>           soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.pad_cfg_dw0_vgpio_thc3.gpiorxdis = 0 
>           soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.pad_cfg_dw0_vgpio_thc3.gpiotxdis = 1 
>
>      (c) Host Software Pad Owner (HOSTSW_OWN) is set to 0x0 (ACPI mode)
>             soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.hostsw_own_vgpio_3_0.hostsw_own_vgpio_thc3 = 0 
>
>      (d) The corresponding GPI GPE enable bit is set to 0x1
>             soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.gpi_gpe_en_vgpio_3_0.gpi_gpe_en_vgpio_thc3 = 1 
>
>      (e) GPIO Input Route SCI (GPIRoutSCI) is set to 0x1
>            soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.pad_cfg_dw0_vgpio_thc3.gpiroutsci = 1 
>
>
>      (f)  GPE GPI/SCI interrupt is a level based interrupt: 
>             soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.pad_cfg_dw0_vgpio_thc3.rxevcfg = 0 
>
>      (g) RxINV bit set to 0 (No inversion - interrupt trigger at active-high signal level): 
>             soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.pad_cfg_dw0_vgpio_thc3.rxinv = 0 
>
>      (h) Enable IOSF-SB message initiation of GPIO interrupt event: 
>
>
> 4) Find and read the current value of PCI BAR (bar1/ba_hi << 32 + bar0/ba_low) for THC1 port: 
> In :  soc.south.psf.showsearch('thc1')
> psf8.psf_8_agnt_t0_shdw_bar0_thc1_rs0_d16_f1_offset30 = 0x1ca10000
> psf8.psf_8_agnt_t0_shdw_bar1_thc1_rs0_d16_f1_offset30 = 0x60
> psf8.psf_8_agnt_t0_shdw_pcien_thc1_rs0_d16_f1_offset30 = 0x2
> psf8.psf_8_agnt_t0_shdw_pmcsr_thc1_rs0_d16_f1_offset30 = 0x0
> psf8.psf_8_agnt_t0_shdw_cfg_dis_thc1_rs0_d16_f1_offset30 = 0x0
> psf8.psf_8_t0_agent_function_config_thc1_rs0_d16_f1_offset30 = 0x102
> psf8.psf_8_agent_tgt_rootspace_thc1_rs0_d16_f1_offset30 = 0x0
>
> pcibar_hi = soc.south.psf.psf8.psf_8_agnt_t0_shdw_bar1_thc1_rs0_d16_f1_offset30.read()
> pcibar_low = soc.south.psf.psf8.psf_8_agnt_t0_shdw_bar0_thc1_rs0_d16_f1_offset30.read()
>
> pcibar = (pcibar_hi << 32) + pcibar_low
>
> In : pcibar 
> 0x601CA10000
>
> 5) Find and read the address offset for THC_M_PRT_CONTROL register, and the field-bit position of SWGPIO_INT.
>    Then, trigger SCI interrupt by writing to the same register as follows: 
> reg_addr = soc.south.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_control.get_address_map_info()['u_thc_mmio_port']['offset']
> swgpio_bit = soc.south.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_control.swgpio_int.lowerbit
>
> # Read THC_M_PRT_CONTROL register using IPC/ITP memory read method vs Namednodes read (via 'tap2sb') 
> # Expect both read methods to have the same values
> itp.threads[0].mem('0x%xp' % (pcibar + reg_addr), 0x4)
> soc.south.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_control
>
> read_thc1_prt_ctrl = int(itp.threads[0].mem('0x%xp' % (pcibar + reg_addr),0x4))
>
> # Write 1 to SWGPIO_INT[4] bit in THC_M_PRT_CONTROL register. 
> itp.threads[0].mem('0x%xp' % (pcibar + reg_addr), 0x4, (read_thc1_prt_ctrl + (1 << swgpio_bit)))
>
> # Read back THC_M_PRT_CONTROL register and the namednodes 
>
> itp.threads[0].mem('0x%xp' % (pcibar + reg_addr), 0x4)
> soc.south.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_control.read()
> soc.south.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_control.swgpio_int.read()
>
> 6) Check and read the status PAD_CFG_DW0_VGPIO_THC3 register, i.e. GPIORxState will change from 0  to 1  (After SWGPIO_INT set to 1).
> In :  soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.pad_cfg_dw0_vgpio_thc3
> 0x40080102
>
> soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.pad_cfg_dw0_vgpio_thc3.show()
> In :  soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.pad_cfg_dw0_vgpio_thc3.show
> 0x00000001 : padrstcfg (31:30) -- \n                    [p]This register controls which reset is used to reset the specified Pad Register fiel...
> 0x00000000 : rxpadstsel (29:29) -- \n                    [p]Determine from which node the RX pad state for native function should be source fr...
> 0x00000000 : rxraw1 (28:28) -- \n                    [p]This bit determines if the selected pad state is being overridden to '1'.  This field ...

> 0x00000000 : pregfrxsel (24:24) -- \n                    [p]Determine if the synchronized version of the raw RX pad state should be subjected ...
> 0x00000000 : rxinv (23:23) -- \n                    [p]This bit determines if the selected pad state should go through the polarity inversion ...

> 0x00000001 : gpiroutsci (19:19) -- \n                    [p]Determine if the pad can be routed to cause SCI when configured in GPIO input mode...
> 0x00000000 : gpiroutsmi (18:18) -- \n                    [p]Determine if the pad can be routed to cause SMI when configured in GPIO input mode...
> 0x00000000 : gpiroutnmi (17:17) -- \n                    [p]Determine if the pad can be routed to cause NMI when configured in GPIO input mode...

> 0x00000000 : gpiorxdis (09:09) -- \n                   [p] RX buffer enable control when PMode = 0 ONLY.  No effect when the pad in native mod...
> 0x00000001 : gpiotxdis (08:08) -- \n                    [p] TX buffer enable control when PMode = 0 ONLY.  No effect when the pad in native mo...
> 0x00000000 : rsvd_1 (07:02) -- Reserved
> 0x00000001 : gpiorxstate (01:01) -- \n                    [p]This is the current internal RX pad state after Glitch Filter logic stage and is ...
> 0x00000000 : gpiotxstate (00:00) -- \n                    [p] TX state in when PMode = 0 ONLY.  No effect when the pad in native mode.[/p]\n  ...
>
> 7) As a result, SCI/GPE interrupt will be generated/asserted from GPIO to PMC, which can be verified by checking the following STATUS bits: 
>    (a) GPIO: GPI_GPE_STS_VGPIO_THC3 = 0x1
>    (b) PMC: GPE0_EN/STS_x_x = 0x1000
> # GPIO : 
> In :  soc.south.gpio.showsearch('vgpio_thc3', 'f')
> com3.sb.pad_own_vgpio_3_1.pad_own_vgpio_thc3 = 0x0
> com3.sb.gpi_vwe_vgpio_3_0.gpi_vwe_vgpio_thc3 = 0x0
> com3.sb.padcfglock_vgpio_3_0.padcfglock_vgpio_thc3 = 0x0
> com3.sb.padcfglocktx_vgpio_3_0.padcfglocktx_vgpio_thc3 = 0x0
> com3.sb.hostsw_own_vgpio_3_0.hostsw_own_vgpio_thc3 = 0x0
> com3.sb.gpi_is_vgpio_3_0.gpi_int_sts_vgpio_thc3 = 0x0
> com3.sb.gpi_ie_vgpio_3_0.gpi_int_en_vgpio_thc3 = 0x0
> com3.sb.gpi_gpe_sts_vgpio_3_0.gpi_gpe_sts_vgpio_thc3 = 0x1     # GPIO-SCI Interrupt asserted
> com3.sb.gpi_gpe_en_vgpio_3_0.gpi_gpe_en_vgpio_thc3 = 0x1
> com3.sb.gpi_smi_sts_vgpio_3_0.gpi_smi_sts_vgpio_thc3 = 0x0
> com3.sb.gpi_smi_en_vgpio_3_0.gpi_smi_en_vgpio_thc3 = 0x0

> com3.sb.gpi_nmi_en_vgpio_3_0.gpi_nmi_en_vgpio_thc3 = 0x0
>
> soc.south.pmc.io.show()
> In :  soc.south.pmc.io.show

> 0x0000000000002000 : gpe0_sts_63_32           # SCI/GPE [vGPIO_THC3 @ bit:13] status asserted 
> 0x0000000000002000 : gpe0_sts_95_64           # SCI/GPE [vGPIO_THC3 @ bit:13] status asserted 
> 0x0000000000006000 : gpe0_sts_127_96

>
> 8) Remove/De-assert SCI interrupt by configuring the following registers: 
>
> # clearing SCI interrupt from vgpio_thc3
>
> soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.gpi_gpe_en_vgpio_3_0.gpi_gpe_en_vgpio_thc3 = 0         
> soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.pad_cfg_dw0_vgpio_thc3.rxevcfg = 2                             
> soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.pad_cfg_dw0_vgpio_thc3.gpiroutsci = 0
>
> # Revert back THC_M_PRT_CONTROL register to previous settings. Then, read back the register.
> itp.threads[0].mem('0x%xp' % (pcibar + reg_addr), 0x4, (read_thc1_prt_ctrl ))
> itp.threads[0].mem('0x%xp' % (pcibar + reg_addr), 0x4)
>
> soc.south.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_control.read()
> soc.south.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_control.swgpio_int.read()
> soc.south.gpio.com3.sb.gpi_gpe_sts_vgpio_3_0.gpi_gpe_sts_vgpio_thc3 = 1     # Write 1 to clear interrupt status bit
>

>    (a) GPIO: GPIORxState = 0, GPI_GPE_STS_VGPIO_THC3 = 0
>    (b) PMC: GPE0_EN/STS_x_x = 0

> com3.sb.gpi_smi_en_vgpio_3_0.gpi_smi_en_vgpio_thc3 = 0x0
> com3.sb.gpi_nmi_sts_vgpio_3_0.gpi_nmi_sts_vgpio_thc3 = 0x0
> com3.sb.gpi_nmi_en_vgpio_3_0.gpi_nmi_en_vgpio_thc3 = 0x0
>
> soc.south.pmc.io.show()
> In :  soc.south.pmc.io.show
> 0x0000000000000000 : devtrap_sts
> 0x0000000000000000 : gpe0_en_31_0

>
> 10) Perform itp.go() to un-halt the cores to resume the system.
>
> SW Requirements:
>
>   - N/A
> HW Requirements:
>
>   - RVP booted to UEFI Shell with feature rework to enable SPI interface on THC1 port.
>   - 3PE POR touch panel (i.e. BOM52, BOM36A/37A) with touch cable connected on RVP via the THC1 touch port/connector.
>   - PythonSV with USB2DBC debug cable connected from SUT/RVP to host system.
>   - (Optional): Acute logic analyzer to monitor the HID-SPI traffic for debug/analysis purposes.   
> (Note: Please check RVP schematic/layout to confirm the THC1 port naming label and location) 
> Dependencies:
>
>   - BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements / config.
> Test Pass Criteria:
>
>   - THC1's SW-GPIO interrupt register bit can effectively assert and de-assert the vGPIO signal level (i.e. THC1 port = vgpio_thc3), ensuring it is reflected on GPIO via register verification on vgpio_thc3. (i.e. thc_m_prt_control.swgpio_int  =  pad_cfg_dw0_vgpio_thc3.gpiorxstate )
>   - [Stretch-Goal]:  The assertion of THC1's SW-GPIO interrupt able to trigger and generate Tier-1 SCI/GPE interrupt via vgpio_thc3 to PMC.
>   - On contrary, Tier-1 SCI/GPE interrupt can be cleared/de-asserted once THC1's SW-GPIO interrupt is de-asserted and the interrupt removed on vGPIO_THC3. 
> Test Fail Criteria:
>
>   - Unable to meet any of the passing criteria above.  
> Intent Verify:
>
>   - Verify that the system correctly reflects interrupt status changes at each stage, including GPIO and PMC status indicators.
>   - Ensure the touch controller interface and related system components respond as expected to interrupt events, with no unexpected error/loss of functionality.
>   - Confirm the SCI interrupt for THC can be successfully configured, asserted and de-asserted throught both software and hardware mechanisms.
>
> Ecosystem SW Requirements:
> Below is the example from NVL-Hx, please refer to Wiki for different project: THC Device Enablement on NVL-HX RVP1/RVP4 - Validation and Debug Encyclopedia - Intel Enterprise Wiki
>
>   - BIOS version:
>   - ifwi_nvl_hx_a1a0_pp_release_2025ww50.1.01/ or later version
>
>   - OS kernel:
>   - Window OS
>
> NVL-HX-CONS-25.48.2.287 or later version (NOTE: NON-POR, does not supported by driver team, intended solely for debugging/validation purposes)
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> \\amr.corp.intel.com\ec\proj\sv\Debug\PowerOn\NVL_Hx\NVL_Hx_A1\OS_Image\Ubuntu\ubuntu-24.04.3-desktop-amd64.iso
>
>   - Driver versions:
>   - Windows OS
>
>  https://ubit-artifactory-or.intel.com/artifactory/one-windows-local/Ingredients/hidi2c_touch/ OR https://ubit-artifactory-ba.intel.com/artifactory/owr-repos/Submissions/quickspi/
>
>   - Ubuntu OSBuilt in QuickSPI kernel driver

### ❌ [TC] THC1 Timestamping on Touch Input Report from RxDMA (when THC entering D0i2/D3)

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458400 |
| **Status** | rejected |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test Description :
>
>   - The objective/intent of this test is to verify that Timestamping feature on THC1 port is enabled and functional as expected as stated in the test steps and TCD.
>
> Pre-Test Configuration :
>
>   - BKM for enabling BOM36A Touch Panel (QuickSPI/HIDSPI), or
>   - BKM for enabling BOM37A Touch Panel (QuickI2C/HIDI2C)
> SW Requirements :
>
>   - Install SW HID-SPI/I2C Driver on Windows OS. (i.e. QuickSPI/QuickI2C driver for THC1 port) 
>   - (Optional) For debugging, users can utilize the TraceView tool to capture .ETL log files, enabling detailed analysis of HID-SPI/I2C traffic/data payloads, interrupt rates/frequencies, and error/failure logging.
>
> HW Requirements :
>
>   - PTL RVP booted to WinOS with feature rework to enable SPI/I2C interface on THC1 port.   
>   - 3PE POR touch panel  (i.e. BOM36/37) with touch cable connected on PTL RVP via the THC1 touch port/connector.
>   - Acute logic analyzer to monitor the HID-SPI/I2C traffic for debug/analysis purposes.
>   - PythonSV with USB2DBC debug cable connected from SUT/RVP to host system.   
>   - Optional: Custom rework required to wire-out all the THC-SPI/I2C IO pins for Acute LA signals tracing. (Note: Make sure to use single-core wires and keep the length as short as possible to avoid/minimize any signal integrity impact.)
> (Note: Please check PTL RVP schematic/layout to confirm the THC1 port naming label and location)    
> Dependencies:
>
>   - BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements / config.  
>
> Test Steps / Procedure : 
> 1) PTL RVP successfully booted to WinOS with all the HW/SW requirements met with all the BIOS configuration done accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test Configuration' section above.
>
> 2) In PythonSV, enable timestamping feature on THC1 port by programming the following registers, and read back the registers to verify the configured values: 
>      soc.thc1.pvt.thc_sb_pm_ctrl.thc_ts_d0i2_mode = 0         # TS timer paused during D0i2 entry
>      soc.thc1.pvt.thc_sb_pm_ctrl.thc_microsec_cnt= 0x19     # Using SoC PGCB_CLK frequency for THC generate 10 us tick internally
>      soc.thc1.pvt.thc_sb_pm_ctrl.thc_timestamp_src= 0         # 0 - External PMC clocks tick as reference , 1 - Internal counter on core CLK 
>      soc.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_timestamp_2.thc_timestamp_en = 1  # Enable timestamp feature
>
> 3) Generate RxDMA input touch report by performing touch gestures onto the touch device, and then obtain the current timestamp value by reading the following register namednode in PythonSV. 
>      Poll reading the register for continuous dumping the timestamp value in PythonSV for ease of analysis/verification purpose. 
>       while(1):  print(soc.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_timestamp_2.thc_timestamp.read());     # Poll read timestamp value 
>
> 4) Ensure that the returned value of timestamp for each and every input report keeps incrementing from previous returned value (in every 10 us tick), 
>      whether or not THC1 port entered D0i2 idle state, as timer is paused and will NOT reset back to 0 (when THC_TS_D0I2_MODE set to 0) during D0i2 entry. 
>      (Note: After every D0i2 exit, the timer starts incrementing immediately out of D0i2 without waiting for the next input report frame.)
>
> 5) Wait for a couple of seconds (depending on the configured D0I2_ENTRY_TIMER register value).
>     When THC1 port entered D0i2 idle state (i.e. No activity on THC-SPI/I2C interface signal lines), the timestamp's timer will paused and the THC_TIMESTAMP register will not reset back to a value of 0.
>     Read to THC_TIMESTAMP register will returned the previous timestamp value from the last input report 
>      soc.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_timestamp_2.thc_timestamp.read()       # Read current THC timestamp 
>
> 6) Place the THC1 port into D3 power state by using the following PythonSV command,  or by disabling the THC1 device in WinOS Device Manager.
>     Read to THC_TIMESTAMP register will returned a value of 0, as the timestamp's timer will always reset to 0 during D3 entry.
>      soc.thc1.cfg.thc_cfg_pmd_pmcsrbse_pmcsr.pwrst = 0x3        # 0 - D0 state, 3 - D3 state 
>
> 7) (a) Enable timestamp timer mode (i.e. THC_TS_D0I2_MODE=1):  
>            soc.thc1.pvt.thc_sb_pm_ctrl.thc_ts_d0i2_mode = 1
>     (b) Configure the D0i2 entry timeout value to max value (i.e. 0b1001 / 0x9h = 10 seconds).
>            soc.thc1.pvt.thc_sb_pm_ctrl.d0i2_entry_timer = 0x9
>
>     (c) Poll reading to THC_TIMESTAMP register in PythonSV ; the returned value will keep incrementing in every 10 us tick as expected. 
>            while(1):  print(soc.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_timestamp_2.thc_timestamp.read());     # Poll read timestamp value 
>
>     (d) And, then perform the following tests for two different scenarios: 
> 		Scenario A: D0I2_ENTRY_TIMER timeout occurred between each input report
>                  Expected outcome: The timestamp's timer will reset to 0 during D0i2 entry. Read to THC_TIMESTAMP register will returned a value of 0. 
>                  After every D0i2 exit, the timer starts incrementing when the first input report frame is received, i.e. the timestamp for the first frame out of D0i2 is always starts from 0. 
>
> 		Scenario B: Continuous Input Touch Report before D0I2_ENTRY_TIMER timeout occurred
> 		Perform tap-and-hold gesture onto the touch device to generate input touch reports back-to-back so that THC1 port will always remained in active state. 
>                 Expected outcome: Poll reading to THC_TIMESTAMP register; the returned value will keep incrementing in every 10 us tick as expected. 
>
> 8) Stop the register poll reading in PythonSV.  
>
> 9) Repeat same steps #3 to #8 with a different timestamp clock reference/source (i.e. THC_TIMESTAMP_SRC = 1) 
>      soc.thc1.pvt.thc_sb_pm_ctrl.thc_timestamp_src= 1         #  1 - Internal counter on core CLK 
>
> Additional Note: 
>
>   - THC has option to use either an external microsecond tick reference from SoC(PMC), or an internal core clock counter to generate the 10 microsecond pulse based on THC_TIMESTAMP_SRC register bit configuration.
>   - Timestamping is not supported on SWDMA, TXDMA or PIO.
>
> Test Pass Criteria :
>
>   - Timestamping feature on THC1 port is working as expected as stated in this TC's test steps above. 
> Test Fail Criteria :
>
>   - Unable to meet any of the passing criteria above.

---

### ❌ [TC] THC1 Touch Panel Display Sync (DSYNC) Signal to THC via TCON GPIO

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458405 |
| **Status** | rejected |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test Description :
>
>   - The objective/intent of this test is to verify and ensure that TCON Display SYNC (DSYNC) GPIO function working as expected on THC1 port when touching the BOM36A/37A display screen. 
>
> Pre-Test Configuration :
>
>   - BKM for enabling BOM36A Touch Panel (QuickSPI/HIDSPI), or
>   - BKM for enabling BOM37A Touch Panel (QuickI2C/HIDI2C)
> SW Requirements :
>
>   - Install SW HID-SPI/I2C Driver on Windows OS. (i.e. QuickSPI/QuickI2C driver for THC1 port) 
>   - (Optional) For debugging, users can utilize the TraceView tool to capture .ETL log files, enabling detailed analysis of HID-SPI/I2C traffic/data payloads, interrupt rates/frequencies, and error/failure logging.
>
> HW Requirements :
>
>   - PTL RVP booted to WinOS with feature rework to enable SPI/I2C interface on THC1 port.   
>   - 3PE POR touch panel  (i.e. BOM36/37) with touch cable connected on PTL RVP via the THC1 touch port/connector.
>   - Acute logic analyzer to monitor the HID-SPI/I2C traffic for debug/analysis purposes.
>   - PythonSV with USB2DBC debug cable connected from SUT/RVP to host system.
>   - Optional: Custom rework required to wire-out all the THC-SPI/I2C/DSYNC IO pins for Acute LA signals tracing. (Note: Make sure to use single-core wires and keep the length as short as possible to avoid/minimize any signal integrity impact.)
> (Note: Please check PTL RVP schematic/layout to confirm the THC1 port naming label and location)
> Dependencies:
>
>   - BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements / config.  
>
> Test Steps / Procedure : 
> 1) PTL RVP successfully booted to WinOS with all the HW/SW requirements met with all the BIOS configuration done accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test Configuration' section above.
>
> 2) Dump the GPP_F_22 GPIO pin/pad related registers in PythonSV, check and ensure that all the configuration are done appropriately as expected for TCON-GPIO display sync support.
> (Note: Please check the PAD_CFG_DWx, pmode, iosstate, iosterm registers configuration on GPP_F_22 pin.)
>
> TCON DSYNC Disabled
>  | 
> TCON DSYNC Enabled
>
>  | 
>
>  | 
>
>  | 
>
> 3) In PythonSV, enable TCON Display Sync feature on THC1 port by programming the following registers, and read back the registers to verify the configured values: 
>     Important Note:
>
>   - The Sync signal source is selected by SW based on user or policy setting before enabling the coalescing using DISP_SYNC_EVT_SRC register.
>   - SW shall make sure the corresponding DISP_SYNC_EVT_SRC is enabled before enabling the SYNC_EVT interrupt.
> (a) Enable timestamp capability on THC1 port as DSYNC event timestamping using the exact same timer as Timestamping on Input Report (LNL+).
>      soc.thc1.pvt.thc_sb_pm_ctrl.thc_ts_d0i2_mode = 0         # TS timer paused during D0i2 entry (Will not reset back to 0)
>      soc.thc1.pvt.thc_sb_pm_ctrl.thc_microsec_cnt= 0x19     # Using SoC PGCB_CLK frequency for THC generate 10 us tick internally
>      soc.thc1.pvt.thc_sb_pm_ctrl.thc_timestamp_src= 0         # 0 - External PMC clocks tick as reference , 1 - Internal counter on core CLK 
>      soc.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_timestamp_2.thc_timestamp_en = 1  # Enable timestamp feature
> (b) Enable TCON DSYNC via GPIO capability on THC1 port. 
>       soc.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_disp_sync.disp_sync_evt_src = 0b10              # or 2 = Select GPIO SYNC as the source of DSYNC Event
>       soc.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_disp_sync.c_enable = 0                                      # 0- Disabled coalescing, 1- Enable coalescing based on the sync signal
>       soc.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_disp_sync.disp_sync_delay = 0                         # No Delay [William]: May need to change with delay timing ?? 
>       soc.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_disp_sync_2.disp_sync_gpio_mode = 0          # 0: SYNC assertion on GPIO rising edge, 1: Falling edge, 2: Both edge
>       soc.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_disp_sync_2.disp_sync_coal_delay_en = 0     #  No delay sync event for coalescing [William]: May need to change with delay timing ??
>       soc.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_disp_sync_2.disp_sync_int_delay_en = 0        #  No delay sync event for interrupt [William]: May need to change with delay timing ??
>       soc.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_int_en.disp_sync_evt_int_en = 1                        # Enable DSYNC event interrupt
>       soc.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_int_status.disp_sync_evt_int_sts = 1                # Write 1 to clear DSYNC event interrupt status bit
>
> 4) Read the initial starting value of these registers in below: 
>      soc.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_int_status.disp_sync_evt_int_sts.read()            # Must be 0 after write-1-to-clear
>      soc.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_timestamp_2.thc_timestamp.read()                   # Read the initial RxDMA input report timestamp
>      soc.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_sync_timestamp.read()                                         # Read the initial DSYNC Event timestamp
>
> 5) Poll reading the register for continuous dumping the timestamp value in PythonSV for ease of analysis/verification purpose. 
>     (Note: Both the RxDMA input report and DSYNC event timestamping capability are using the exact same timestamp timer.)
>       while(1): 
>           if soc.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_int_status.disp_sync_evt_int_sts == 1:     # DSYNC event occurred 
>
>               # Read current timestamp value: RxDMA2 input report/packet , Display SYNC Event Timestamp 
>                print(soc.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_timestamp_2.thc_timestamp.read(), soc.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_sync_timestamp.read())    
>                soc.thc1.port.mem.thc_m_prt_int_status.disp_sync_evt_int_sts = 1   # Write 1 to clear DSYNC event interrupt status bit
>
> 6) Generate multiple RxDMA input touch reports by performing touch gestures repetitively (e.g. tap-and-hold) onto the touch device for at least couple of seconds, and check the current DSYNC event timestamp register value.
> Expected outcome: 
>
>   - TCON Display Sync signal originated from Touch Panel (via eDP connector) is expected to toggle / logic transition "0->1->0" or vice-versa over time,
> whenever TCON GPIO input is received as a display frame sync signal on every display frame. 
>   - Once every DSYNC input signal assertion on GPIO rising/falling-edge detected on THC1 port, THC HW shall write the current time stamp to the CUR_SYNC_EVT_TS register. As a result, THC_M_PRT_SYNC_TIMESTAMP register returned value will increases over time whenever there's input touch activity/traffic on-going. 
>   - DISP_SYNC_EVT_INT_STS will be asserted when DSYNC event occurs, if DISP_SYNC_EVT_INT_EN is enabled. 
> (Note: SW is required to clear this bit before THC sends another SYNC MSI.)
>
> 7) Stop the register poll reading in PythonSV. And then, check and verify if the THC_M_PRT_SYNC_TIMESTAMP register returned value increased over time in each and every DSYNC input signal assertion.
>
> 8) Repeat the same test steps #2 to #7 with touch filter/smoothing INF driver extension installed on SUT's WinOS. 
>      (Note: For step #3, only check/verify if both SW driver/BIOS have configured all the required THC/GPIO registers to enable this TCON DSYNC feature.)
>
> Additional Note:
>
>   - THC's timestamp timer has option to use either an external microsecond tick reference from SoC(PMC), or an internal core clock counter to generate the 10 microsecond pulse based on THC_TIMESTAMP_SRC register bit configuration.
>   - Timestamping is not supported on SWDMA, TXDMA or PIO.
>
> Test Pass Criteria :
>
>   - TCON Display SYNC (DSYNC) GPIO function working as expected on THC1 port as stated in this TC's test steps above. 
>   - Expectation: SW driver will enable and utilize this particular TCON GPIO DSYNC feature.
> Test Fail Criteria :
>
>   - Unable to meet any of the passing criteria above.

---

### ✅ [TC] THC1 WoT via vGPIO_THC1 as Interrupt pin to Wake System from S0ix

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458289 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test Description :
>
>   - The objective/intent of this test is to verify that THC0 Wake-on-Touch (WoT) feature is enabled and functional as expected in Ubuntu OS via a virtual GPIO pin (i.e. vGPIO_THC1).
>
> Pre-Test Configuration :
>
>   - THC1 port will required to be enabled in BIOS settings.
>   - RVP booted to BIOS Menu, go to Intel Advance Menu -> PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration ->   <Refer to the BKM settings below to enable THC1 port.>
>   - RVP booted to BIOS Menu, go to Intel Advance Menu -> PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration ->  Enabled Wake on Touch
>   - To unlock all GPIO pads for SW configuration purpose, go to Intel Advance Menu -> PCH-IO Configuration -> Security Configuration -> Force unlock on all GPIO pads -> Enabled
>   - To ensure the system goes into S0ix state, go to Intel Advance Menu -> ACPI Configuration -> Enabled Low Power Idle Capability
>   - Note: The BKM below are captured from NVL RVP
>
>
>
>
> Test Steps / Procedure : 
>
>   - RVP successfully booted to Ubuntu OS with all the HW/SW requirements met with all the BIOS configuration done accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test Configuration' section above.
>   - Dump the vGPIO_THC1 related registers in PythonSV, check and ensure that all the configurations are done appropriately as expected for WoT support.
> (Note: Please check the PAD_CFG_DW0, HOSTSW_OWN, and GPI_INT_EN/STS registers configuration on vGPIO_THC1 pin.)
> WoT Disabled
>  | 
> WoT Enabled
>  | 
>
>  | 
>
>  | 
>
>   - Enter the following command to verify kernel version and THC kernel modules
> uname -r                                                            #kernel version
> lsmod | grep thc                                              #THC kernel modules
> lspci -nn | grep "Touch Host Controller"     #THC0 PCI device
>
>
>   - Check the Wake-on-Touch enablement by entering the following commands
>
> THC_BDF=$(lspci -D | grep "Touch Host Controller" | head -1 | awk '{print $1}')              # Find THC0 BDF first
> echo "THC0 BDF: $THC_BDF"
> cat /sys/bus/pci/devices/$THC_BDF/power/wakeup                                                              # Check if wake is enabled, expected: "enabled"
>
> # If it shows "disabled", enable it
> echo enabled | sudo tee /sys/bus/pci/devices/$THC_BDF/power/wakeup
>
>   - On the RVP/SUT: Go to top right corner, click on the power button -> suspend, to put the system into sleep state (a.k.a Modern/Connected Standby, S0ix). Read the residency counters, the residencies should be incrementing.
> (Note: Please wait for a couple of seconds for the system to completely entered S0ix state. Observe postcode transition from 0000 -> 00C5 --> 01C5)
>
>
>   - Perform Wake-on-Touch to wake SUT from CS/sleep state (PostCode = ABC5). Read the residency counters again, S0i2.1/2.2 should stop incrementing.
>
>   - Repeat steps #5 and #6 to perform WoT again for another 5 or 10 cycles, to check for consistency.
>   - When enter sleep state -> HID-SPI traffic traced with Saleae/Acute LA: THC1 will send a single output report/packet to TIC for touch panel device to enter sleep state.
> (Note: Expecting only single output report/packet, and NO other packets or activity observed on signal lines.)
>
>   - When wake from touch -> Observe postcode transition from 01C5 -> ABC5. HID-SPI traffic traced with Saleae/Acute LA: THC1 will send a single output report/packet to TIC for touch panel device to power on.
>
>
> SW Requirements :
>
>   - Ubuntu OS with built in HID-SPI/I2C kernel driver.
>   - Install SW HID-SPI/I2C driver in WinOS (i.e. QuickSPI/QuickI2C driver for THC1 port). 
>   - For WinOS, install the WoT Extension INF Driver (i.e. WoT_Extension.inf) for Wake-on-Touch support.
>   - For WinOS, install Intel Serial IO GPIO Driver (which is part of the LPSS driver stack) 
>   - (Optional) For debugging, users can utilize the TraceView tool to capture .ETL log files, enabling detailed analysis of HID-SPI/I2C traffic/data payloads, interrupt rates/frequencies, and error/failure logging.
> HW Requirements :
>
>   - RVP booted to Ubuntu/WinOS with feature rework to enable SPI/I2C interface on THC1 port.   
>   - 3PE POR touch panel (i.e. BOM52, BOM36/37) with touch cable connected on RVP via the THC1 touch port/connector.
>   - Acute logic analyzer to monitor the HID-SPI/I2C traffic for debug/analysis purposes.
>   - PythonSV with USB2DBC debug cable connected from SUT/RVP to host system.
>   - Optional: Custom rework required to wire-out all the THC-SPI/I2C IO pins for Acute LA signals tracing. (Note: Make sure to use single-core wires and keep the length as short as possible to avoid/minimize any signal integrity impact.)
> (Note: Please check RVP schematic/layout to confirm the THC1 port naming label and location) 
> Dependencies:
>
>   - BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements / config. 
>   - Ensure that WoT function is enabled on THC1 port in BIOS settings.  
>   - GPIO-IP: Legacy GPIO Interrupt controlled by Host SW GPIO driver mode.
>   - THC0 able to PG + CG, and entered into D3 power state. 
> Test Pass Criteria :
>
>   - Able to achieve N targeted number of cycles of WoT successfully.
>   - No errors and Yellow-Bang observed on THC1 port. 
>   - SleepStudy indicates system wake caused by Wake-on-Touch (WoT) only.
>   - THC1-SPI/I2C signal lines are clean and no activity after the S0iX entry (i.e. No periodic wakes/interrupts occurred) 
> Test Fail Criteria :
>
>   - Unable to meet any of the passing criteria above.
> Intent Verify:
>
>   - Verify that VGPIO_THC0/THC1 and PCI wake up settings are correctly configured for WoT operation.
>   - Ensure kernel modules and PCI device are properly detected and operational.
>   - Verify HID-SPI traffic shows only a single packet during sleep/wake, indicating proper touch panel power management.
>   - Validate that the system reliably enters S0ix and wakes on touch input, with correct residency counter and postcode transitions.
> Ecosystem SW Requirements:
> Below is the example from NVL-Hx, please refer to Wiki for different project: THC Device Enablement on NVL-HX RVP1/RVP4 - Validation and Debug Encyclopedia - Intel Enterprise Wiki
>
>   - BIOS version:
>   - ifwi_nvl_hx_a1a0_pp_release_2025ww50.1.01/ or later version
>
>   - OS kernel:
>   - Window OS
>
> NVL-HX-CONS-25.48.2.287 or later version (NOTE: NON-POR, does not supported by driver team, intended solely for debugging/validation purposes)
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> \\amr.corp.intel.com\ec\proj\sv\Debug\PowerOn\NVL_Hx\NVL_Hx_A1\OS_Image\Ubuntu\ubuntu-24.04.3-desktop-amd64.iso
>
>   - Driver versions:
>   - Windows OS
>
>  https://ubit-artifactory-or.intel.com/artifactory/one-windows-local/Ingredients/hidi2c_touch/ OR https://ubit-artifactory-ba.intel.com/artifactory/owr-repos/Submissions/quickspi/
>
>   - Ubuntu OSBuilt in QuickSPI kernel driver

---

### ✅ [TC] Warm Reset Cycles with THC0 (HID-I2C) Enabled only

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458387 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test Description:
> 	• The objective/intent of this test is to conduct a thorough system-level regression that includes 'N' iterations of PM Cycling, specifically Warm Resets (WR), while running test traffic through a single port THC0 that configured as HID-I2C interface.
>          (Note: 'N' iterations of WR Cycling will be specified/defined at TR level, the number will vary across different project milestones.)
> 	• This test is designed to implicitly encompass the verification of Fuses/Soft-straps loading, BIOS, SW Driver and THC Controller + TIC device initialization/enumeration sequences.
> 	• It will also assess and exercise the HID-I2C PIO and DMA traffic cycling on the THC0 port to ensure robust touch operation between the host controller and TIC device.
> 	• Solar SW tool will be utilized to initiate the PM regression/cycling test in Ubuntu OS environment.
> 	• Additionally, the THC controller will undergo a reset during each Warm-Reset cycle. This WR sequence will implicitly encompass the various elements outlined in the IP-HAS. (Note: Refer to TCD --> 'Overview' Section for more information.)
>
> Pre-Test Configuration:
> 	• THC0 port will required to be enabled in BIOS settings.
> 	• RVP booted to BIOS Menu, go to Intel Advance Menu -> PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration ->   <Refer to the BKM settings below to enable THC0 port.>
> 	• Note: The BKM below are captured from NVL RVP
>
>
>
> Test Steps / Procedure: 
> 	1. RVP booted to Ubuntu OS with all the HW/SW requirements met, follow and configure all the BIOS settings accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test Configuration' section above; Ensure latest Solar tool is installed.
> 	2. Check if there is yellow bang on THC touch panel with the following commands
> 	sudo lspci                     #list pci devices
> 	lspci -kvv -s 00:10.0   #list pci device with very detail kernel info and which bus device function
> 	The expected results is where "Kernel driver in use: intel_quicki2c" & "Kernel modules: intel_quicki2c" can be observed, no yellow bang occurs
> 	3. In Ubuntu OS, go into the Solar folder, right click to "open in terminal".
> 	4. Enter the following command to open the Solar GUI
> 	sudo ./SolarPanel.sh
> 	5. In Solar Tool, validator/user may use the following settings below as example, which will enable 100 cycles of Warm-Reset with Yellow-Bank Check, Exit On Failure and Bypass Windows Login enabled.
> 		a. Tick to enable Yellow Bang Check in Advanced Verification PRE/POST Check tab.
> 		b. Tick to enable Exit On Failure in Knobs tab.
> 		c. Tick to enable Bypass Windows Login Knobs tab.
> 		d. Specify 'N' total number of cycles per loop/test in Test Time tab. (e.g. 'N' = 100)
> 		e. Specify Start Delay Time (Optional)
> 		f. Specify total sleep time allowed, i.e. total allowed time to reboot/reset/wale the system back to Ubuntu OS state (e.g. 180 seconds)
> 		g. Specify total Active Time in Ubuntu OS before starting the next PM cycle (e.g. 15 seconds)
> 		h. Select the "Warm-Reset" button. It will be shown as green box once clicked, and the "Warm-Reset|sec:180|sec:15|Timer" will be shown in the big blue box in the right -> which implied it is confirmed being set.
> 		i. Finally, click on the green play button to start the PM Regression/Cycling test.
> 	(Note: Alternatively, instead of setup a new settings every time for each PM regression test, validator/user may load the existing config solar .xml file with pre-existing settings saved previously.)
>
>
> 	(Note: The photo below serves as a reference for the Solar Tool interface, as the setup/settings shown are used for Cold-Reset test.)
> 	6. Wait for the WR cycling test to complete. Solar tool will return a Pass/Fail result in the "Test Status" section, the result will be shown in the log file as well.
>
> [Alternate Method: Automated Touch traffic using NGA Framework + IVT Released NGA supported WIM image  -- (More info TBD)]
> 	• NGA Testline for WR Regression existing example used in MTL-S/MTP-S project: https://nga.laas.intel.com/#/mtl_validation/planning/testlines/13898a8c-5b6d-4a13-873e-9eba1ef39b17
> 	• If using the existing testline above, validator/user will need to port it over to PTL compliant framework/environment,  using IVT released WIM image for PTL with NGA support and all its' dependencies included.
>
> SW Requirements:
> 	• RVP/SUT booted to Ubuntu OS.
> 	• Ubuntu OS is with built-in HID-SPI/I2C kernel driver.
>
> HW Requirements:
> 	• RVP booted to Ubuntu OS with feature rework to enable I2C interface on THC0 port.
> 	• 3PE POR touch panel (i.e. BOM52, BOM36A/37A) with touch cable connected on RVP via the THC0 touch port/connector.
> 	• (Optional): Acute logic analyzer to monitor the HID-I2C traffic for debug/analysis purposes.
> (Note: Please check RVP schematic/layout to confirm the THC0 port naming label and location) 
>
> Dependencies:
> 	• BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements / config.
>
> Test Pass Criteria:
> 	• Using Solar tool, verify and ensure targeted 'N' number of Warm-Reset (WR) PM cycling count can be achieved successfully with NO error(s) returned/encountered. 
> 	• Make sure NO yellow-bank occurred on THC0 controller/Device Driver (i.e. QuickI2C).
> 	• Extra: Ensure that the touch functionality of the HID-I2C touch device connected on THC0 still working normally after each WR cycled. Alternatively, this functionality can be verified by conducting both PIO and DMA operations/traffic tests.
> (Note: If NGA/Automation is not enabled especially during the early PO phase, manual touch gesture method by human input can be used to verify if touch panel still functional.)
>
> Test Fail Criteria:
> 	• Unable to meet the passing criteria above.
> 	• Solar tool unable to pass/complete the targeted WR cycling count with error code returned, 
> 	• Yellow-bang occurred on THC - QuickI2C device and/or caused by any other drivers/devices, 
> 	• Touch functionality of the touch device broken and not working after WR. (i.e. PIO and DMA operations/traffic tests failed.)
> 	• Other IP/domains, WinOS, IFWI, SW driver or any other system/platform-level components utilized in this test that may caused the WR cycle failed to complete the targeted "N" Iterations of WR cycle.
>
> Intent Verify:
> 	• Verify no yellow-bang or driver errors occur on THC controller/driver after any warm-reset cycle.
> 	• Ensure the touch device maintains functionality post-reset (can operate via HID-SPI/I2C interface, PIO/DMA)
>
> Ecosystem SW Requirements:
>
> Below is the example from NVL-Hx, please refer to Wiki for different project: THC Device Enablement on NVL-HX RVP1/RVP4 - Validation and Debug Encyclopedia - Intel Enterprise Wiki
>
>   - BIOS version:
>   - ifwi_nvl_hx_a1a0_pp_release_2025ww50.1.01/ or later version
>
>   - OS kernel:
>   - Window OS
>
> NVL-HX-CONS-25.48.2.287 or later version (NOTE: NON-POR, does not supported by driver team, intended solely for debugging/validation purposes)
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> \\amr.corp.intel.com\ec\proj\sv\Debug\PowerOn\NVL_Hx\NVL_Hx_A1\OS_Image\Ubuntu\ubuntu-24.04.3-desktop-amd64.iso
>
>   - Driver versions:
>   - Windows OS
>
>  https://ubit-artifactory-or.intel.com/artifactory/one-windows-local/Ingredients/hidi2c_touch/ OR https://ubit-artifactory-ba.intel.com/artifactory/owr-repos/Submissions/quickspi/
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
>                              Built in QuickSPI/QuickI2C kernel driver

---

### ✅ [TC] Warm Reset Cycles with THC0 (HID-I2C) and THC1 (HID-I2C) Enabled

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458388 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test Description:
> 	• The objective/intent of this test is to conduct a thorough system-level regression that includes 'N' iterations of PM Cycling, specifically Warm Resets (WR), while running test traffic through THC0 and THC1 ports concurrently with both ports configured as HID-I2C interface.
>          (Note: 'N' iterations of WR Cycling will be specified/defined at TR level, the number will vary across different project milestones.)
> 	• This test is designed to implicitly encompass the verification of Fuses/Soft-straps loading, BIOS, SW Driver and THC Controller + TIC device initialization/enumeration sequences.
> 	• It will also assess and exercise the HID-I2C PIO and DMA traffic cycling on the THC0 and THC1 ports to ensure robust touch operation between the host controller and TIC device.
> 	• Solar SW tool will be utilized to initiate the PM regression/cycling test in Ubuntu OS environment.
> 	• Additionally, the THC controller will undergo a reset during each Warm-Reset cycle. This WR sequence will implicitly encompass the various elements outlined in the IP-HAS. (Note: Refer to TCD --> 'Overview' Section for more information.)
>
> Pre-Test Configuration:
> 	• THC0 and THC1 ports will required to be enabled in BIOS settings.
> 	• RVP booted to BIOS Menu, go to Intel Advance Menu -> PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration ->   <Refer to the BKM settings below to enable THC0 and THC1 ports.>
> 	• Note: The BKM below are captured from NVL RVP
>
> Test Steps / Procedure: 
> 	1. RVP booted to Ubuntu OS with all the HW/SW requirements met, follow and configure all the BIOS settings accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test Configuration' section above; Ensure latest Solar tool is installed.
> 	2. Check if there is yellow bang on THC touch panel with the following commands
> 	sudo lspci                     #list pci devices
> 	lspci -kvv -s 00:10.0    #list pci device with very detail kernel info and which bus device function
> 	The expected results is where "Kernel driver in use: intel_quicki2c" & "Kernel modules: intel_quicki2c" can be observed, no yellow bang occurs
> 	3. In Ubuntu OS, go into the Solar folder, right click to "open in terminal".
> 	4. Enter the following command to open the Solar GUI
> 	sudo ./SolarPanel.sh
> 	5. In Solar Tool, validator/user may use the following settings below as example, which will enable 100 cycles of Warm-Reset with Yellow-Bank Check, Exit On Failure and Bypass Windows Login enabled.
> 		a. Tick to enable Yellow Bang Check in Advanced Verification PRE/POST Check tab.
> 		b. Tick to enable Exit On Failure in Knobs tab.
> 		c. Tick to enable Bypass Windows Login Knobs tab.
> 		d. Specify 'N' total number of cycles per loop/test in Test Time tab. (e.g. 'N' = 100)
> 		e. Specify Start Delay Time (Optional)
> 		f. Specify total sleep time allowed, i.e. total allowed time to reboot/reset/wale the system back to Ubuntu OS state (e.g. 180 seconds)
> 		g. Specify total Active Time in Ubuntu OS before starting the next PM cycle (e.g. 15 seconds)
> 		h. Select the "Warm-Reset" button. It will be shown as green box once clicked, and the "Warm-Reset|sec:180|sec:15|Timer" will be shown in the big blue box in the right -> which implied it is confirmed being set.
> 		i. Finally, click on the green play button to start the PM Regression/Cycling test.
> 	(Note: Alternatively, instead of setup a new settings every time for each PM regression test, validator/user may load the existing config solar .xml file with pre-existing settings saved previously.)
>
> 	(Note: The photo below serves as a reference for the Solar Tool interface, as the setup/settings shown are used for Cold-Reset test.)
> 	6. Wait for the WR cycling test to complete. Solar tool will return a Pass/Fail result in the "Test Status" section, the result will be shown in the log file as well.
>
> [Alternate Method: Automated Touch traffic using NGA Framework + IVT Released NGA supported WIM image  -- (More info TBD)]
> 	• NGA Testline for WR Regression existing example used in MTL-S/MTP-S project: https://nga.laas.intel.com/#/mtl_validation/planning/testlines/13898a8c-5b6d-4a13-873e-9eba1ef39b17
> 	• If using the existing testline above, validator/user will need to port it over to PTL compliant framework/environment,  using IVT released WIM image for PTL with NGA support and all its' dependencies included.
>
> SW Requirements:
> 	• RVP/SUT booted to Ubuntu OS.
> 	• Ubuntu OS is with built-in HID-SPI/I2C kernel driver.
>
> HW Requirements:
> 	• RVP booted to Ubuntu OS with feature rework to enable I2C interface on THC0/THC1 port.
> 	• 3PE POR touch panel (i.e. BOM52, BOM36A/37A) with touch cable connected on RVP via the THC0/THC1 touch port/connector.
> 	• (Optional): Acute logic analyzer to monitor the HID-I2C traffic for debug/analysis purposes.
> (Note: Please check RVP schematic/layout to confirm the THC0/THC1 port naming label and location) 
>
> Dependencies:
> 	• BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements / config.
>
> Test Pass Criteria:
> 	• Using Solar tool, verify and ensure targeted 'N' number of Warm-Reset (WR) PM cycling count can be achieved successfully with NO error(s) returned/encountered. 
> 	• Make sure NO yellow-bank occurred on THC0/THC1 controller/Device Driver (i.e. QuickI2C).
> 	• Extra: Ensure that the touch functionality of the HID-I2C touch device connected on THC0/THC1 still working normally after each WR cycled. Alternatively, this functionality can be verified by conducting both PIO and DMA operations/traffic tests.
> (Note: If NGA/Automation is not enabled especially during the early PO phase, manual touch gesture method by human input can be used to verify if touch panel still functional.)
>
> Test Fail Criteria:
> 	• Unable to meet the passing criteria above.
> 	• Solar tool unable to pass/complete the targeted WR cycling count with error code returned, 
> 	• Yellow-bang occurred on THC - QuickI2C device and/or caused by any other drivers/devices, 
> 	• Touch functionality of the touch device broken and not working after WR. (i.e. PIO and DMA operations/traffic tests failed.)
> 	• Other IP/domains, WinOS, IFWI, SW driver or any other system/platform-level components utilized in this test that may caused the WR cycle failed to complete the targeted "N" Iterations of WR cycle.
>
> Intent Verify:
> 	• Verify no yellow-bang or driver errors occur on THC controller/driver after any warm-reset cycle.
> 	• Ensure the touch device maintains functionality post-reset (can operate via HID-SPI/I2C interface, PIO/DMA)
>
> Ecosystem SW Requirements:
>
> Below is the example from NVL-Hx, please refer to Wiki for different project: THC Device Enablement on NVL-HX RVP1/RVP4 - Validation and Debug Encyclopedia - Intel Enterprise Wiki
>
>   - BIOS version:
>   - ifwi_nvl_hx_a1a0_pp_release_2025ww50.1.01/ or later version
>
>   - OS kernel:
>   - Window OS
>
> NVL-HX-CONS-25.48.2.287 or later version (NOTE: NON-POR, does not supported by driver team, intended solely for debugging/validation purposes)
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> \\amr.corp.intel.com\ec\proj\sv\Debug\PowerOn\NVL_Hx\NVL_Hx_A1\OS_Image\Ubuntu\ubuntu-24.04.3-desktop-amd64.iso
>
>   - Driver versions:
>   - Windows OS
>
>  https://ubit-artifactory-or.intel.com/artifactory/one-windows-local/Ingredients/hidi2c_touch/ OR https://ubit-artifactory-ba.intel.com/artifactory/owr-repos/Submissions/quickspi/
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
>                              Built in QuickSPI/QuickI2C kernel driver

| **ID** | 13013458390 |
| **Status** | complete |

| **Owner** | willychi |

**Description:**

> Test Description:
> 	• The objective/intent of this test is to conduct a thorough system-level regression that includes 'N' iterations of PM Cycling, specifically Warm Resets (WR), while running test traffic through THC0 port that configured as HID-I2C interface and THC1 port configured as HID-SPI interface.
>          (Note: 'N' iterations of WR Cycling will be specified/defined at TR level, the number will vary across different project milestones.)
> 	• This test is designed to implicitly encompass the verification of Fuses/Soft-straps loading, BIOS, SW Driver and THC Controller + TIC device initialization/enumeration sequences.
> 	• It will also assess and exercise the HID-I2C/SPI PIO and DMA traffic cycling on the THC0/THC1 port to ensure robust touch operation between the host controller and TIC device.
> 	• Solar SW tool will be utilized to initiate the PM regression/cycling test in Ubuntu OS environment.
> 	• Additionally, the THC controller will undergo a reset during each Warm-Reset cycle. This WR sequence will implicitly encompass the various elements outlined in the IP-HAS. (Note: Refer to TCD --> 'Overview' Section for more information.)
>
> Pre-Test Configuration:
> 	• THC0 and THC1 ports will required to be enabled in BIOS settings.
> 	• RVP booted to BIOS Menu, go to Intel Advance Menu -> PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration ->   <Refer to the BKM settings below to enable THC0 and THC1 ports.>
> 	• Note: The BKM below are captured from NVL RVP
>
> Test Steps / Procedure: 
> 	1. RVP booted to Ubuntu OS with all the HW/SW requirements met, follow and configure all the BIOS settings accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test Configuration' section above; Ensure latest Solar tool is installed.
> 	2. Check if there is yellow bang on THC touch panel with the following commands
> 	sudo lspci                     #list pci devices
> 	lspci -kvv -s 00:10.0    #list pci device with very detail kernel info and which bus device function
> 	The expected results is where "Kernel driver in use: intel_quicki2c/spi" & "Kernel modules: intel_quicki2c/spi" can be observed, no yellow bang occurs
> 	3. In Ubuntu OS, go into the Solar folder, right click to "open in terminal".
> 	4. Enter the following command to open the Solar GUI
> 	sudo ./SolarPanel.sh
> 	5. In Solar Tool, validator/user may use the following settings below as example, which will enable 100 cycles of Warm-Reset with Yellow-Bank Check, Exit On Failure and Bypass Windows Login enabled.
> 		a. Tick to enable Yellow Bang Check in Advanced Verification PRE/POST Check tab.
> 		b. Tick to enable Exit On Failure in Knobs tab.
> 		c. Tick to enable Bypass Windows Login Knobs tab.
> 		d. Specify 'N' total number of cycles per loop/test in Test Time tab. (e.g. 'N' = 100)
> 		e. Specify Start Delay Time (Optional)
> 		f. Specify total sleep time allowed, i.e. total allowed time to reboot/reset/wale the system back to Ubuntu OS state (e.g. 180 seconds)
> 		g. Specify total Active Time in Ubuntu OS before starting the next PM cycle (e.g. 15 seconds)
> 		h. Select the "Warm-Reset" button. It will be shown as green box once clicked, and the "Warm-Reset|sec:180|sec:15|Timer" will be shown in the big blue box in the right -> which implied it is confirmed being set.
> 		i. Finally, click on the green play button to start the PM Regression/Cycling test.
> 	(Note: Alternatively, instead of setup a new settings every time for each PM regression test, validator/user may load the existing config solar .xml file with pre-existing settings saved previously.)
>
> 	(Note: The photo below serves as a reference for the Solar Tool interface, as the setup/settings shown are used for Cold-Reset test.)
> 	6. Wait for the WR cycling test to complete. Solar tool will return a Pass/Fail result in the "Test Status" section, the result will be shown in the log file as well.
>
> [Alternate Method: Automated Touch traffic using NGA Framework + IVT Released NGA supported WIM image  -- (More info TBD)]
> 	• NGA Testline for WR Regression existing example used in MTL-S/MTP-S project: https://nga.laas.intel.com/#/mtl_validation/planning/testlines/13898a8c-5b6d-4a13-873e-9eba1ef39b17
> 	• If using the existing testline above, validator/user will need to port it over to PTL compliant framework/environment,  using IVT released WIM image for PTL with NGA support and all its' dependencies included.
>
> SW Requirements:
> 	• RVP/SUT booted to Ubuntu OS.
> 	• Ubuntu OS is with built-in HID-SPI/I2C kernel driver.
>
> HW Requirements:
> 	• RVP booted to Ubuntu OS with feature rework to enable I2C/SPI interface on THC0/THC1 port.
> 	• 3PE POR touch panel (i.e. BOM52, BOM36A/37A) with touch cable connected on RVP via the THC0/THC1 touch port/connector.
> 	• (Optional): Acute logic analyzer to monitor the HID-I2C/SPI traffic for debug/analysis purposes.
> (Note: Please check RVP schematic/layout to confirm the THC0/THC1 port naming label and location) 
>
> Dependencies:
> 	• BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements / config.
>
> Test Pass Criteria:
> 	• Using Solar tool, verify and ensure targeted 'N' number of Warm-Reset (WR) PM cycling count can be achieved successfully with NO error(s) returned/encountered. 
> 	• Make sure NO yellow-bank occurred on THC0/THC1 controller/Device Driver (i.e. QuickI2C, QuickSPI).
> 	• Extra: Ensure that the touch functionality of the HID-I2C/SPI touch device connected on THC0/THC1 still working normally after each WR cycled. Alternatively, this functionality can be verified by conducting both PIO and DMA operations/traffic tests.
> (Note: If NGA/Automation is not enabled especially during the early PO phase, manual touch gesture method by human input can be used to verify if touch panel still functional.)
>
> Test Fail Criteria:
> 	• Unable to meet the passing criteria above.
> 	• Solar tool unable to pass/complete the targeted WR cycling count with error code returned, 
> 	• Yellow-bang occurred on THC - QuickI2C/SPI device and/or caused by any other drivers/devices, 
> 	• Touch functionality of the touch device broken and not working after WR. (i.e. PIO and DMA operations/traffic tests failed.)
> 	• Other IP/domains, WinOS, IFWI, SW driver or any other system/platform-level components utilized in this test that may caused the WR cycle failed to complete the targeted "N" Iterations of WR cycle.
>
> Intent Verify:
> 	• Verify no yellow-bang or driver errors occur on THC controller/driver after any warm-reset cycle.
> 	• Ensure the touch device maintains functionality post-reset (can operate via HID-SPI/I2C interface, PIO/DMA)

### ✅ [TC] Warm Reset Cycles with THC0 (HID-SPI) Enabled only

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458391 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test Description:
> 	• The objective/intent of this test is to conduct a thorough system-level regression that includes 'N' iterations of PM Cycling, specifically Warm Resets (WR), while running test traffic through a single port THC0 that configured as HID-SPI interface.
>          (Note: 'N' iterations of WR Cycling will be specified/defined at TR level, the number will vary across different project milestones.)
> 	• This test is designed to implicitly encompass the verification of Fuses/Soft-straps loading, BIOS, SW Driver and THC Controller + TIC device initialization/enumeration sequences.
> 	• It will also assess and exercise the HID-SPI PIO and DMA traffic cycling on the THC0 port to ensure robust touch operation between the host controller and TIC device.
> 	• Solar SW tool will be utilized to initiate the PM regression/cycling test in Ubuntu OS environment.
> 	• Additionally, the THC controller will undergo a reset during each Warm-Reset cycle. This WR sequence will implicitly encompass the various elements outlined in the IP-HAS. (Note: Refer to TCD --> 'Overview' Section for more information.)
>
> Pre-Test Configuration:
> 	• THC0 port will required to be enabled in BIOS settings.
> 	• RVP booted to BIOS Menu, go to Intel Advance Menu -> PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration ->   <Refer to the BKM settings below to enable THC0 port.>
> 	• Note: The BKM below are captured from NVL RVP
>
> Test Steps / Procedure: 
> 	1. RVP booted to Ubuntu OS with all the HW/SW requirements met, follow and configure all the BIOS settings accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test Configuration' section above; Ensure latest Solar tool is installed.
> 	2. Check if there is yellow bang on THC touch panel with the following commands
> 	sudo lspci                     #list pci devices
> 	lspci -kvv -s 00:10.0   #list pci device with very detail kernel info and which bus device function
> 	The expected results is where "Kernel driver in use: intel_quickspi" & "Kernel modules: intel_quickspi" can be observed, no yellow bang occurs
> 	3. In Ubuntu OS, go into the Solar folder, right click to "open in terminal".
> 	4. Enter the following command to open the Solar GUI
> 	sudo ./SolarPanel.sh
> 	5. In Solar Tool, validator/user may use the following settings below as example, which will enable 100 cycles of Warm-Reset with Yellow-Bank Check, Exit On Failure and Bypass Windows Login enabled.
> 		a. Tick to enable Yellow Bang Check in Advanced Verification PRE/POST Check tab.
> 		b. Tick to enable Exit On Failure in Knobs tab.
> 		c. Tick to enable Bypass Windows Login Knobs tab.
> 		d. Specify 'N' total number of cycles per loop/test in Test Time tab. (e.g. 'N' = 100)
> 		e. Specify Start Delay Time (Optional)
> 		f. Specify total sleep time allowed, i.e. total allowed time to reboot/reset/wale the system back to Ubuntu OS state (e.g. 180 seconds)
> 		g. Specify total Active Time in Ubuntu OS before starting the next PM cycle (e.g. 15 seconds)
> 		h. Select the "Warm-Reset" button. It will be shown as green box once clicked, and the "Warm-Reset|sec:180|sec:15|Timer" will be shown in the big blue box in the right -> which implied it is confirmed being set.
> 		i. Finally, click on the green play button to start the PM Regression/Cycling test.
> 	(Note: Alternatively, instead of setup a new settings every time for each PM regression test, validator/user may load the existing config solar .xml file with pre-existing settings saved previously.)
>
> 	(Note: The photo below serves as a reference for the Solar Tool interface, as the setup/settings shown are used for Cold-Reset test.)
> 	6. Wait for the WR cycling test to complete. Solar tool will return a Pass/Fail result in the "Test Status" section, the result will be shown in the log file as well.
>
> [Alternate Method: Automated Touch traffic using NGA Framework + IVT Released NGA supported WIM image  -- (More info TBD)]
> 	• NGA Testline for WR Regression existing example used in MTL-S/MTP-S project: https://nga.laas.intel.com/#/mtl_validation/planning/testlines/13898a8c-5b6d-4a13-873e-9eba1ef39b17
> 	• If using the existing testline above, validator/user will need to port it over to PTL compliant framework/environment,  using IVT released WIM image for PTL with NGA support and all its' dependencies included.
>
> SW Requirements:
> 	• RVP/SUT booted to Ubuntu OS.
> 	• Ubuntu OS is with built-in HID-SPI/I2C kernel driver.
>
> HW Requirements:
> 	• RVP booted to Ubuntu OS with feature rework to enable SPI interface on THC0 port.
> 	• 3PE POR touch panel (i.e. BOM52, BOM36A/37A) with touch cable connected on RVP via the THC0 touch port/connector.
> 	• (Optional): Acute logic analyzer to monitor the HID-SPI traffic for debug/analysis purposes.
> (Note: Please check RVP schematic/layout to confirm the THC0 port naming label and location) 
>
> Dependencies:
> 	• BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements / config.
>
> Test Pass Criteria:
> 	• Using Solar tool, verify and ensure targeted 'N' number of Warm-Reset (WR) PM cycling count can be achieved successfully with NO error(s) returned/encountered. 
> 	• Make sure NO yellow-bank occurred on THC0 controller/Device Driver (i.e. QuickSPI).
> 	• Extra: Ensure that the touch functionality of the HID-SPI touch device connected on THC0 still working normally after each WR cycled. Alternatively, this functionality can be verified by conducting both PIO and DMA operations/traffic tests.
> (Note: If NGA/Automation is not enabled especially during the early PO phase, manual touch gesture method by human input can be used to verify if touch panel still functional.)
>
> Test Fail Criteria:
> 	• Unable to meet the passing criteria above.
> 	• Solar tool unable to pass/complete the targeted WR cycling count with error code returned, 
> 	• Yellow-bang occurred on THC - QuickSPI device and/or caused by any other drivers/devices, 
> 	• Touch functionality of the touch device broken and not working after WR. (i.e. PIO and DMA operations/traffic tests failed.)
> 	• Other IP/domains, WinOS, IFWI, SW driver or any other system/platform-level components utilized in this test that may caused the WR cycle failed to complete the targeted "N" Iterations of WR cycle.
>
> Intent Verify:
> 	• Verify no yellow-bang or driver errors occur on THC controller/driver after any warm-reset cycle.
> 	• Ensure the touch device maintains functionality post-reset (can operate via HID-SPI/I2C interface, PIO/DMA)
>
> Ecosystem SW Requirements:
>
> Below is the example from NVL-Hx, please refer to Wiki for different project: THC Device Enablement on NVL-HX RVP1/RVP4 - Validation and Debug Encyclopedia - Intel Enterprise Wiki
>
>   - BIOS version:
>   - ifwi_nvl_hx_a1a0_pp_release_2025ww50.1.01/ or later version
>
>   - OS kernel:
>   - Window OS
>
> NVL-HX-CONS-25.48.2.287 or later version (NOTE: NON-POR, does not supported by driver team, intended solely for debugging/validation purposes)
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> \\amr.corp.intel.com\ec\proj\sv\Debug\PowerOn\NVL_Hx\NVL_Hx_A1\OS_Image\Ubuntu\ubuntu-24.04.3-desktop-amd64.iso
>
>   - Driver versions:
>   - Windows OS
>
>  https://ubit-artifactory-or.intel.com/artifactory/one-windows-local/Ingredients/hidi2c_touch/ OR https://ubit-artifactory-ba.intel.com/artifactory/owr-repos/Submissions/quickspi/
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
>                              Built in QuickSPI/QuickI2C kernel driver

---

### ✅ [TC] Warm Reset Cycles with THC0 (HID-SPI) and THC1 (HID-I2C) Enabled

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **ID** | 13013458394 |
| **Status** | complete |
| **Subject** | test_case |
| **Owner** | willychi |
| **Level** | L3 (TC) |

**Description:**

> Test Description:
> 	• The objective/intent of this test is to conduct a thorough system-level regression that includes 'N' iterations of PM Cycling, specifically Warm Resets (WR), while running test traffic through THC0 port that configured as HID-SPI interface and THC1 port configured as HID-I2C interface.
>          (Note: 'N' iterations of WR Cycling will be specified/defined at TR level, the number will vary across different project milestones.)
> 	• This test is designed to implicitly encompass the verification of Fuses/Soft-straps loading, BIOS, SW Driver and THC Controller + TIC device initialization/enumeration sequences.
> 	• It will also assess and exercise the HID-SPI/I2C PIO and DMA traffic cycling on the THC0/THC1 port to ensure robust touch operation between the host controller and TIC device.
> 	• Solar SW tool will be utilized to initiate the PM regression/cycling test in Ubuntu OS environment.
> 	• Additionally, the THC controller will undergo a reset during each Warm-Reset cycle. This WR sequence will implicitly encompass the various elements outlined in the IP-HAS. (Note: Refer to TCD --> 'Overview' Section for more information.)
>
> Pre-Test Configuration:
> 	• THC0 and THC1 ports will required to be enabled in BIOS settings.
> 	• RVP booted to BIOS Menu, go to Intel Advance Menu -> PCH-IO Configuration -> THC Configuration ->   <Refer to the BKM settings below to enable THC0 and THC1 ports.>
> 	• Note: The BKM below are captured from NVL RVP
>
> Test Steps / Procedure: 
> 	1. RVP booted to Ubuntu OS with all the HW/SW requirements met, follow and configure all the BIOS settings accordingly as described in 'Pre-Test Configuration' section above; Ensure latest Solar tool is installed.
> 	2. Check if there is yellow bang on THC touch panel with the following commands
> 	sudo lspci                     #list pci devices
> 	lspci -kvv -s 00:10.0    #list pci device with very detail kernel info and which bus device function
> 	The expected results is where "Kernel driver in use: intel_quickspi/i2c" & "Kernel modules: intel_quickspi/i2c" can be observed, no yellow bang occurs
> 	3. In Ubuntu OS, go into the Solar folder, right click to "open in terminal".
> 	4. Enter the following command to open the Solar GUI
> 	sudo ./SolarPanel.sh
> 	5. In Solar Tool, validator/user may use the following settings below as example, which will enable 100 cycles of Warm-Reset with Yellow-Bank Check, Exit On Failure and Bypass Windows Login enabled.
> 		a. Tick to enable Yellow Bang Check in Advanced Verification PRE/POST Check tab.
> 		b. Tick to enable Exit On Failure in Knobs tab.
> 		c. Tick to enable Bypass Windows Login Knobs tab.
> 		d. Specify 'N' total number of cycles per loop/test in Test Time tab. (e.g. 'N' = 100)
> 		e. Specify Start Delay Time (Optional)
> 		f. Specify total sleep time allowed, i.e. total allowed time to reboot/reset/wale the system back to Ubuntu OS state (e.g. 180 seconds)
> 		g. Specify total Active Time in Ubuntu OS before starting the next PM cycle (e.g. 15 seconds)
> 		h. Select the "Warm-Reset" button. It will be shown as green box once clicked, and the "Warm-Reset|sec:180|sec:15|Timer" will be shown in the big blue box in the right -> which implied it is confirmed being set.
> 		i. Finally, click on the green play button to start the PM Regression/Cycling test.
> 	(Note: Alternatively, instead of setup a new settings every time for each PM regression test, validator/user may load the existing config solar .xml file with pre-existing settings saved previously.)
>
> 	(Note: The photo below serves as a reference for the Solar Tool interface, as the setup/settings shown are used for Cold-Reset test.)
> 	6. Wait for the WR cycling test to complete. Solar tool will return a Pass/Fail result in the "Test Status" section, the result will be shown in the log file as well.
>
> [Alternate Method: Automated Touch traffic using NGA Framework + IVT Released NGA supported WIM image  -- (More info TBD)]
> 	• NGA Testline for WR Regression existing example used in MTL-S/MTP-S project: https://nga.laas.intel.com/#/mtl_validation/planning/testlines/13898a8c-5b6d-4a13-873e-9eba1ef39b17
> 	• If using the existing testline above, validator/user will need to port it over to PTL compliant framework/environment,  using IVT released WIM image for PTL with NGA support and all its' dependencies included.
>
> SW Requirements:
> 	• RVP/SUT booted to Ubuntu OS.
> 	• Ubuntu OS is with built-in HID-SPI/I2C kernel driver.
>
> HW Requirements:
> 	• RVP booted to Ubuntu OS with feature rework to enable SPI/I2C interface on THC0/THC1 port.
> 	• 3PE POR touch panel (i.e. BOM52, BOM36A/37A) with touch cable connected on RVP via the THC0/THC1 touch port/connector.
> 	• (Optional): Acute logic analyzer to monitor the HID-SPI/I2C traffic for debug/analysis purposes.
> (Note: Please check RVP schematic/layout to confirm the THC0/THC1 port naming label and location) 
>
> Dependencies:
> 	• BIOS implementation according to THC BWG requirements / config.
>
> Test Pass Criteria:
> 	• Using Solar tool, verify and ensure targeted 'N' number of Warm-Reset (WR) PM cycling count can be achieved successfully with NO error(s) returned/encountered. 
> 	• Make sure NO yellow-bank occurred on THC0/THC1 controller/Device Driver (i.e. QuickSPI, QuickI2C).
> 	• Extra: Ensure that the touch functionality of the HID-SPI/I2C touch device connected on THC0/THC1 still working normally after each WR cycled. Alternatively, this functionality can be verified by conducting both PIO and DMA operations/traffic tests.
> (Note: If NGA/Automation is not enabled especially during the early PO phase, manual touch gesture method by human input can be used to verify if touch panel still functional.)
>
> Test Fail Criteria:
> 	• Unable to meet the passing criteria above.
> 	• Solar tool unable to pass/complete the targeted WR cycling count with error code returned, 
> 	• Yellow-bang occurred on THC - QuickSPI/I2C device and/or caused by any other drivers/devices, 
> 	• Touch functionality of the touch device broken and not working after WR. (i.e. PIO and DMA operations/traffic tests failed.)
> 	• Other IP/domains, WinOS, IFWI, SW driver or any other system/platform-level components utilized in this test that may caused the WR cycle failed to complete the targeted "N" Iterations of WR cycle.
>
> Intent Verify:
> 	• Verify no yellow-bang or driver errors occur on THC controller/driver after any warm-reset cycle.
> 	• Ensure the touch device maintains functionality post-reset (can operate via HID-SPI/I2C interface, PIO/DMA)
>
> Ecosystem SW Requirements:
>
> Below is the example from NVL-Hx, please refer to Wiki for different project: THC Device Enablement on NVL-HX RVP1/RVP4 - Validation and Debug Encyclopedia - Intel Enterprise Wiki
>
>   - BIOS version:
>   - ifwi_nvl_hx_a1a0_pp_release_2025ww50.1.01/ or later version
>
>   - OS kernel:
>   - Window OS
>
> NVL-HX-CONS-25.48.2.287 or later version (NOTE: NON-POR, does not supported by driver team, intended solely for debugging/validation purposes)
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
> \\amr.corp.intel.com\ec\proj\sv\Debug\PowerOn\NVL_Hx\NVL_Hx_A1\OS_Image\Ubuntu\ubuntu-24.04.3-desktop-amd64.iso
>
>   - Driver versions:
>   - Windows OS
>
>  https://ubit-artifactory-or.intel.com/artifactory/one-windows-local/Ingredients/hidi2c_touch/ OR https://ubit-artifactory-ba.intel.com/artifactory/owr-repos/Submissions/quickspi/
>
>   - Ubuntu OS
>
>                              Built in QuickSPI/QuickI2C kernel driver
